items.title: How to Beat the Far Right: Lessons From History
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
The ideas of the far right, once confined to the margins of politics, have gone mainstream. A decade ago, it was just about possible to think that the right-wing nationalism sweeping through liberal democracies around the world might subside of its own accord. Whatever discontents it profited from could, perhaps, be dealt with or absorbed back into mainstream politics. Or perhaps its figureheads would show themselves to be so incompetent at governing that voters wouldn’t give them a second chance. That is clearly not the case.

As the political analyst Mark Leonard argues in a recent study, this is a movement that has attuned itself to the times we live in. In the West, each new shock, from the rising costs of energy and food to increasing global conflict, falls on the shoulders of communities whose resilience has been hollowed out by an economic system in which inequality has been allowed to soar and the public good disdained. Incumbent elites are forced into defending the existing, crumbling order, even though many of them privately admit it isn’t working.

Yet nowhere have right-wing nationalists gained the support of an overwhelming majority – or in many cases, even a majority at all. So where does that leave us?

The good news is that people all over the world have been grappling with this question for some years now. Nobody has a complete answer, but there are several lessons to draw from what’s been tried, what worked, and what didn’t:

Years of writing about this subject have taught me that there is no carefully worked-out master plan behind the rise of far-right politics. Its movements are riven with contradictions – for instance, between the economic aspirations of many of their supporters and the financial interests of party backers. Populist demagogues are adept at colonizing our attention in an algorithmically distorted media landscape, but they are often just making it up as they go along. What’s more, in the way they define the ‘people’ and the ‘elites,’ it is clear there are millions of us who fall outside either category.

In each of these cases, the far right’s strong points can be turned back against it. The story I always turn to when I’m thinking about this comes from Poland. In October 2020, the constitutional court – which the far-right Law and Justice government had stuffed with friendly judges – tightened already strict abortion laws. In effect, it made abortion illegal even when the fetus had a severe and permanent disability or an incurable and life-threatening disease.

For years, Law and Justice’s opponents had criticized its purging of independent minds in Poland’s judiciary, but the government had always been able to brush this off as an elite concern. Law and Justice thought its Catholic conservatism was what ‘the people’ wanted. Only in this case, it turned out they didn’t. Hundreds of thousands of protesters, led by the women whose bodily freedoms were affected by the new law, came out into the streets to protest against the government. The protests, which united under the name ‘women’s strike,’ demanded abortion rights, the restoration of independent courts, and an end to the Church’s influence on politics and education.

The government tried to ignore the protests, then it tried to suppress them. The organizers were threatened with prosecution, while far-right militias supportive of the government attacked protesters and dragged women who held sit-ins in churches out onto the street. But the protests kept going, and it became harder to deny they were a mass movement, rooted in people’s everyday concerns as well as moral principle: a woman’s right to choose is both.

This message was carried further by protest slogans that expressed both anger at this attempt to restrict women’s rights, and mockery of the politicians who claimed to speak for Polish women: “My body is not a coffin”; “The government is not a pregnancy, it can be removed”; “The cat can stay” – a reference to the Law and Justice leader’s pet cat – “the government get the fuck out.” The energy mobilized by these protests fed into a record high turnout, particularly among young people, at the general election of 2023 that saw Law and Justice kicked out of office.

This isn’t a template to copy: rather, it’s a striking example of where people linked a whole set of abstract issues to one of immediate, material concern. An issue, in fact, that goes right to the heart of right-wing nationalism itself: women’s bodies, which it treats as vessels for national renewal. What other issues are out there that allow us to call the far right’s bluff, and break its monopoly of attention?

The far right fills a vacuum: an absence of challenge to racist and divisive arguments; the holes in the social fabric left by years of failed economic policies; a sense that there’s no better option. It is adept at summoning the emotions and making its arguments sound like common sense. Its opponents need to do that too.

A few years ago, for his book If We Burn, the journalist Vincent Bevins traveled the world to talk to people who had taken part in the mass protest movements of the 2010s. Speaking to people in Brazil, Chile, Tunisia, Egypt, Bahrain, Yemen, Turkey, Hong Kong, and South Korea, he wanted to find out why, in so many places where people had taken to the streets in support of more freedom, or a fairer distribution of wealth, the ultimate outcome had been forms of authoritarian and right-wing reaction. Was there anything, he asked former activists, you’d say to a young person fighting for change in the world right now?

Don’t give up, was one frequent piece of advice. But so was something equally important: think about what comes next. Again and again, the former activists told Bevins they had been naive in thinking that just because they were in the right, things would turn out for the best. Mahmoud Salem, an Egyptian blogger who took part in the 2011 Tahrir Square protests that brought down the regime of Hosni Mubarak, recalled the final battle scene from ‘The Lord of the Rings,’ when the monstrous villain Sauron is defeated once and for all. Why, Salem now wonders, did they think the Arab Spring would be like the movies? Why didn’t they have a plan for what came next? As two Brazilians who took part in the mass protests in their country in 2013 told Bevins, “There is no such thing as a political vacuum.” Either you fill the gap, or someone else will come along and do it for you. (In Brazil, as we now know, that someone was Jair Bolsonaro.) In April 2026, Hungarians rightly celebrated the downfall of Viktor Orbán, Europe’s longest-serving far-right populist. But for Hungary, the task of repairing the country’s damaged institutions – not to mention dealing with the economic discontent that helped do for Orbán – has only just begun.

Yet defeat isn’t the end, either. In 2025, people who had campaigned to kick Trump out of power in 2020 had to pick themselves up and start again. Although Trump began his second term in far more menacing form, the opposition is finding ways to turn this against him. The anti-ICE protests in Minnesota at the turn of the year, for instance, were partly the result of years of patient organizing. Sarah Jaffe, a journalist and author who covers workers’ rights, points out that these efforts came on the back of existing campaigns for causes like better working conditions at the local Amazon distribution center, tenants’ rights, and free school meals for undocumented migrants. “These organizations are used to working together and responding quickly to crisis; they have built bonds of trust,” Jaffe writes, “and they have learned to align their goals without needing perfect agreement.”

This sort of campaigning isn’t just a necessary defense against far-right intimidation. Ultimately, it’s the only way we will reach better politics. If mainstream politicians – who are only as good as we make them – are not talking about the things that would really make our communities stronger and more secure, then we need to take the lead.

If you want to speak out, to oppose the far right’s racism or the excuses people make for it, do it. Your voice is legitimate.

For all the essentialist talk from right-wing nationalists about culture and identity, one thing they understand very well is that nations are made, not born. That’s why, in the UK, where I live, far-right activists spent last summer attaching national flags to lampposts around the UK and encouraging other people to do the same, not as a neutral expression of pride, but as a deliberate attempt to tie people’s sense of home to their false claim that the UK is under invasion. That the country is, in the words of Jim Ratcliffe – Britain’s richest man, who moved to Monaco in 2020, reportedly to save billions in taxes – being “colonized by migrants.”

Many of us do not agree. Take the example of Hannah White, a co-founder of the Sound Lounge, a popular local music venue in Sutton, south London. In the summer of 2025, she asked a group of men wearing masks not to attach a Union Jack to the lamp post outside the venue. They threatened her. White’s response was to invite customers to bring in other flags of countries and causes that meant something to them. She even exchanged messages online with a less hostile local supporter of the flag-raising. “I don’t want to be angry against people who are feeling like they want to celebrate their flag,” White later said. “It’s just, how do we celebrate everyone?”

A few days after White was threatened, the venue’s windows were smashed with a hammer, in the dead of night. She kept going. Hundreds of people contributed to a crowdfunding campaign to repair the damage. Challenging the far right’s narrative – and refusing to back down, without bitterness or hate – was an incredibly brave thing to do. But really, do we have any other choice?

This is an edited extract from Daniel Trilling’s ‘If We Tolerate This: How the British Establishment Made the Far Right Respectable,’ out now in the UK by Picador, an imprint of Pan Macmillan. Copyright 2026.

Daniel Trilling is a journalist and author based in London, UK. He writes about nationalism, migration, and human rights for publications including the London Review of Books, The Guardian, and the New York Times. His work has been shortlisted for the Orwell Prize, the Political Book Awards, and the Bread and Roses Award for Radical Publishing.

items.description: The ideas of the far right, once confined to the margins of politics, have gone mainstream. A decade ago, it was just about possible to think that the right-wing nationalism sweeping through liberal democracies around the world might subside of its own accord. Whatever discontents it profited from…

items.title: Zeteo Turns Two! Exclusive Photos From Our Anniversary Party
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Two whole years!

Last week, Zeteo took a night off and marked its second birthday with a jam-packed, electric evening in Manhattan.

Held at acclaimed Lebanese bistro Au Za’atar in Midtown and sponsored by Substack, the celebration brought together entertainers, politicians, and journalists – from Chef José Andrés to Zohran Mamdani.

The night was a testament to the power of independent media, and a measure of a new age of journalism built on truth-telling, holding power to account, and asking the tough questions that other outlets won’t. In this increasingly chaotic media landscape, we’d like to think Zeteo has become a force to be reckoned with.

We’re thrilled to share these photos with you. We couldn’t have made it two whole years without the generous support of you, the subscribers, and especially our paid subscribers. Thank you for having our back.

Alright, party over. Year three of Zeteo starts now! We have no plans to stop making noise with hard-hitting interviews, sharp analysis, and bombshell documentaries. And we want you along for the ride because this is your platform as much as ours. So tell us in the comments: what do you want from Zeteo this year? We’re listening.

Onwards and upwards!

P.S., find more photos from the party here.

Catch up on more from Zeteo’s anniversary celebration:

items.title: Trump Is Only Pretending to ‘Fight’ Antisemitism
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1970, national guardsmen shot dead four students and wounded nine during an anti-Vietnam War protest at Kent State University in Ohio. No soldier was held accountable, but images of the killings remain famous, most recently being republished as Donald Trump sent the National Guard to Democratic cities.

Happy Monday; it’s Swin again. This is the first full week of Jewish American Heritage Month 2026, and Donald Trump and his administration are continuing to push the absurd lie that they are committed to stopping antisemitism. This next statement won’t shock many Zeteo readers, but: no… the MAGA-era Republican Party – which constantly acts like it wants to jail the families who hide the little girl in the attic – does not actually care about fighting antisemitism.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we have another scoop for you underscoring just how little they care. Plus, Trump’s polling hits another low, the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, oil prices continue to soar, and acting Attorney General Todd Blanche thinks you need to show your ID to eat at a restaurant.

Ever since he returned to power, Donald Trump and his top officials have claimed they are “combating antisemitism” – and have used this as a pretext for launching crackdown after crackdown on free speech, immigration, students, civil liberties, and Trump’s enemies.

It may have been slightly easier for Team Trump to claim that it is fighting antisemitism if the administration weren’t also simultaneously pulling the plug on programs designed to fight antisemitism. Zeteo spoke at length with the director of one such program, which lost its longtime State Department funding shortly after Trump regained the keys to the Oval Office.

In mid-April 2025, Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced on Elon Musk’s Twitter that “under my direction,” the State Department has “canceled another 139 grants worth $214 million.” He added: “American taxpayers should not be funding misguided programs like ‘Building the Migrant Domestic Worker-Led Movement’ in Lebanon or ‘Get the Trolls Out!’ in the United Kingdom.”

According to its website, Get the Trolls Out is “led by the Media Diversity Institute (MDI) with the support of six partners spread throughout Europe.” The project has operated in multiple countries, and its programs are aimed at fighting antisemitism in media, social media, and politics. The project and MDI have long sought to push back against various forms of bigotry and hate, including anti-Muslim hatred.

MDI was also a signatory to a 2024 letter urging the Israeli government to allow reporters open access to Gaza. And in September 2025, the organization joined a protest in Brussels opposing Israel’s slaughter of journalists in Gaza. “We stand by our colleagues in Gaza who bravely risk their lives to document conflict,” Luc Steinberg, the head of operations at MDI Global, said in a statement. “We demand those responsible for their deaths be held accountable.”

But the group’s focus on combating antisemitism was so prominent that – according to documents that were read to Zeteo – when the State Department inked its contracts with MDI to help fund its “Trolls”-related work, the titles or lead paragraphs of multiple grant applications were specifically about fighting anti-Jewish hatred.

The organization had partnered with the US State Department over the course of Republican and Democratic presidencies, and the first Trump administration saw the work worthwhile enough to keep funding the project during his initial four years in office. That all changed last year.

The head of MDI says that the Get the Trolls Out initiatives can no longer operate in multiple countries, now that the Rubio-era State Department pulled the funding. Efforts to appeal to the US government and restore the partnership have, apparently, gone nowhere in recent months.

“We still believe there has been some misunderstanding of the work we have been doing under the State Department’s funding; combating antisemitism is the objective we have in common,” Milica Pesic, executive director at the London-based MDI, tells Zeteo. “It’s the people, the communities we care for, who suffer the most because of this misunderstanding.”

For more than a year, this is the game that the Trump-Vance administration has played, and you’d have to be a stooge or a chump to fall for it. Just last week, Trump uber-loyalist Harmeet Dhillon, one of the Justice Department’s most resplendently MAGAfied figures, delivered a speech comparing the alleged antisemitism among the “educated elites” in the United States today to the rise of Nazi Germany.

Meanwhile, an array of Jewish groups and activists have spent the last year warning that the Trump administration’s actions carried out under the guise of countering antisemitism are, in fact, making American Jews less safe. A study last year found nearly three-quarters of American Jews can easily see through President Trump’s bullshit, and believe he “is using antisemitism as an excuse to penalize and tax college campuses.”

Indeed, the Trump administration is now in court trying to force the University of Pennsylvania to hand over a list of Jews on its campus. (The easiest possible IQ test on the planet is: If the Trump administration asks you to turn over your list of Jews, is it a good idea to do it?)

None of this should be surprising, given that Trump is a demagogue and demagogues are always looking for minorities to scapegoat. In the past decade, he has repeatedly stereotyped and lashed out at the clear majority of American Jews, sneering at them that they are bad, disloyal Jews because they don’t vote for Trump – and don’t appreciate everything he’s done for Israel.

This president and his goon squad running the federal bureaucracy only care about “antisemitism” to the extent that they can use it to try to crush their enemies, or to cynically play minority groups off of one another.

The GOP and this administration, by their actions and their words, very clearly do not give a damn about eliminating the scourge of antisemitism – they care about protecting the feelings and interests of their fellow far-right Trump fans in Israel.

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state mediareported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,679 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountry’s Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

  • ‘Operation Freedom’:Donald Trumpsaidthe US would start “guiding” ships from neutral countries through the Strait of Hormuz –apparentlywithout a naval escort. He also claimed “very positive” talks with Iran, but promised “forceful” action if the movement of ships was “interfered with.”
  • Iran balks:Iran’s state mediacalledTrump’s plan part of his “delirium,” and the country’s officialswarnedthat “foreign armed forces, especially the aggressive ​U.S. Army, will be attacked.”
  • Strait strikes:The UKsaida bulk carrier wasattackedby a small craft in the waterway. The US said its blockade of Iranian ports extended to a 49th ship.
  • OPEC acts:Seven countries in the oil countries’ alliancesaidthey would increase production in June, in pursuit of “market stability.”
  • Dire diesel:The closure of the Strait continued to affect US fuel prices. In Michigan, a swing state, diesel pricesreachedan all-time average high of $5.99.
  • Press plea:Pope LeomarkedWorld Press Freedom Day by paying tribute to “the many journalists and reporters who have been victims of war and violence.” Israel haskilledat least 260 journalists since 2023.

New poll lows are common for Donald Trump these days – however often he retreats to his own personal safe space, the Villages, to rave about his supposed success, as he did on Friday. But even by the president’s standards, the news from the Washington Post, ABC News, and Ipsos on Sunday was very far from good.

In the survey, Trump’s disapproval rating soared to 62%. Given that such numbers take work – not just adverse political or economic headwinds, but real effort to sink so low – it’s worth taking a look at just how Trump is screwing up. On the cost of living, a whopping 76% of Americans disapprove of his performance. (He says cost-of-living concerns are Democratic lies, which is, of course, a lie.) On inflation, 72% disapprove. (He says inflation is “solved” and “done.”) On the war with Iran, which is exacerbating such problems, 66% don’t like what Trump is doing. On the economy as a whole, disapproval stands at 65%.

Trump’s standing with Republicans remains good, at 85%, but only 25% of independents approve of the job he’s doing. Predictably, the WaPo/ABC/Ipsos poll made good reading for Democrats hoping to take the US House.

And then there’s the mental health question, which Trump himself keeps harping on about, as in his rambling speech at the Villages when he went back to the well about a “cognitive test” he supposedly aced. You know, the one about “a lion, a giraffe, a bear, and a shark [and] they say, which one is the bear?”

Oddly enough, given such utterances and according to the Post, “Nearly 6 in 10 (59%)” say Trump “does not have the mental sharpness” to serve effectively, while “a majority (55%) say he is not in good enough physical health to serve effectively. Both are slightly more negative than they were in the February survey and are significantly more negative than last September.”

  • Odd Todd:Acting AG Todd BlanchetoldNBC the indictment of former FBI Director James Comey for threatening Donald Trump with an “86 47” message made of seashells was “not just about a single Instagram post. This is about a body of evidence the grand jury collected over … 11 months.” Funnily enough, Blanche didn’t say what that evidence was – and also said thousands of other people who have broadcast such messages are not being investigated.
  • Threat spree:Trump carried on issuing actual threats,sayingformer AG Eric Holder, who resigned in 2015, was guilty of “treasonous conduct” for supposedly rigging the 2020 election – which Trump lost fair and square.
  • Italian job:Secretary of State Marco Rubio is off to Italy,TheGuardianreported, with a mission to thaw relations with PM Giorgia Meloni and Pope Leo, subjects of Trump’s abuse and bizarre AI posts.
  • Speaking of which…Trumpsharedan image of himself, Rubio, JD Vance, and Doug Burgum, all shirtless, with a bikini-clad woman in the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool. In the real world, the construction site for Trump’s renovation of the pool was sprayed with graffiti referencing Comey’s “86 47” message.
  • True colors:Trump alsoposteda real picture of House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, accompanied by racist abuse. Jeffriesfiredback.
  • Giuliani hospitalized:Former New York Mayor turned Trump henchman Rudy Giuliani, 81, was on Sunday in critical condition in a Florida hospital, his spokespersonsaid.

According to a debunked if persistent urban myth, the profile of which prominent historical figure is carved into the back of the head of the Abraham Lincoln statue at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelextendedits detention of two members of an aid flotilla, as their lawyer said they’ve suffered beatings by Israeli authorities. Meanwhile, Israeli National Security Minister (and sociopath?) Itamar Ben-Gvir celebrated his birthday withcakesdecorated with nooses and guns: a reference to the recent law imposing the death penalty for Palestinians only.
  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺 Drone wave:Ukrainelaunchedattacks against Russia, hitting Baltic naval and oil facilities. A Kremlin spokesperson said such strikes could cause global oil prices affected by the US-Israel-Iran war to rise yet further.
  • 🇿🇦 Virus alert:Three are dead and at least three are in intensive care in asuspected outbreakof the rodent-spread hantavirus on a cruise ship in the Atlantic. Victims were taken to South Africa, where authorities were checking for further cases.
  • 🇲🇱 Critic taken:A former minister turned critic of the Mali junta was abducted by armed and hooded men, APreported. Mountaga Tall, a former education minister, now leads a party opposed to the military government, which has been attacked by militant groups.


Not sure what kind of restaurants Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche eats at, where he says you have to show ID to enter. Maybe they’re next door to wherever Donald Trump buys groceries.

  • On

    CNN’s ‘State of the Union,’former Massachusetts congressmanBarney Frank, who has entered hospice care, continued publicity for a book he may not see published. A leading campaigner for LGBTQ+ rights, Frank, 86, now thinks Democrats have shifted too far left too quickly on the “T” part of that acronym. Nor is he much of a fan of Graham Platner, the insurgent progressive who beat Governor Janet Mills in the Maine Senate primary. According to Frank, Platner “actually shares with Trump this capacity toward making the most out of the anger that people feel.”

  • Former Fox host

    Jeannine Pirrois both the US attorney for the District of Columbia and the owner of one of the best rude nicknames in politics. On Sunday, “Judge Box of Wine”spoke to Jake Tapperof CNN about the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner in DC last weekend. Confirming it was the suspect who shot a Secret Service agent saved by his bulletproof vest, Pirro also seemed inadvertently to confirm the shooter’s manifesto use of the phrase “a pedophile rapist and traitor” was a reference to Donald Trump.

  • On

    CBS’s ‘Face the Nation,’Senator Raphael Warnockof Georgia lamented the effective death of the Voting Rights Act of 1965, as confirmed this week by the right-wing Supreme Court in a ruling that triggered action in Louisiana to wipe out Black congressional seats. “Let’s be clear,” said the senator, also a pastor at thechurchwhere Martin Luther King, Jr. preached. “What happened this week is nothing less than a massive and devastating blow, not only to our democracy, but particularly to people of color in the South.”
  • Tuesday, May 5:Ohio and Indiana arescheduledto hold primary elections. Plus, EU finance ministers are scheduled to meet to discuss the Iran War. Panama’s President José Raúl Mulino issetto host Israeli President Isaac Herzog.
  • Wednesday, May 6:Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick issetfor a voluntary interview by the House Oversight Committee in its Jeffrey Epstein investigation.
  • Thursday, May 7:The US Court of Appeals for DCwillconsider the Pentagon’s bid to punish Senator Mark Kelly for telling troops to resist illegal orders.
  • Friday, May 8:Right-wing populist Laura Fernandez issetto take office as Costa Rica’s president.


Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

  • Thursday, May 7:The US Court of Appeals for DCwillconsider the Pentagon’s bid to punish Senator Mark Kelly for telling troops to resist illegal orders.
  • Friday, May 8:Right-wing populist Laura Fernandez issetto take office as Costa Rica’s president.

items.title:
Debunking 5 Myths A Pro-Israel Lobby Group Is Spreading About the US-Israel
 Relationship
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Almost everyone, at this point, has heard of AIPAC, it seems. The Israel-first lobby has made itself notorious through its hardline stance – and has become an increasingly toxic name in American politics, particularly on the Democratic side of the aisle. But that’s where AIPAC’s lesser-known counterpart, Democratic Majority for Israel (DMFI), hopes to fill the gap being created as Democrats forswear AIPAC funding.

Founded in 2019 in the wake of Donald Trump’s election and dismantling of the US-Iran deal negotiated by the Obama administration – both of which many Democrats saw as having been supported by AIPAC – DMFI sends a softer message: It supports a two-state solution for Israel-Palestine. And it is sometimes critical of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. But make no mistake – it is here to shore up the flagging (one might say, evaporating) support for Israel among Democrats, through lobbying and, via its sister organization DMFI PAC, with political spending that totals over $25 million since its founding. As DMFI PAC’s webpage explains, the organization “works to elect pro-Israel Democrats up and down the ballot and defeat those few anti-Israel Democratic candidates” – a description it rounds off by explaining that “being pro-Israel isn’t only good policy, it’s also good politics.” That’s a catchphrase that will surely be familiar to long-time AIPAC watchers. And if there was any remaining doubt, it’s worth noting that not only are some of DMFI PAC’s biggest contributors are also top contributors to AIPAC, but also that DMFI and AIPAC often back the same candidates.

AIPAC lists on its website a series of supposed benefits the US receives as a result of its relationship with Israel – a series of myths that we responded to with the actual facts here.

So, last month, when DMFI posted its arguments for “Why the US-Israel Alliance Matters for Democrats,” we felt it was only fair to give its list the same treatment. Here are its assertions – and why they are so, so wrong:

“Democratic voters support maintaining the US-Israel alliance. Polling commissioned by… DMFI PAC, late last year shows that a majority of Democrats favor maintaining a strong US-Israel relationship.”

The Facts

Whatever the results of a poll by an organization explicitly dedicated to maintaining Democratic Party support for Israel may claim (and DMFI has not released the full data, or even the question it asked to obtain this result), multiple independent polls in recent years have shown cratering US public support for Israel, particularly among Democrats. For instance, a March 2026 NBC News Poll found that just 13% of Democrats hold a positive view of Israel, with 57% seeing it in a negative light. An October 2025 Pew Poll found that 77% of Democrats have an unfavorable view of the government of Israel. A February 2026 Gallup Poll found that a supermajority of Democrats (65%) have greater sympathies for Palestinians compared to those who favor Israel (17%). And, most recently, an April 7, 2026, Pew Poll shows that 80% of Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents view Israel negatively.

DMFI should release the full poll it conducted, including the precise phrasing of its question. Without that, we’re marking this one a complete myth.

“The US-Israel alliance is independent of any single administration or conflict. Israel maintains the ability to defend itself by itself and strengthens regional deterrence without requiring permanent US troop deployments.”

The Facts

The first part of this statement isn’t actually a myth; it’s a problem. No US relationship with any country should exist in a vacuum, with no regard to US politics or world events. As Thomas Jefferson said, the US should not enter “entangling alliances” – and what could be more entangling of an alliance than one that is entirely independent of reality? As we have seen with back-to-back administrations that have pursued an approach of unconditional support for Israel, the absence of any willingness to question the relationship is a harm to the US, not a reason for it to matter to Democrats.

When it comes to Israel’s ability to defend itself – and to do so “without requiring permanent US troop deployments” – would DMFI like to put this to the test? The US provides Israel with billions of dollars every year in taxpayer funding to support its military, and is also almost the sole source of Israel’s fighter jet fleet and most of its munitions. In recent months, the US has deployed multiple naval vessels equipped with missile defense systems to protect Israel from incoming Iranian ballistic missiles – firing several years’ worth of US interceptor production to defend Israel. Meanwhile, US troop deployments around the Middle East – many of which seem greatly intended to respond to conflicts generated relative to Israel – certainly seem permanent.

“US security assistance to Israel is worth the investment, comparable to what the US invests in other critical partners such as South Korea, Japan, and Ukraine. Unlike those allies, however, Israel does not require a permanent US troop presence.”

The Facts

On this one, DMFI is simply – and profoundly – factually incorrect. The US provides Israel with $3.8 billion a year in security assistance – but Korea and Japan pay the US for its role in their defense. In fact, South Korea pays the United States $1.14 billion annually in host nation cost-sharing under the 2024 Special Measures Agreement, while under the US-Japan Host Nation Agreement, Japan pays $1.3 billion a year. Korea and Japan also spend their own money when buying US weapons – rather than relying, as Israel does, on handouts from the US taxpayer. And while it is true that Korea and Japan host extensive US bases (which, again, they pay for), DMFI is also wrong that Israel does not require a permanent US presence: a recent presentation by the US Defense Department confirmed the presence of a long-rumored US base in Israel at Site 512, and since DMFI made their post, Israel has explicitly invited the US to relocate much of their regional presence to bases in the country.

“Preserving the alliance advances long-term US interests. Israel provides unique capabilities, including missile defense systems, cyber expertise, regional intelligence, and operational experience, that strengthen American security and reduce the likelihood that the US would face future crises without capable partners.”

The Facts:

Are those “unique capabilities” Israel provides, such as missile defense systems, the same Iron Dome, Arrow, and David’s Sling systems that the US itself subsidizes to the tune of billions of dollars? And have any of those systems ever been used to help defend the US (aside from our own forces who are based in Israel)? Moreover, given the conflict with Iran, how can anyone argue that the partnership with Israel “reduces the likelihood that the US would face future crises” – with or without capable partners? The US-Israel relationship is a direct cause of the crises we currently face, and Americans – certainly including an overwhelming majority of Democrats – would rather we not face them at all, rather than enter them at the behest of Israel.

Here’s a fact: DMFI faces an unenviable challenge. Its mission these days is to defend the indefensible to a swiftly thinning audience. Its greatest tool in this effort remains the war-chest of DMFI PAC and the constellation of dark money organizations that align with it. But when it comes to rational persuasion, all the group can offer are unfounded assertions, faulty logic, and, in at least one instance above, outright falsehoods.

If you visit the DMFI PAC webpage, you will find, prominently displayed, a quote from Democratic House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries: “I am grateful for the support of DMFI PAC and look forward to continuing to advance the unbreakable US-Israel bond for the good of the Middle East and the world.” For as long as Democratic leaders associate themselves with AIPAC, as well as an organization that, for all intents and purposes, functions as an AIPAC cut-out, the party’s credibility will be the greatest myth of all.

Josh Paul is the co-founder of A New Policy, which was founded in October 2024 with the goal of reshaping US policies toward Israel and Palestine and bringing them in line with America’s interests. Paul resigned from the Biden administration over its policy of unconditional support for Israel's assault on Gaza.

Robert McDonald is the senior legislative analyst at A New Policy.

items.title: Memo to Dems: Fight Fire With Fire!
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
The ultra-reactionary majority on the Supreme Court has finished gutting the Voting Rights Act. In Louisiana v. Callais, Justice Samuel Alito struck down the remainder of Section 2 of that law, reversing a lower-court decision that required Louisiana to draw two majority-minority districts, to ensure its African American population had representation. As Adam Serwer explains in the Atlantic, Alito’s argument rests on constitutional prohibitions against racism in the 14th and 15th amendments. A requirement to ensure Black representation is actually an “unconstitutional racial gerrymander,” Alito wrote.

The Republican governor of Louisiana, Jeff Landry, immediately halted primary elections already in progress so he could draw new maps disenfranchising as many Black residents as possible. The rest of the Republican Party is following suit. They might easily net 13 additional seats just in the South, eliminating a huge chunk of the Congressional Black Caucus. At the state level, it will be a bloodbath for Black representation, just as it was in the 1890s when Republicans’ political ancestors in the Ku Klux Klan finished consolidating Jim Crow tyranny.

Callais is so tendentious, it’s kind of morbidly fascinating.

In a sense, Alito has come up with an entirely new theory of constitutional interpretation that we might call “anti-originalism,” in which constitutional provisions are held to mean the opposite of what the people who wrote them said they meant. In terms of following the obvious and well-documented intentions of constitutional fathers like John Bingham and William Stewart, it is exactly like arguing that the preamble of the Constitution intends to create the worst possible union, prevent all justice, leave the community open to attack, and place “we the people” under the permanent subjugation of the British crown.

It’s one more ruling to add to the pile of Chief Justice John Roberts’s crimes against the Constitution, law, and plain common decency – much worse than the oppressions that kicked off the American Revolution in the first place, incidentally – a record that justifies a wholesale restructuring of the Supreme Court to restore freedom and democracy to the United States.

But in the meantime, Democrats should fight fire with fire. It’s time to redistrict every last possible Republican out of office. It’s time to unleash the baconmanders.

For years, election nerds have been drawing up the most outrageous possible gerrymanders to favor Democrats. Because liberals tend to be concentrated in cities, such maps typically involve cutting big metro areas like Chicago or New York City into narrow slices that then stretch all the way across the state, like rashers of bacon. This Illinois map, for instance, would eliminate three Republican seats in part by creating a district that stretches all the way from Chicago to Cairo, in the very southern tip of the state.

The argument for doing this is game theory 101. Since 2010, Republicans have conducted an utterly ruthless campaign of gerrymandering, which has succeeded wildly. In the 2012 election, for instance, Democrats got more votes for the House of Representatives, but Republicans kept a 33-seat majority because of their prior cheating. (Imagine what President Barack Obama might have accomplished with another trifecta from 2013-14.) In the 2018 election in Wisconsin, Democrats won the popular vote for the state assembly by more than eight points, but Republicans won almost two-thirds of the seats. That is arguably more aggressive cheating than anything Victor Orbán ever did in Hungary.

This happened in part because Democrats did not immediately fight back in states they controlled. In the prisoner’s dilemma, if you defect and the other side does not, then defecting is the smart strategy. More generally, in any competition, if you can win by cheating, people will tend to do it. Just look at professional sports.

If cheating cannot be prohibited, then the rational response is retaliation. If Republicans gerrymander, Democrats should counter-gerrymander, thus not only restoring a semblance of fairness to national elections but also proving that cheating is not a guaranteed way to win. As Greg Sargent writes at The New Republic, analysis from Fair Fight Action shows that if Democrats gerrymander their remaining blue states, they might net 10 seats, and if they win a big victory in the upcoming midterms (which seems likely) and then gerrymander swing states as Republicans did in 2011, they might net 22 seats. That would more or less counteract Republicans’ plans for Jim Crow II.

To be fair, Democrats have been fighting back. California and Virginia passed gerrymanders in response to Donald Trump demanding that Texas do a highly unusual mid-decade gerrymander. But the California map was not nearly as aggressive as it might have been, and the first round of retaliation did not take. Republicans responded to a taste of their own medicine by spitefully doubling down. Clearly, they’ll need another, larger dose.

And that illustrates a critical point: the Republican Party is not some coolly rational participant in a social science experiment. We are dealing with a pack of depraved monsters who love lying, cheating, and stealing for their own sakes. None but a party of utter moral degenerates could possibly nominate someone like Trump for president. They will keep cheating until it costs them many elections in a row, or they are forced to stop.

Make no mistake, a nationwide gerrymander-off is far from ideal. An election in which most states send single-party delegations that might as well have been elected by a statewide vote makes a hash of the whole idea of the House of Representatives, which is supposed to provide some semblance of local control. Ideally, such a goofy situation would open the door to some kind of détente in which fair maps could be mandated by federal law. Or better yet, representatives could be elected in multi-member districts with ranked choice voting (as advocated by Fair Vote), which would not only make gerrymandering basically impossible, but provide representation to minority party voters in red and blue states.

But the only way that is going to happen is if Democrats win national power, break the illegitimate, traitorous, racist majority on the Supreme Court, and restore fair elections by force. Republicans will not see reason unless they are strapped into the Ludovico Device and made to see.

Ryan Cooper is a senior editor at the American Prospect, and the author of ‘How Are You Going to Pay for That?: Smart Answers to the Dumbest Question in Politics.’

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] FURTHER TRIBUTES
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

This week’s cartoon is pretty self-explanatory, as our hard-working president installs gold statues of himself on one of his golf courses and prepares to spend one billion taxpayer dollars on his ballroom. Trump has actually floated the idea of adding his head to Mount Rushmore, though according to a long article I read in the New York Times, this would be a geological impossibility. I just decided to up the ante, having him replace all four existing heads with his own, and in the process created one of the most cursed images I believe I have ever drawn. The only line that may confuse people is the word balloon in the bottom middle panel, in which I am deliberately referencing the revised signage on the Kennedy Center: “The Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Center.” Which makes me cringe every time I read it. And in this version, Lincoln doesn’t even get his first name, which was also a deliberate choice. Also, the final panel was meant to evoke that thing in North Korea where everyone has the same haircut as the glorious leader, but I felt that specifically referencing it would be too heavy handed.

***

Flaming Hydra, the collective and writers and artists I was recently invited to join, had a MAD magazine week last week, spearheaded by Zach Rabiroff. When the fact of my teenage visit to the offices of MAD came up in the group chat, I was encouraged to write about it. I think it’s behind a paywall, but as subscribers to this newsletter, you get the bonus content. (Though I do encourage you to support the Hydra!) Big thanks to Zach, Maria Bustillos, and Joe McLeod for invaluable editing and fact-checking assistance.

MAD man

I grew up on MAD Magazine, and it had a profound impact on both my creative sensibilities and my general outlook on the world. Through the subversive satire of MAD, a whole generation of kids in the 1960s and 1970s learned to distrust advertising, politicians, and authority in general—all of which turned out to be lessons that would serve me well in life. And on a specifically personal note, it’s obvious that I was heavily influenced by the magazine’s text-heavy movie and TV parodies. Whenever someone suggests that my cartoons have “too many words,” I just want to say: have you ever seen an issue of the 20th century’s preeminent journal of satire and whimsical commentary, cleverly disguised as a comic magazine for children? 

MAD was published by the legendary William M. Gaines, a man seemingly born to the job. The American comic book itself had, arguably, been invented by his father, Max. In 1933, Gaines père was employed as a salesman at Eastern Color Printing when he took advantage of some unused printing presses to package newspaper comic strips into bound booklets. Funnies on Parade turned out to be an enormous hit, and Gaines went to found two companies of his own: All-American Publications (later merged into what became DC Comics), and then Educational Comics, or EC for short.


Funnies On Parade (1933) fair use image via Wikipedia

When Max was killed in a motorboat accident in 1947, his n’er-do-well son Bill took over the family business, and within a few years began publishing MAD in its original comic book format, along with a variety of suspense, science fiction, and horror titles—a body of work that would one day land him before a U.S. Senate subcommittee. There, he was interrogated by Tennessee Senator Estes Kefauver, who was less than appreciative of his contributions to American culture.

“This seems to be a man with a bloody axe holding a woman’s head up which has been severed from her body,” Kefauver famously said at one point. “Do you think that is in good taste?”

Gaines responded by defending his artist’s restraint and subtlety, noting that a different composition might have emphasized even more blood.

Within two years of the disastrous hearings, industry censorship and public opprobrium had led Gaines to shutter his comic book line—but not MAD, which had converted to a larger magazine format a year earlier.  The rest was history. 
 
***

How much did I love this magazine? When I was in my early teens, I’d visit my dad for the summer in Iowa, and I spent a significant portion of my days in the Rare Books room at the University of Iowa library, reading back issues and plugging nickels into a mid-1970s photocopier to make poor-quality, barely-legible Xerox copies of my favorite pieces. Older material from the magazine was also available in paperback anthologies (Certifiably MAD, Raving MAD, etc.), which I’d pored over at an even younger age, struggling to understand the numerous references that predated me, and, by extension, to make sense of the mysterious adult world they parodied. The MAD version of West Side Story was especially perplexing, moving the location east to the United Nations, and turning the rival street gangs into politicians from either side of the Iron Curtain. Since I had never seen the movie, nor had much idea who Khrushchev even was, it was like trying to decode a mysterious artifact that had somehow fallen through a hole in space and time—a key to secret knowledge I could sense, but not quite comprehend. 

There were dozens of movies I only knew through their MAD parodies. A while back, I spent a few weeks finally watching some of these, including Lawrence of Arabia—or, as I first knew it, “Flawrence of Arabia.” It was strangely familiar, like a friend I had only ever seen reflected in a funhouse mirror. And I became well-versed in secondhand cultural references, like the fact that the Ford Edsel had been a colossal failure, or that there had briefly been a television show about a man’s mother reincarnated as his car. When I started writing and drawing my own childhood cartoons a few years later—many of them multipage movie satires, drawn on lined notebook paper with a No. 2 pencil—they included punchlines referencing both the Edsel and My Mother the Car. Because I had read the jokes in MAD, and I knew they were funny, even if I didn’t fully understand why.

I could go on, but you get the idea. I was a fan, a devotee, and a student of MAD. I read, and re-read, and re-re-read Frank Jacobs’s 1972 biography, The MAD World of William M. Gaines, immersing myself over and over in the story of Gaines and his ostentatiously zany band of cartoonists (aka “the usual gang of idiots”). Looking back over that book recently, I realized that some of their wacky office pranks were actually just kind of cruel, and from my current vantage point, I was appalled that MAD’s artists did not retain any rights to their own work. Nonetheless, Gaines was the ringmaster of a circus that enchanted and captivated me, and he undeniably created something extraordinary in the process.

***
 
In my senior year of high school in Iowa, I applied for a U.N.-sponsored trip to the East Coast. I had moved around quite a bit as a kid, from Iowa to Florida to Georgia to Arkansas, and eventually back to Iowa; I was a child of divorce, and my mom always seemed to believe in the promise of the next horizon, the fresh start. Which was, in fairness, more of a possibility before the internet settled over us all like smog trapped under an inversion layer. I inherited her restless spirit, and was in a hurry to see more of the world. Within a couple of years I would drop out of college and move to New York City with barely any money in my pocket, and live in Williamsburg long before it was “Williamsburg.” But that’s an essay for a different day.
I was living with my dad at this point, and I saw a way to visit New York that he’d probably allow, despite his deeply small-town distrust of big cities. I submitted some sort of contest essay about the noble purpose of the United Nations, or the vital necessity of world peace, or whatever it was, I honestly have no idea. But it got me on the bus. We drove from Iowa to D.C. and got a tour of the Capitol from our Congressman, stopped in Philly to see the Liberty Bell, and then, finally, arrived in New York City.

We couldn’t have been greener, more clearly from the sticks, myself most of all. I got my first suit for that trip, and I use the term loosely: the salesman sold my father on a camel’s hair jacket and a pair of navy blue slacks, which made me look like a security guard in a mid-tier department store. But here’s the wild thing: somehow, apart from the various structured activities, the trip chaperones basically let a bunch of kids from Iowa run free in late-seventies New York City, unsupervised. And so of course the first thing I did was grab a couple of my buddies and make a beeline for the address I knew so well from the masthead of every issue of my favorite magazine: 485 MADison Avenue. 

How we even found our way there, wandering around an unfamiliar and overwhelming city, I don’t really know. But somehow we did, and, I guess, announced that we were teenagers from Iowa here for a visit, and… they invited us right in, which was apparently standard office policy whenever random kids showed up. Longtime MAD editor Nick Meglin gave us a tour of the offices: the stacks of flat files (what I wouldn’t give now to have had a half an hour just to poke around in that treasure trove); a wall covered in sketches from all the artists whose styles I knew so well—Jack Davis, Sergio Aragonés, Don Martin, Al Jaffee. 

And then a visit to Gaines’s office.



Photo: Dan Perkins

He was an avuncular man in his late fifties, with long grey hair swept back from his forehead and what I can only describe as a jolly beard. He wore a short-sleeved pinstriped shirt and thick black-framed glasses, the case for which was visible in his shirt pocket. According to his biography, he was compulsively neat and kept the items on his desk precisely arranged, but all I remember is a chaotic jumble: MAD zeppelins hanging from the ceiling, a giant paper mache King Kong head (designed by Aragonés) peering in through a window. 

Gaines greeted us from behind his desk, and then paused and gave me a piercing look and said, slowly and with great intensity, “Young man—you are clearly destined to become a modestly successful cartoonist with a relatively small, yet fiercely loyal, readership.” The lights flickered briefly and the windows rattled as a strange breeze whipped through the room, sending papers and artwork flying off his desk.

Okay, that didn’t really happen. Actually he chatted with us politely for a moment as I snapped a few photos with the Nikkormat I carried pretty much everywhere in those days, and then offered us coupons for something called THE NEW YORK EXPERIENCE, which Google tells me was a “long-running multi-screen extravaganza.” We didn’t use them, but I’ve had a lifetime of New York experiences since then, so I guess it all evens out. 

And that’s pretty much it. For a few short minutes, I stood in the presence of this absolute icon, more of a hero to me at that age than any musician or actor—not really a creator himself, but an enabler of creativity, and of cartooning, and satire, and much of what I hold dear in this world. This man who personified MAD Magazine at least as much as the gap-toothed mascot they’d appropriated from turn-of-the-century advertisements and old penny postcards. The moment still looms large in my memory, nearly half a century later: I made the pilgrimage to that shrine of an office, and I shook the hand of the man that made MAD.



Photo: Dan Perkins

 And that's it for another week! 

Until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] LESSONS LEARNED
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

This week’s cartoon refers back to an incident that occurred a full week ago, which is about a decade in Trump Time. After a gunman tried to get into the Correspondents’ Dinner ballroom, and was stopped by security before he got in, the Trump administration — never ones to waste a good opportunity — immediately pivoted to the need for Trump’s ballroom. And various Republicans started talking about using taxpayer money to pay for it, in direct contradiction of Trump’s oft-repeated promises that the whole thing would be paid for by private donors. This raises many questions. Is the WHCA, a private organization unaffiliated with the White House, supposed to let the president host their events moving forward? What better way to demonstrate the independence of the media! Of course, as far as I’m concerned, the whole thing was shameful to begin with, and should have been canceled anyway. As the last panel suggests, the media have no business celebrating freedom of speech with an administration devoted to the exact opposite. 

And while I am no fan of James Comey, the idea that an instragram pic of seashells spelling out “86 47” was intended as a call for assassination is absurd on the face of it. 86 is a food service term for something being out of inventory, and also slang for getting ejected from a bar due to unruly behavior. It's not a term for killing someone! In my experience, people who get kicked out of bars are not summarily executed! Like so many things in this administration, it is ludicrous on the face of it, because they are lying and they know that most people know they are lying, and the very act of it is an assertion of power.

Oh, and if you’re wondering why Patel is wearing that hat — here’s a picture of him from his podcaster days, hawking K$H merch. Extremely serious people we’ve got, running this government of ours.

Wish list thanks to St. Chris, Pam S., and this week's inevitable unintentionally-anonymous gifter!

And that's it for me this week!

Until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE BEST AND THE BRIGHTEST
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

So, I made it to Iowa City and back, a whirlwind weekend trip sponsored by my local paper there, Little Village, and their publisher, my friend Jordan Sellengren. We had a comics panel featuring several local cartoonists and moderated by Kembrew McLeod, with whom I have previously spoken at length for an interview. Also in attendance were Paul Brennan, with whom I spoke by Zoom when my previously-scheduled event which was supposed to celebrate LV’s 25th anniversary had to be canceled due to the big East Coast blizzard last January; former FCC head and longtime Iowa City resident Nicholas Johnson; and a former art professor of mine, Mel Andringa. Mel says that he always remembered me from my feedback about guest speakers in class symposiums, which was apparently notably cranky for an 18 year old — “why are we even listening to this person?”, or something like that. I have no memory of this, but have to admit that it does sound like something I would say, especially as a young man untempered by a little more life experience. Anyway, it was great to meet and/or spend more time with everyone.

While the regional Cedar Rapids airport is actually closer to Iowa City, I know from long experience, when I used to fly in to visit my dad, that CR shuts down at the slightest hint of a raindrop, so I flew direct to Des Moines. My flight Friday evening, on one of those small regional jets, was delayed by bad weather — a string of tornadoes throughout the midwest, apparently — and while there were no stomach-churning altitude drops, there was plane-rattling turbulence most of the way there, the kind that makes you feel like you’re in an Indiana Jones movie and you’re not entirely sure the plane is going to hold together, so that was fun. And then my flight out was delayed by an hour due to a ground stop at LGA because of fog. If I’m not sleeping on an airport floor, and I make it where I’m going in one piece, then I figure I don’t have too much to complain about, but the weather on this trip was not my friend.

And fortunately the trip schedule was such that I was able to get back home in time to correct last week’s truly inexplicable typo — “revals” instead of “rivals”. Don’t ask me where *that* came from, but thanks to all who pointed it out — I’d rather have too many emails about a typo than none.

This week’s cartoon is a bit of a hodgepodge of various news stories from the last week or two. I realized too late that I missed my window of opportunity to do something in time for the White House Correspondent’s Dinner, with the media hosting the man who wants to throw them all in jail, and bravely protesting by wearing little first amendment pocket squares, god help us all. You probably know that Obama’s mild roast of Trump at the WHCD is Trump’s supervillain origin story, allegedly inspiring him to run for the presidency out of revenge. I actually attended one of these things myself, back when Clinton was president — media reformer James Fallows had been put in charge of US News and World Report by Mort Zuckerman, and brought me in to contribute a cartoon for a few months, until Zuckerman said “what the hell is this?” and made him drop me. As a consolation prize, Jim invited me to sit at the US News table. I saw Oliver North there, with whom -- fun fact -- I shared an editor at St. Martin's Press at the time, and Sharon Stone, who was dating the editor of the San Francisco Examiner, where I'd run for a number of years, and then somehow talked my way into the Vanity Fair afterparty where I chatted briefly with Matt Drudge. What can I say, it was the nineties. (A few years later, Drudge would steal a photo I took of Michael Moore arguing with Bill O'Reilly in a parking lot at the DNC in Boston, clumsily altering it by flipping it horizontally and changing the traffic lights so that they were green in all directions, but that's another story.)

(LAST MINUTE UPDATE: I had the WHCD on in the background last night when I looked up and saw that ... things were not normal. Trump reportedly wanted to come back out and give his speech after the shooter was apprehended, but the secret service wasn't on board with that. I am curious if he would have come out and given the same speech, in which he was planning to roast the media. He seemed momentarily subdued when he spoke to the press corps later in the evening, though of course with him that never lasts with him. And he did still manage to use the incident in order to promote his ballroom, where he said such a thing never would have happened, and I suppose he’s technically correct — security inside the perimeter of one of the most highly protected complexes on the planet is probably better than that of a hotel on the street. I was also reminded, watching the chaos unfold, that the year I went, someone at US News told me to be at the Hinckley Hilton at a certain time, and I spent a bunch of time trying to figure out what and where the Hinckley Hilton was, until someone finally told me it was a rather dark nickname commemorating the Reagan assassination attempt, not the official name of the hotel.)

The first panel of this cartoon reflects my own deeply held belief, borne of simple observation, that Trump’s staff works desperately to keep bad news away from him. The second panel presented a dilemma — basically everything else in this strip is based on an actual news story, and every panel could have the “this is true!” footnote, but I felt like this one particularly needed it. Dr. Oz told the story in a podcast that Trump believes that diet soda, because it kills grass when poured on the lawn, might also kill cancer. That seems like a story a lot of readers might have missed, and without the footnote, I think a lot of people would be left scratching their heads at that panel.

Pete Hegseth did just eliminate mandatory flu vaccinations for the troops, because why would troops residing in tightly packed quarters possibly need protection from highly communicable diseases. Hegseth has famously said that he doesn’t wash his hands after going to the bathroom because he doesn’t believe in germs. RFK is also said to reject germ theory, the 19th century foundation of modern medical science, in favor of the miasma theory, dating back to ancient Greece, that disease is caused by bad air. 

You have probably read that Kash Patel is suing the Atlantic, for its reporting on his partying and erratic behavior, including a morning after when he could not be roused, allegedly leading his underlings to request breaching equipment to get through his locked door. If this lawsuit actually proceeds to discovery, there may be popcorn shortages, because that’s going to be quite the show. I mean, we have all seen the photo of Kash Patel chugging a beer as he partied with the Olympic hockey team, while the search for Savannah Guthrie’s mother was in full swing back in the states. I threw in the Trump “who are you, again?” line because I suspect that his days in the administration may be numbered, though you can never really be sure — Trump seems to mainly fire women, after all.

Trump’s presidential daily briefing really does consist of a two-minute sizzle reel of things blowing up in Iran, according to news reports. And there does seem to be a lot of insider trading going on, both in the stock market and the prediction markets, conveniently timed around Trump’s various shifting pronouncements. 

It has been a busy week in Tomorrowland! Wish list thanks to Daniel N., and what may be a new record of three unintentionally anonymous gifters (no gift receipts in the packages), including the generous soul who sent the cookware set, for which I am most grateful. Bye-bye, cheap teflon cookware!

And so, until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] WHAT WILL HE SCREW UP TODAY
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

Greetings, readers of the future! I’m writing these words on Thursday, to be queued up for weekend distribution. This is something I always struggle with, given that I usually queue up the newsletter shortly before it goes out, keeping an eye on the news the whole time to make sure you-know-who hasn’t done something that negates the previous week’s work somehow. But I had a curtailed work week due to upcoming weekend travel, which incidentally always screws up my writing process. Even though I generally try to have the cartoon wrapped up by Friday, part of being able to do that is knowing that I have the weekend as a buffer zone in case I run into a mental block or something happens in the news that necessitates a change to the cartoon. It’s pretty neurotic, I’m aware, but this is the brain I’ve got to work with and I do the best I can with it. It makes travel complicated; I haven’t been on a proper vacation since 2023. I’m hoping to switch that up this summer, even if it means running the occasional evergreen that, like this one, may end up feeling completely out of sync with the news cycle, depending on what history’s most special boy Donald J. Trump does on any given week. Of course, with rising travel costs and the general global uncertainty unleashed by history’s most special boy, well. We shall see if these best laid plans gang a-gley.

Anyway I decided to go with an evergreen from my drafts folder this week. Which gives you all that rarest of treats, a cartoon that does not even mention Donald Trump! I’ve had this one mostly ready to go for awhile, though once I pulled it out I had to keep poking and prodding at it, because that’s just how I roll. It took me awhile to decide which tech billionaires to use. Altman and Zuckerberg felt obligatory, but Musk could have been just as easily replaced with Bezos. (Any more than three of them would have crowded the panel too much.) And I try to keep my cartoons internally consistent, so I felt that while I was presenting them as a united front, I also needed to add the line about how they all hate each other — Musk and Altman, in particular, are currently feuding, and of course Musk and Zuckerberg once talked about having an MMA style fight, though Musk backed out of that. I was also aware that Sparky’s rundown of a.i. drawbacks was incomplete, and certain that I would be endlessly reminded of this throughout the week, so I added his line “and that’s just for starters.” A.i. proponents will undoubtedly let me know that ACTUALLY the technology has *many* uses while ignoring the secondary question of the cartoon — at what cost? This is all being shoved down our throats by a handful of billionaires who want to reshape the world in their image and don’t care much about consequences. Are the benefits they claim worth the economic and environmental havoc they generate? Is your chatbot pal worth wrecking the entire world? It reminds me of a novel that I read in high school, by Thomas M. Disch, called The Genocides, about an invasive alien plant species that colonizes Earth and eventually makes it unlivable. I feel like we’re watching the digital version of this happening in real time and I am not a fan. 

ANYWAY! I’m off soon on my whirlwind trip to my old stomping grounds, Iowa City, a make-up trip I promised last January when I had to cancel an appearance there due to the gigantic blizzard. I fly in late Friday and out Sunday morning, so I won’t have as much time there as I would have preferred. I wanted to stay an extra day but the flight schedules for Monday just didn’t work out. Maybe I’ll see/will have seen some of you there! As I say, this is a missive from the distant past of last Thursday. 

Wish list thanks to Michael B., Nova V., and the (probably unintentionally) anonymous gifter who sent the cooking pan (no gift receipt, once again). I’m not proud of this, but when I was first divorced I barely knew how to cook, and I left all the good cookware with my ex. It made sense at the time, our son was still in high school and she was going to be cooking for the two of them. One of her parting gifts on my way out the door was a set of really cheap, terrible cookware, the kind you might buy for a kid going off to college for the first time. Now it’s eight years later and I have become a normal human who cooks pretty regularly, and that stuff is in bad shape — it’s getting beat up, and it’s teflon, so it’s possibly even hazardous to my health at this point — and I’m starting to replace it with, you know, grown-up cookware. I’m 65 years old now, it feels like time! Now if only I could learn to dress like an adult! But you can go a long way with t-shirts and jeans when you are a cartoonist.

Okay, I think that’s it for this week. I appreciate you all, you’re literally the reason I am still able to do this work. Given that I have mostly outlived the altweekly industry that birthed my career, that’s no small thing.

Until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] WHAT WILL HE SCREW UP TODAY
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=d9698d02ad&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

Well. Having just had that milestone birthday (celebrated at different points through the weekend with my son and various friends), I think I am now officially a wizened political observer, and friends: I've never seen anything like this last week. The president of the United States posts “open the fuckin’ strait you crazy bastards” and threatens that “a whole civilization will die tonight.” You’d think that even Republicans in Congress would be a little uneasy at the prospect of a deranged dementia case igniting World War III, but I guess you would be mistaken. And until they start to turn on him, removal from office isn't going to happen, though I'm still in the "impeach him anyway" camp, because it's the right thing to do, and it's a matter of political messaging. (Not that Democratic leadership is interested in even raising the issue (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=6da3abbfbd&e=ed35377427) .) Anyway this cartoon is not about the

situation in Iran, exactly, and it’s definitely not meant as a 1:1 parallel. It’s about the overall anxiety, the constant sense of dread that is inescapable under Trump’s presidency. We're trapped on a roller coaster ride, and I hate roller coasters. “A whole civilization will die” is the most extreme example so far, but every morning, I wake up and check the news with a sinking feeling in the pit of my stomach, wondering what the absolute fuck this lunatic has done now. Or what he will do in a few hours. And then what he will do after that.

I myself am very tired of it! When I came up with this shirt (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=e1edf38171&e=ed35377427) thirteen or fourteen years ago, I thought it was just an amusing bit. I did not expect it to become my daily mantra.

I don’t actually enjoy writing cartoon after cartoon about Donald J. Trump, history’s most special boy. Those of you who were kind enough to back the Kickstarter, and have copies of OUR LONG NATIONAL NIGHTMARE (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=73bc1606bb&e=ed35377427) , can go back and look through — I had a much wider range of topics when Biden was president. Frankly, Biden rarely made appearances in the strip. I was okay with that. Though thinking back on it, that “wider range of topics” frequently included the possibility that Trump might regain power, and what a horrific prospect that was. And here we are!

And we’re only a year and change in. The only bright spot on the near horizon is the possibility of a blue wave in November, and while I am hoping for that as fervently as any of you, November feels like a long time away right now. A lot can happen between now and then, including the death of whole civilizations. I just don’t think the former host of Celebrity Apprentice should have that power.

NOTE TO IOWANS: my event in Iowa City is on Saturday (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=2d6291cecf&e=ed35377427) , April 18 from 3-5 p.m. at Merge. It’s a panel with four other cartoonists, moderated by Kembrew McLeod. There will apparently be pizza! And even a limited number of copies of OLNN for sale, making this possibly the only opportunity for an in-person booksigning. And if you already have the book, or any of the others, feel feel to bring them, I’ll sign whatever you’ve got! I mean, within reason.

And on a completely different note, this is George. He is 28 years old and lives in the basement of an UWS pet store, down with the aquariums. I used to visit him more often when I had a fish tank, several years ago, and was strolling by on a pleasant afternoon and decided to drop in and see if he was still around. Happily, he is!

And finally, one for the "graphic design is my passion" file.

Wish list thanks this week to Karen C. And that's a wrap on another exhausting week.

Until next time,

Dan
