items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE UNITED STATES OF ANXIETY
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=2df192466a&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

Well. Having just had that milestone birthday (celebrated at different points through the weekend with my son and various friends), I think I am now officially a wizened political observer, and friends: I've never seen anything like this last week. The president of the United States posts “open the fuckin’ strait you crazy bastards” and threatens that “a whole civilization will die tonight.” You’d think that even Republicans in Congress would be a little uneasy at the prospect of a deranged dementia case igniting World War III, but I guess you would be mistaken. And until they start to turn on him, removal from office isn't going to happen, though I'm still in the "impeach him anyway" camp, because it's the right thing to do, and it's a matter of political messaging. (Not that Democratic leadership is interested in even raising the issue (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=add9ff492c&e=ed35377427) .) Anyway this cartoon is not about the

situation in Iran, exactly, and it’s definitely not meant as a 1:1 parallel. It’s about the overall anxiety, the constant sense of dread that is inescapable under Trump’s presidency. We're trapped on a roller coaster ride, and I hate roller coasters. “A whole civilization will die” is the most extreme example so far, but every morning, I wake up and check the news with a sinking feeling in the pit of my stomach, wondering what the absolute fuck this lunatic has done now. Or what he will do in a few hours. And then what he will do after that.

I myself am very tired of it! When I came up with this shirt (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=9a0d9b5a03&e=ed35377427) thirteen or fourteen years ago, I thought it was just an amusing bit. I did not expect it to become my daily mantra.

I don’t actually enjoy writing cartoon after cartoon about Donald J. Trump, history’s most special boy. Those of you who were kind enough to back the Kickstarter, and have copies of OUR LONG NATIONAL NIGHTMARE (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=edba161b2c&e=ed35377427) , can go back and look through — I had a much wider range of topics when Biden was president. Frankly, Biden rarely made appearances in the strip. I was okay with that. Though thinking back on it, that “wider range of topics” frequently included the possibility that Trump might regain power, and what a horrific prospect that was. And here we are!

And we’re only a year and change in. The only bright spot on the near horizon is the possibility of a blue wave in November, and while I am hoping for that as fervently as any of you, November feels like a long time away right now. A lot can happen between now and then, including the death of whole civilizations. I just don’t think the former host of Celebrity Apprentice should have that power.

NOTE TO IOWANS: my event in Iowa City is on Saturday (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=d4fa835f2f&e=ed35377427) , April 18 from 3-5 p.m. at Merge. It’s a panel with four other cartoonists, moderated by Kembrew McLeod. There will apparently be pizza! And even a limited number of copies of OLNN for sale, making this possibly the only opportunity for an in-person booksigning. And if you already have the book, or any of the others, feel feel to bring them, I’ll sign whatever you’ve got! I mean, within reason.

And on a completely different note, this is George. He is 28 years old and lives in the basement of an UWS pet store, down with the aquariums. I used to visit him more often when I had a fish tank, several years ago, and was strolling by on a pleasant afternoon and decided to drop in and see if he was still around. Happily, he is!

And finally, one for the "graphic design is my passion" file.

Wish list thanks this week to Karen C. And that's a wrap on another exhausting week.

Until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE INCREDIBLE SHRINKING TRUMP
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=16069e7db0&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

OK, this is admittedly a weird one. I was sitting at my desk trying to write a cartoon about How Donald Trump Fucks Up Everything, but it just wasn’t flowing, and felt flat and uninspired, and I just wanted to have some fun. I had a random thought about a doing a 50 Foot Woman bit, with Trump growing oversized and out of control, but then I got to thinking that actually it’s the opposite, we’re watching him grow diminished in real time, with approval ratings at historic lows even compared to his first term. Now, I have only the vaguest memory of The Incredible Shrinking Man, which I guess I probably saw as a kid at some point, so I took a quick look at the summary, and saw that the character starts shrinking after being exposed to a “mysterious cloud of gas.” I started turning that over in my head, and it occurred to me that Elon Musk is constantly being fined for dumping toxic waste from his various enterprises, so I thought that would be an appropriate way to start the ball rolling.

Panel two sets up the premise (obvious from the title of the strip of course). The third panel was a no-brainer — faced with a medical mystery like this, who does Trump have on hand to call? The experts he has put in charge of the nation’s health care system, obviously! The fourth and fifth panels just came to me unbidden — Trump in front of a giant phone, texting with both hands (note the bruise), characteristically denying that there are any problems whatsoever, and then getting swept off the desk by an errant feather duster. (Trump actually did misspell “strait” in a post, which put me in an awkward position — if I spelled it correctly, readers would be reminding me of the misspelling all week, but if I misspelled it, other readers would think the mistake was mine. I decided to thread the needle by putting the word in Trumpian quotes.) Then the question was, how to finish up? I could have gone with his ultimate fate — and if there were a White House cat, that would have clearly been the

way to end it, per the classic movie still of the tiny guy being chased by a giant cat, but of course everyone knows Trump hates pets, so that wasn’t gonna work. And then my mind wandered back to the original 50 Foot Woman riff, and I decided to close with that, using Miller to give the cartoon a bit of an ominous ending — even though we will eventually be done with Trump, the fascist, authoritarian movement he has empowered will still be with us. It also seemed appropriate to portray Miller as a vengeful lunatic with far too much power.

There are a lot of other panels I could have included in this, with so many potential scenarios, such as the reaction of right wing media to the shrinking president. I wrote one panel that didn’t make it in with Jesse Watters saying, “We must stand behind our tiny president — but be careful not to step ON him!” Which is a pretty good line, but there just wasn’t quite enough room.

Last week’s cartoon, with shirtless RFK eating fried rats, Trump in Sherlockian mode, “Drinky Guy” Hegseth and Bellhop Vance, still somehow managed to inspire a couple of “this isn’t satire, it’s just what’s really happening” comments, which long time readers may remember is my absolute least-favorite genre of feedback, as it both cheapens the effort I put into these things and absolutely misunderstands the nature of satire. If someone looks at *this* week’s cartoon and is inspired to post that comment, I think my head may literally explode. (Fortunately I’m starting up on Medicare on April 1!)

And finally, a few quick shots from Saturday's No Kings march, starting off with Padma Lakshi, Deniro and Sharpton at the front of the march:

And this was fun -- people send me pics all the time but I rarely run across my own work in the wild at these things. The Trump face on the sign is a mask IDW gave out at New York Comic Con in 2016, before he'd even been elected. Because it was such an early caricature, I had to look at it twice to be certain it was actually my own work!

Okay. Wish list thanks this week to David B., Barbara H., and (as often happens) an unintentionally anonymous gifter. Speaking of, several readers have alerted me to the changes in Amazon’s policy which might make it possible for my home address to be revealed, which of course I’m cautious about. But having dug into it a bit, it seems that this only applies to third-party sellers, so I’ll just be avoiding those.

And, one more reminder -- we currently have +/- 70 unsigned copies of OLNN remaining, and about 30 signed. If you want one, or want another one to give to someone, act quickly, (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=90ed3d1ae3&e=ed35377427) because we are close to clearing these things out.

Until next week!

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] DETECTIVE-IN-CHIEF
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=1cf669bb1e&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

My challenge each week, or at least most weeks, is figuring out how to encapsulate the ongoing, omnidirectional insanity and chaos of this administration. RFK Jr. doesn’t have anything to do with the main theme of this cartoon, but I wanted to include him as a reminder that there’s always something else going on at the same time, and it’s exhausting. As Trump and Hegseth celebrate Schroedinger’s war, which we have already won and yet are continuing to escalate, RFK is still working to tank the nation’s health infrastructure, Stephen Miller is still plotting the expulsion of everyone he hates, apparently Tom Homan is spending the day planning to deploy ICE to airports tomorrow, etc., etc. Also RFK released a weird a.i. video last week showing himself shirtless, fighting a (computelr generated) guy in a Twinkie suit in a wrestling ring, and I just thought it would be funny if shirtless RFK became a recurrent theme, like bellhop Vance. Walz had it exactly right in 2024 — these people are just

*weird*.

Newt Gingrich really did suggest nuking a new opening as an alternative to the Strait of Hormuz. And I wanted to at least gesture toward the fact that Vance is walking a tightrope, forced to support his degenerate master’s pointless war, but trying to signal that he’s not fully on board, because he knows full well that this is all going to go sideways in a big hurry and he still dreams of being president some day. And of course, Hegseth did say, out loud, that he can’t wait until Ellison takes over CNN so news coverage will be more favorable. Which is exactly how Putin handled the media in the early aughts; the second wave of oligarchs were Putin allies, and they took over Russian news media to ensure favorable coverage.

I set this one on the White House lawn just for a change of pace, visually, from all the Oval Office cartoons I do.

It is a strange moment, in which we stand on the brink of an abyss. I mean, yes, it feels like we’ve been standing on the brink of an abyss for quite a long time now, but now we’re facing a global oil shock, a global economic meltdown, even global famine as fertilizer supplies have been interrupted by the Hormuz closure. (I owe some taxes this year, due to the Kickstarter, but at least I can rest secure in the knowledge that my money will be spent on a pointless war that’s going to make everything more expensive!) And those are just the ancillary complications of this idiotic war of choice, which could absolutely escalate at a moment’s notice. Meanwhile, Donald Trump obsesses over his ballroom, and having the mint produce a gold coin with his face on it. And he also wants his birthday made into a federal holiday, his face on Mount Rushmore (that one’s old news, but apparently still an active obsession), and a “Trump-shaped monument built in our nation’s capital.

(https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=c201ad6f94&e=ed35377427) ” He may set some sort of record for the inverse relationship between his tanking approval and the number of monuments to himself he leaves behind. It all reminds me of this cartoon from 2017:

I’ve got some wish list thanks that will need to wait until next week. Also, it seems that it might be time to wind down the wish list, as Amazon has made some changes which could potentially expose my home address. I’m keeping an eye on that.

Finally, since it has been awhile, here's a bonus picture of the indolent gentleman of leisure with whom I share my apartment.

Until next week!

Dan

items.title: Judicial Notice (05.10.26): You Chose... Poorly
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Chambers-ranked and Harvard-educated, Jeff is the trusted closer for high-stakes disputes. His battle-tested system of managed communications empowers clients to make clear, strong decisions in a calm, informed environment. It makes the right resolution obvious. If settlement seems impossible, call him.

I hope all of the moms among you had a wonderful Mother’s Day. Our family had a lovely time celebrating with my mother and mother-in-law over brunch yesterday.

The highlight of last week for me was an event on Wednesday morning, a breakfast panel that I moderated about Biglaw firms moving into plaintiff-side and contingency litigation. Hosted by Burford Capital (a longtime sponsor of OJ), it featured two leading litigators, Eva Cole of Winston & Strawn and Avi Weitzman of Paul Hastings, and Burford’s own Evan Meyerson. The discussion was excellent, as I knew it would be—and the attendance was robust, which was a relief. (Whenever I’m involved with a live event, I never believe people will actually show up, until they do.)

In a prior edition of Judicial Notice, I alluded to some minor health issues. Thanks to those of you who reached out to express concern—but fear not, they’re quite minor. And they’re being addressed: I found an endodontist to deal with my root canal retreatment, as well as a physical therapist to help me with my Achilles tendonitis.

Now, on to the news. As usual when I publish Judicial Notice on Monday morning, this roundup covers news through Sunday, May 10; subsequent developments will appear in the next edition of JN.

Lawyer of the Week: Neal Katyal.

Since Wednesday night, everyone has been talking about acclaimed Supreme Court advocate Neal Katyal, a former acting U.S. solicitor general and current partner at Milbank. Why? Because of a tweet and subsequent TED Talk, discussing how he harnessed the power of Harvey to win the Supreme Court tariffs case, which were poorly received—for reasons I covered in my story from yesterday.

Despite my overall admiration for Katyal, I opined that his tweet and TED Talk were ill-advised, and it appears I’m not alone. In a reader poll, around 70% of you expressed a “very negative” or “negative” view of his tweet and TED Talk, with only 15% on the positive side. But if you haven’t done so already, check out my post and read the substantive, thoughtful statement that Katyal shared with me, explaining the message he wanted to convey in his tweet and talk. What he was trying to communicate was entirely sensible, even wise; the execution just left something to be desired.

Other lawyers in the news:

  • In remarks made last month at a private club in Washington, D.C., former special counsel

    Jack Smithcriticizedthe U.S. Department of Justice under Donald Trump, alleging that the DOJ had been “corrupted” by Trump loyalists.

  • After he argues in defense of the Trump administration’s executive orders against four Biglaw firms, before the D.C. Circuit this coming Thursday, Deputy Associate Attorney General

    Abhishek Kambliwilldepart from the DOJ(for an unknown position in the private sector).

  • Reflecting the Justice Department’s challenges in attracting and retaining talent, Assistant Attorney General

    Brett Shumateannouncedretention bonusesfor lawyers willing to stick it out at the Civil Division—as well as $25,000 signing bonuses for lawyers willing to join certain (especially controversial) sections of the Civil Division, such as ones focused on investigating youth transgender treatments and advancing the Trump administration’s immigration agenda.

  • U.S. Attorney

    Jeanine Pirrois also paying outretention bonuses, in the range of roughly $5,000 to $7,000, to federal prosecutors in D.C.
  • Kevin Bolan, who leads the Civil Division in the U.S. Attorney’s Office for Rhode Island, is beinginvestigatedby a special counsel, ProfessorNiki Kuckes. Bolan reportedly failed to inform JudgeMelissa DuBosethat an undocumented immigrant was wanted for homicide in the Dominican Republic—and after Judge DuBose ordered his release, the Trump administration issued apress releasecriticizing Judge DuBose, a Biden appointee, for ordering the release of a “criminal illegal alien… with an international warrant for homicide.”

  • If you’re fascinated by the improbable career trajectory of

    George Conway—theWachtell Liptonpartner turned Trump antagonist turned Democratic candidate for Congress—check out Timothy Bella’s profile of Conway forThe Washington Post(gift link).

  • The Georgia Supreme Court

    sanctionedassistant district attorneyDeborah Leslie, previously mentioned in these pages for an AI-related snafu, by suspending her privilege to practice before that court for six months.

  • Congratulations to

    Robert Boxie III, a Harvard Law School graduate andformer associateatVinson & Elkins, who this summer will become the youngest bishop in the United States. Pope Leo announced Boxie’s promotion last Friday, as reported byThe Washington Post(gift link). From Biglaw associate toYour Excellency—pretty excellent!

In memoriam: Abraham Foxman—who graduated from NYU Law and used his legal training to fight antisemitism, as a lawyer and then the CEO of the Anti-Defamation League—passed away at 86. May he rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Judge James Ho.

Last Wednesday, at a Federalist Society event in Los Angeles, Judge James Ho (5th Cir.) called out some of his fellow judges. Or as former Ho clerk Eric Wessan put it (in sharing a video of his former boss), Ho criticized “conservative judges for ‘punching right’ but not left,” while “call[ing] out his colleagues for cowardice in the face of unpopular decisions.” For a write-up of his comments, see Bloomberg Law.

After reminding the audience of his boycott against hiring clerks from Yale Law School—after free-speech problems at YLS, and the administration’s unacceptable response thereto—and complaining that no judges joined him in that boycott (other than Judge Lisa Branch of the Eleventh Circuit), Judge Ho complained about what he described as a “double standard[]”:

Just last year, when the Heritage Foundation was charged with antisemitism, a number of judges made clear that they would refuse to associate with the Heritage Foundation. And they specifically boycotted an event that would have featured the Heritage Foundation’s work. There was even a whole panel of judges to talk about these issues during the most recent Federalist Society convention.

So just to review the bidding: It’s okay to boycott Heritage. But you can’t boycott woke law schools. Let’s just be very honest about what’s going on here. Let’s be candid about the double standards that plague the judiciary. It’s okay to boycott Heritage, because you’ll never be punished for attacking conservatives. It’s okay to boycott Heritage, because it’s okay to virtue signal to cultural elites. It’s okay to boycott Heritage, because judges who punch left are excoriated—but judges who punch right are celebrated.

For the full text of Judge Ho’s remarks, as well as supportive commentary, see this Volokh Conspiracy post by Professor Josh Blackman. For criticism of Judge Ho’s speech—and it really was a speech, even though he was nominally a moderator—see this Dorf on Law post by Professor Eric Segall.

I do wonder: will Judge Ho announce a boycott against hiring clerks from UCLA Law, after a recent disruptive protest of an appearance by James Percival, general counsel of the Department of Homeland Security? And at what point will Judge Ho reconsider his boycott against Yale Law?

The disruptive protest of Kristen Waggoner at YLS took place in March 2022, more than four years ago. In the intervening period, YLS introduced a number of reforms to protect free speech. To improve intellectual diversity, it hired two right-of-center faculty members, Professors Garrett West and Keith Whittington. And Heather Gerken, the dean of YLS during “The Troubles,” left last year for the Ford Foundation.

It seems to me that the administration of UCLA Law—whose response to Percival-gate was problematic on several levels, for reasons outlined in a letter sent to Dean Michael Waterstone by Jessie Appleby of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE)—is a bigger offender against free-speech principles than the (new) administration of Yale Law, now led by Dean Cristina Rodríguez (who has not, as far as I know, done anything problematic on speech issues).

So although I personally object to judicial boycotts—both the YLS boycott specifically, as well as the notion of judges going around boycotting X, Y, or Z (which strikes me as outside the judicial role)—if you’re going to do a boycott, do it right, Judge Ho. Take YLS off the list, at least on a “probationary” basis, and replace it with UCLA.

Could it be that YLS, because it’s a higher-ranked school than UCLA, makes for better headlines and more media attention? If that’s the issue, allow me to point out that Yale Law is no longer the #1 law school—so perhaps it can have the “consolation prize” of having its graduates eligible to clerk for Judge Ho. ;-)

By the way, Judge Ho suggests that judges who silently decline to hire clerks from certain schools, such as lower-ranked schools, are “boycotting” those schools. Personally speaking, I don’t consider that much of a boycott, which Black’s Law Dictionary defines as “an action designed to achieve the social or economic isolation of an adversary, especially by the concerted refusal to do business with it” (emphasis added). It seems to me that speaking out publicly against said adversary, by encouraging or pressuring others to join you in a concerted refusal to do business, is an important part of a boycott. (For example, I might be a gold-star gay, but I do not consider myself to be engaged in a “boycott”—or “girlcott,” as the case may be.)

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

  • Picking up on the theme of

    when judges stop being politeand start getting real, JusticesSamuel AlitoandKetanji Brown Jacksontraded barbs in a quick coda toLouisiana v. Callais(or actually, as a technical matter,). Last Monday, as Amy Howe reported atCallais v. LouisianaSCOTUSblog, the Supreme Court granted a “granted a request to immediately finalize its opinion in [Callais], in which it struck down that state’s congressional map, to allow Louisiana to draw a new map in time for the 2026 elections.” Justice Jackson dissented, claiming that her colleagues’ decision “to issue the judgment forthwith”—i.e., to give immediate effect to its ruling—represented “principles giv[ing] way to power.” Justice Alito, joined by JusticesClarence ThomasandNeil Gorsuch, criticized the dissent’s arguments as “baseless and insulting”—and said that instead of the majority going hog wild, it is “the dissent’s rhetoric that lacks restraint.” For more on theCallaisdust-up, read this editorial inThe Wall Street Journal(gift link), titled “Justice Alito’s Intriguing Footnote,” or listen to the latest episode ofAdvisory Opinions. And for a fascinating historical look at when justices have turned on each other, see thisSCOTUSblog postby Nora Collins.

  • In happier news about the justices, congratulations to Justice

    Brett Kavanaugh, who placed fifth among federal judges in last Wednesday’s Capital Challenge. He completed the three-mile road race in an entirely respectable 26:23, as reported byBloomberg Law(via Howard Bashman’sHow Appealing).

  • Justice Kavanaugh wasn’t happy with his time, telling Bloomberg Law that he should have trained more—and next Term, he might be able to spend less time in robes and more time in running shorts. At last week’s Third Circuit Judicial Conference, Chief Justice

    John Robertssaid that the Court’s current oral argument format has “blown up”—which he apparently meant not as a compliment, but as what happens when you go off Ozempic—and he added that the justices might revisit it this summer (via Lawrence Hurley onTwitter).

  • Why is a California judge in the pages of

    The New York Post? In a barnburner of abrief, the U.S. Attorney’s Office for Los Angeles shines the spotlight on JudgeFernando Olguin(C.D. Cal.), describing his history of dismissing serious criminal charges against undocumented immigrants. The government contends that Judge Olguin is engaged in an improper effort “to push back against ICE’s detention of aliens charged with federal crimes, if not against ICE itself.”

  • In other news of judges upsetting conservatives, Magistrate Judge

    Zia Faruqui(D.D.C.)apologizedat a hearing last Monday to Cole Tomas Allen, the California man accused of attempting to assassinate Trump at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, for the treatment Allen received inside a D.C. jail. Specifically, Judge Faruqui objected to Allen being placed on suicide watch, after hetold FBI agentsthat he didn’t expect to survive the attempted assassination.

  • Turning to the state courts, Justice

    Diana Hagenresignedfrom the Utah Supreme Court, after her ex-husband accused her of sending “inappropriate” text messages to an attorney who helped challenge a Republican-friendly map in her court. (Justice Hagen had previously recused herself from cases involving the lawyer in question,David Reymann.)

Job of the Week: a general-counsel role at a high-growth manufacturing company.

Lateral Link is working with a high-growth global manufacturing company to identify its first U.S.-based general counsel. Based out of Greenville, South Carolina, this is a hands-on, high-impact opportunity to shape legal strategy, infrastructure, and risk-management frameworks from the ground up, while working directly with executive leadership. The GC will oversee all U.S. legal matters, with a particular focus on dispute management, risk mitigation, and supporting complex commercial and operational decisions. The ideal candidate will have 10–20+ years of practice experience, a strong litigation background, and GC- or deputy GC-level experience in a fast-moving business environment. Interested candidates should contact Marion Wilson at mwilson@laterallink.com and Brittany Zoll at bzoll@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: Scott v.

Last month, Virginia voters approved a Democratic-backed ballot measure that would have reconfigured the state’s congressional map. The new map would have flipped as many as four seats in the U.S. House of Representatives from Republican to Democratic control in the upcoming midterm elections, moving Virginia’s congressional delegation from a 6-5 to 10-1 split in favor of Democrats.

But last Friday, in Scott v. McDougle, the Virginia Supreme Court held that the procedure used to advance this proposal violated the Virginia Constitution. So the court invalidated the referendum vote and nullified the electoral map, dealing what both

The vote was 4-3, with Justice Arthur Kelsey writing the majority opinion and Chief Justice Cleo Powell penning the dissent. Here’s the reasoning, via Reuters:

Under Virginia state law, two consecutive legislatures—with a ​state election in between—must approve a proposed constitutional ⁠amendment before it can be put to a vote.

The Democratic legislative majority approved the amendment in October, days before the November state election. Democrats, who gained additional legislative seats in that vote, then passed the amendment for a second time in January and scheduled the referendum for April.

Republicans filed multiple lawsuits, claiming in part that there was no intervening election ​since early voting had already started when the amendment was first passed. In Friday’s decision, the Virginia Supreme Court agreed.

Lawyers for Virginia said in a filing to the Virginia Supreme Court that they plan to seek emergency relief from the U.S. Supreme Court. But because the Virginia Supreme Court’s ruling turned on state rather than federal law, legal experts from across the ideological spectrum, from Ed Whelan to Richard Hasen, cast doubt on Old Dominion’s prospects for success. As Rick Hasen told The Times, the state’s likely argument on appeal—a claim that the Virginia high court improperly interfered with the state legislature’s power over elections—is “a long shot.”

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

  • . In an opinion by JudgeCantero v. Bank of America, N.AMichael Park, a divided panel of the Second Circuit held that New York’s interest-on-escrow requirement is preempted by federal banking law. JudgeMyrna Pérezdissented.
  • . A defendant who was convicted of three counts of unlawful possession of an unregistered silencer challenged his convictions under the Second Amendment—and in an opinion by JudgeUnited States v. SpeedMarvin Quattlebaum, the Fourth Circuit shot down his arguments. In concurrences, JudgeJ. Harvie Wilkinsonargued that “silencers are categorically unprotected by the Second Amendment,” while JudgeJulius Richardsoncontended that his court’s approach to evaluating Second Amendment claims, even if binding on theSpeedpanel, can’t be squared with the Supreme Court’sBrahimiframework.
  • . From John Ross ofUnited States v. BlockerShort Circuit: “After some musings on narrower ways to resolve whether there was a Fourth Amendment violation when Dropbox shared information about a user’s child porn with a quasi-governmental entity, this breezy Seventh Circuit opinion [by JudgeFrank Easterbrook] entrenches a circuit split by holding that the fine print in all the online terms of service you never read means you’ve consented to government searches of your electronic files. Some folks [including Fourth Amendment guruOrin Kerr] are skeptical.”
  • . In an opinion by JudgesBurlap and Barrel, Inc. v. TrumpMark BarnettandClaire Kelly, the U.S. Court of International Trade rejected the Trump administration’s attempt to reimpose broad global tariffs under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, the statute that Trump turned to after the Supreme Court smacked down his effort to impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). JudgeTimothy Stanceudissented.

Litigation of the Week: United States v. Nourafchan .

Last Wednesday, federal prosecutors unsealed two indictments, both filed in the District of Massachusetts, charging 30 individuals with participating in a massive insider-trading scheme that ran for roughly a decade, from 2014 to 2024. The indictments, in United States v. Nourafchan and

The plot described in the indictments involved garden-variety insider trading. Lawyers at top corporate law firms shared material, nonpublic information, concerning an estimated 30 future M&A deals, with co-conspirators. The co-conspirators then traded on it, earning tens of millions of dollars, and paid kickbacks to the attorneys.

What’s striking about the scheme is its sheer scope. It involved lawyers who collectively have worked at some of the most prestigious and profitable law firms in the world, including DLA Piper, Goodwin Procter, Latham & Watkins, Sidley Austin, Wachtell Lipton, Weil Gotshal, and Willkie Farr. (There are no allegations of wrongdoing against any of the firms; to the contrary, they’re described in the indictments as victims, and they have all been cooperating with law enforcement.)

The world of Biglaw is surprisingly small. So I wouldn’t be surprised if some of the individuals whose names have appeared in the indictments or surfaced in the news reporting—as defendants, cooperators, or unindicted co-conspirators—are known to my readers. They include the following:

  • Nicolo Nourafchan. A 2011 graduate of Yale Law School who went on to work at Sidley, Latham, and Goodwin, Nourafchan was an alleged ringleader of the scheme, recruiting other attorneys to join it.
  • Gabriel Gershowitz. A friend of Nourafchan from their time together as undergraduates at George Washington University, he graduated from Columbia Law and worked at Weil, DLA, and Willkie. After pleading guilty in February 2025, he startedcooperatingwith prosecutors (who as of now are recommending a two-year prison sentence for him).
  • Avi Sutton. An alumnus of Yale Law, where he was friends with Nourafchan, and a former Wachtell associate, Sutton isn’t named in the indictments. But various outlets, including Law360 and Roll on Friday, report that he’s the unindicted co-conspirator referred to as “CC-2.” He left Wachtell to work at the Liontree investment bank, where he was most recently the chief operating officer and general counsel (but as of Friday morning, he was gone from its website).

Beyond Biglaw, another lawyer, Robert Yadgarov, was also allegedly involved. A friend of Nourafchan and Gershowitz from their GW years, he was a Touro Law alum with his own personal-injury practice. He wasn’t privy to M&A info himself, but he allegedly assisted Nourafchan in recruiting.

My overall reaction—which may apply to certain other stories in this edition of JN—is admittedly obvious and unoriginal: how can such smart people do such dumb things? When you graduate from places like Yale and Columbia Law and then land that first job in Biglaw, you’re arguably set for life. You might switch firms, go in-house, or even become a legal blogger—I understand, more than most, that legal careers can take surprising turns—but big-picture, you should be able to spend the rest of your days doing reasonably interesting work, for decent (or more than decent) pay, and not in an orange jumpsuit (unless it’s by Hermès). Why risk throwing all that away?

Although the traders made tens of millions, it appears that the lawyers who provided the tips made considerably less. The Fejal indictment alleges that Yadgarov received $6.3 million from the traders, but it didn’t all go to him; he split it with Nourafchan, Sutton, and at least one other source. Round it up to $8 million—or heck, double it to $12 million—and divide by four, yielding $3 million. Setting aside all the legal and ethical problems and conducting a pure risk-benefit analysis, is it really worth it to throw away your career—and perhaps your life—for $3 million?

I wouldn’t do it for any price—not because I’m a paragon of virtue (although I like to think I’m reasonably ethical), but because I’m extremely anxious, and I couldn’t live for years fearing that I might get arrested any day. But even if I were a cold-blooded mercenary, you’d have to add another zero to the end of that $3 million for me to even consider such conduct, even setting aside the fact that it’s illegal and wrong (or at least illegal—I acknowledge there’s a debate about whether it should be illegal).

In other litigation news:

  • What’s the latest in the legal battle between Louisiana and two manufacturers of the abortion drug mifepristone? It’s complicated, but the bottom line is that as of now, because of an administrative stay issued last Monday by Justice

    Samuel Alito, mifepristone remains widely available nationwide. That stayexpiresat 5 p.m. today, however—so stay tuned.

  • I must confess that I wasn’t terribly interested in the long-running, acrimonious legal battle between Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni. Fortunately, I won’t have to devote any more brain cells to this topic: with a May 18 trial date fast approaching, they

    settledthe remaining claims (except forone claimby Lively, relating to what she alleges was a retaliatory defamation lawsuit filed by Baldoni).

  • Speaking of settlements, Apple agreed to pay

    $250 millionto resolve class-action litigation alleging that it made misrepresentations to consumers about the capabilities of its AI system, Apple Intelligence.

  • If your friends in finance have been buzzing about a salacious sexual-assault and harassment lawsuit that banker Chirayu Rana filed against his former employer, JPMorgan Chase, and a former colleague, here’s a helpful overview of the case from

    The Wall Street Journal(gift link).

  • Speaking of employment-related litigation against iconic companies, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission is

    suingThe New York Times, alleging that its hiring and promotion practices discriminate against white male workers.

Deal of the Week: the $3.9 billion sale of the San Diego Padres to a buyer’s group led by José Feliciano and Kwanza Jones.

Back in November, I broke the news of Davis Polk & Wardwell hiring Jon Oram, a top dealmaker in the world of sports law, from Proskauer. And Oram appears to be plenty busy at DPW: he’s leading a team advising the San Diego Padres and the family of its late owner, Peter Seidler, on the team’s $3.9 billion sale to a group of buyers led by private-equity billionaire José Feliciano and his wife Kwanza Jones. The buyers are represented by a Wachtell Lipton team led by Ed Herlihy and Eric Feinstein.

Law Firm of the Week: Paul Weiss.

Over the past year, Paul Weiss has lost a number of heavy hitters in litigation—including Karen Dunn, Bill Isaacson, and Jeannie Rhee, who left to launch Dunn Isaacson Rhee, and Kannon Shanmugam, who moved to Davis Polk. After rainmaking partners depart from a firm, there’s often less work for the associates they leave behind—which might explain why Paul Weiss parted ways with an unknown number of litigation associates, as reported by Above the Law and Law.com.

The firm characterized the reductions as “performance-based decisions based on the review process we conduct every year.” But on Reddit, purported Paul Weiss posters dispute that characterization, describing the cuts as “bogus performance-based send-offs—bogus because most affected folks are getting negative feedback in the annual review that none of the individual teams are corroborating.”

Meanwhile, Paul Weiss continues to expand its partner ranks. In its three-month-old Houston office, it added three new partners: tax partner James Cole (from Latham & Watkins), M&A partner Aisha Lavinier (from Kirkland & Ellis), and litigation partner Andrea Smith (from Gibson Dunn). Hiring litigators like Smith and Adam Hoeflich, who joined last month from Bartlit Beck, shows that PW isn’t abandoning litigation. But it’s not unreasonable to wonder whether the talent it has added is sufficient to compensate for the talent it has lost.

In other firm news:

  • My overall sense is that Biglaw litigation is treading water, while boutiques are bustling. Take

    Hecker Fink, just profiled by Tatyana Monnay forBloomberg Law—which now boasts 100 lawyers, including partners who came over from firms likeCravathandArnold & Porter.

  • Like Paul Weiss,

    McDermott Will & Schultealsoreduced its associate ranks—but unlike Paul Weiss, McDermott didn’t claim that the dozen or so associates it let go were fired for poor performance. Instead, a spokesperson explained that the firm “made the difficult decision to separate from a small number of associates across the firm, as we continue to align with shifting client needs”—and expressed gratitude to the departing associates, stating that “their work has made a meaningful impact on our clients and the firm.”

Move of the Week: Alston & Bird hiring IP trial lawyers Jennifer Golinveaux, Michael Elkin, and Sean Anderson from Winston & Strawn.

Continuing to expand in IP litigation, Alston & Bird hired a trio of IP trial lawyers from Winston & Strawn: Michael Elkin and Sean Anderson, in New York, and Jennifer Golinveaux, in San Francisco. Elkin previously served as vice-chair of Winston and managing partner of the New York office, while Golinveaux had been global co-chair of Winston’s IP practice. They’re departing ahead of Winston’s merger with a U.K. firm, Taylor Wessing, which is expected to become effective next month.

Other notable moves:

  • Dechertrecruitedthree restructuring partners—Marcus Helt,Debbie Green, andJack Haake—fromMcDermottin Dallas.
  • CooleyhiredMona Dajanito co-chair its new infrastructure, energy, and real estate practice group (alongsideMichelle Schulman, an 18-year Cooley veteran). Dajani came over from the New York office ofBaker Botts.

This brings to a close another edition of Judicial Notice. Sorry for the delay, thanks as always for your readership and support, and I hope you have an excellent week.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

items.title: Judicial Notice (05.10.26): You Chose... Poorly
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Chambers-ranked and Harvard-educated, Jeff is the trusted closer for high-stakes disputes. His battle-tested system of managed communications empowers clients to make clear, strong decisions in a calm, informed environment. It makes the right resolution obvious. If settlement seems impossible, call him.

I hope all of the moms among you had a wonderful Mother’s Day. Our family had a lovely time celebrating with my mother and mother-in-law over brunch yesterday.

The highlight of last week for me was an event on Wednesday morning, a breakfast panel that I moderated about Biglaw firms moving into plaintiff-side and contingency litigation. Hosted by Burford Capital (a longtime sponsor of OJ), it featured two leading litigators, Eva Cole of Winston & Strawn and Avi Weitzman of Paul Hastings, and Burford’s own Evan Meyerson. The discussion was excellent, as I knew it would be—and the attendance was robust, which was a relief. (Whenever I’m involved with a live event, I never believe people will actually show up, until they do.)

In a prior edition of Judicial Notice, I alluded to some minor health issues. Thanks to those of you who reached out to express concern—but fear not, they’re quite minor. And they’re being addressed: I found an endodontist to deal with my root canal retreatment, as well as a physical therapist to help me with my Achilles tendonitis.

Now, on to the news. As usual when I publish Judicial Notice on Monday morning, this roundup covers news through Sunday, May 10; subsequent developments will appear in the next edition of JN.

Lawyer of the Week: Neal Katyal.

Since Wednesday night, everyone has been talking about acclaimed Supreme Court advocate Neal Katyal, a former acting U.S. solicitor general and current partner at Milbank. Why? Because of a tweet and subsequent TED Talk, discussing how he harnessed the power of Harvey to win the Supreme Court tariffs case, which were poorly received—for reasons I covered in my story from yesterday.

Despite my overall admiration for Katyal, I opined that his tweet and TED Talk were ill-advised, and it appears I’m not alone. In a reader poll, around 70% of you expressed a “very negative” or “negative” view of his tweet and TED Talk, with only 15% on the positive side. But if you haven’t done so already, check out my post and read the substantive, thoughtful statement that Katyal shared with me, explaining the message he wanted to convey in his tweet and talk. What he was trying to communicate was entirely sensible, even wise; the execution just left something to be desired.

Other lawyers in the news:

  • In remarks made last month at a private club in Washington, D.C., former special counsel

    Jack Smithcriticizedthe U.S. Department of Justice under Donald Trump, alleging that the DOJ had been “corrupted” by Trump loyalists.

  • After he argues in defense of the Trump administration’s executive orders against four Biglaw firms, before the D.C. Circuit this coming Thursday, Deputy Associate Attorney General

    Abhishek Kambliwilldepart from the DOJ(for an unknown position in the private sector).

  • Reflecting the Justice Department’s challenges in attracting and retaining talent, Assistant Attorney General

    Brett Shumateannouncedretention bonusesfor lawyers willing to stick it out at the Civil Division—as well as $25,000 signing bonuses for lawyers willing to join certain (especially controversial) sections of the Civil Division, such as ones focused on investigating youth transgender treatments and advancing the Trump administration’s immigration agenda.

  • U.S. Attorney

    Jeanine Pirrois also paying outretention bonuses, in the range of roughly $5,000 to $7,000, to federal prosecutors in D.C.
  • Kevin Bolan, who leads the Civil Division in the U.S. Attorney’s Office for Rhode Island, is beinginvestigatedby a special counsel, ProfessorNiki Kuckes. Bolan reportedly failed to inform JudgeMelissa DuBosethat an undocumented immigrant was wanted for homicide in the Dominican Republic—and after Judge DuBose ordered his release, the Trump administration issued apress releasecriticizing Judge DuBose, a Biden appointee, for ordering the release of a “criminal illegal alien… with an international warrant for homicide.”

  • If you’re fascinated by the improbable career trajectory of

    George Conway—theWachtell Liptonpartner turned Trump antagonist turned Democratic candidate for Congress—check out Timothy Bella’s profile of Conway forThe Washington Post(gift link).

  • The Georgia Supreme Court

    sanctionedassistant district attorneyDeborah Leslie, previously mentioned in these pages for an AI-related snafu, by suspending her privilege to practice before that court for six months.

  • Congratulations to

    Robert Boxie III, a Harvard Law School graduate andformer associateatVinson & Elkins, who this summer will become the youngest bishop in the United States. Pope Leo announced Boxie’s promotion last Friday, as reported byThe Washington Post(gift link). From Biglaw associate toYour Excellency—pretty excellent!

In memoriam: Abraham Foxman—who graduated from NYU Law and used his legal training to fight antisemitism, as a lawyer and then the CEO of the Anti-Defamation League—passed away at 86. May he rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Judge James Ho.

Last Wednesday, at a Federalist Society event in Los Angeles, Judge James Ho (5th Cir.) called out some of his fellow judges. Or as former Ho clerk Eric Wessan put it (in sharing a video of his former boss), Ho criticized “conservative judges for ‘punching right’ but not left,” while “call[ing] out his colleagues for cowardice in the face of unpopular decisions.” For a write-up of his comments, see Bloomberg Law.

After reminding the audience of his boycott against hiring clerks from Yale Law School—after free-speech problems at YLS, and the administration’s unacceptable response thereto—and complaining that no judges joined him in that boycott (other than Judge Lisa Branch of the Eleventh Circuit), Judge Ho complained about what he described as a “double standard[]”:

Just last year, when the Heritage Foundation was charged with antisemitism, a number of judges made clear that they would refuse to associate with the Heritage Foundation. And they specifically boycotted an event that would have featured the Heritage Foundation’s work. There was even a whole panel of judges to talk about these issues during the most recent Federalist Society convention.

So just to review the bidding: It’s okay to boycott Heritage. But you can’t boycott woke law schools. Let’s just be very honest about what’s going on here. Let’s be candid about the double standards that plague the judiciary. It’s okay to boycott Heritage, because you’ll never be punished for attacking conservatives. It’s okay to boycott Heritage, because it’s okay to virtue signal to cultural elites. It’s okay to boycott Heritage, because judges who punch left are excoriated—but judges who punch right are celebrated.

For the full text of Judge Ho’s remarks, as well as supportive commentary, see this Volokh Conspiracy post by Professor Josh Blackman. For criticism of Judge Ho’s speech—and it really was a speech, even though he was nominally a moderator—see this Dorf on Law post by Professor Eric Segall.

I do wonder: will Judge Ho announce a boycott against hiring clerks from UCLA Law, after a recent disruptive protest of an appearance by James Percival, general counsel of the Department of Homeland Security? And at what point will Judge Ho reconsider his boycott against Yale Law?

The disruptive protest of Kristen Waggoner at YLS took place in March 2022, more than four years ago. In the intervening period, YLS introduced a number of reforms to protect free speech. To improve intellectual diversity, it hired two right-of-center faculty members, Professors Garrett West and Keith Whittington. And Heather Gerken, the dean of YLS during “The Troubles,” left last year for the Ford Foundation.

It seems to me that the administration of UCLA Law—whose response to Percival-gate was problematic on several levels, for reasons outlined in a letter sent to Dean Michael Waterstone by Jessie Appleby of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE)—is a bigger offender against free-speech principles than the (new) administration of Yale Law, now led by Dean Cristina Rodríguez (who has not, as far as I know, done anything problematic on speech issues).

So although I personally object to judicial boycotts—both the YLS boycott specifically, as well as the notion of judges going around boycotting X, Y, or Z (which strikes me as outside the judicial role)—if you’re going to do a boycott, do it right, Judge Ho. Take YLS off the list, at least on a “probationary” basis, and replace it with UCLA.

Could it be that YLS, because it’s a higher-ranked school than UCLA, makes for better headlines and more media attention? If that’s the issue, allow me to point out that Yale Law is no longer the #1 law school—so perhaps it can have the “consolation prize” of having its graduates eligible to clerk for Judge Ho. ;-)

By the way, Judge Ho suggests that judges who silently decline to hire clerks from certain schools, such as lower-ranked schools, are “boycotting” those schools. Personally speaking, I don’t consider that much of a boycott, which Black’s Law Dictionary defines as “an action designed to achieve the social or economic isolation of an adversary, especially by the concerted refusal to do business with it” (emphasis added). It seems to me that speaking out publicly against said adversary, by encouraging or pressuring others to join you in a concerted refusal to do business, is an important part of a boycott. (For example, I might be a gold-star gay, but I do not consider myself to be engaged in a “boycott”—or “girlcott,” as the case may be.)

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

  • Picking up on the theme of

    when judges stop being politeand start getting real, JusticesSamuel AlitoandKetanji Brown Jacksontraded barbs in a quick coda toLouisiana v. Callais(or actually, as a technical matter,). Last Monday, as Amy Howe reported atCallais v. LouisianaSCOTUSblog, the Supreme Court granted a “granted a request to immediately finalize its opinion in [Callais], in which it struck down that state’s congressional map, to allow Louisiana to draw a new map in time for the 2026 elections.” Justice Jackson dissented, claiming that her colleagues’ decision “to issue the judgment forthwith”—i.e., to give immediate effect to its ruling—represented “principles giv[ing] way to power.” Justice Alito, joined by JusticesClarence ThomasandNeil Gorsuch, criticized the dissent’s arguments as “baseless and insulting”—and said that instead of the majority going hog wild, it is “the dissent’s rhetoric that lacks restraint.” For more on theCallaisdust-up, read this editorial inThe Wall Street Journal(gift link), titled “Justice Alito’s Intriguing Footnote,” or listen to the latest episode ofAdvisory Opinions. And for a fascinating historical look at when justices have turned on each other, see thisSCOTUSblog postby Nora Collins.

  • In happier news about the justices, congratulations to Justice

    Brett Kavanaugh, who placed fifth among federal judges in last Wednesday’s Capital Challenge. He completed the three-mile road race in an entirely respectable 26:23, as reported byBloomberg Law(via Howard Bashman’sHow Appealing).

  • Justice Kavanaugh wasn’t happy with his time, telling Bloomberg Law that he should have trained more—and next Term, he might be able to spend less time in robes and more time in running shorts. At last week’s Third Circuit Judicial Conference, Chief Justice

    John Robertssaid that the Court’s current oral argument format has “blown up”—which he apparently meant not as a compliment, but as what happens when you go off Ozempic—and he added that the justices might revisit it this summer (via Lawrence Hurley onTwitter).

  • Why is a California judge in the pages of

    The New York Post? In a barnburner of abrief, the U.S. Attorney’s Office for Los Angeles shines the spotlight on JudgeFernando Olguin(C.D. Cal.), describing his history of dismissing serious criminal charges against undocumented immigrants. The government contends that Judge Olguin is engaged in an improper effort “to push back against ICE’s detention of aliens charged with federal crimes, if not against ICE itself.”

  • In other news of judges upsetting conservatives, Magistrate Judge

    Zia Faruqui(D.D.C.)apologizedat a hearing last Monday to Cole Tomas Allen, the California man accused of attempting to assassinate Trump at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, for the treatment Allen received inside a D.C. jail. Specifically, Judge Faruqui objected to Allen being placed on suicide watch, after hetold FBI agentsthat he didn’t expect to survive the attempted assassination.

  • Turning to the state courts, Justice

    Diana Hagenresignedfrom the Utah Supreme Court, after her ex-husband accused her of sending “inappropriate” text messages to an attorney who helped challenge a Republican-friendly map in her court. (Justice Hagen had previously recused herself from cases involving the lawyer in question,David Reymann.)

Job of the Week: a general-counsel role at a high-growth manufacturing company.

Lateral Link is working with a high-growth global manufacturing company to identify its first U.S.-based general counsel. Based out of Greenville, South Carolina, this is a hands-on, high-impact opportunity to shape legal strategy, infrastructure, and risk-management frameworks from the ground up, while working directly with executive leadership. The GC will oversee all U.S. legal matters, with a particular focus on dispute management, risk mitigation, and supporting complex commercial and operational decisions. The ideal candidate will have 10–20+ years of practice experience, a strong litigation background, and GC- or deputy GC-level experience in a fast-moving business environment. Interested candidates should contact Marion Wilson at mwilson@laterallink.com and Brittany Zoll at bzoll@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: Scott v.

Last month, Virginia voters approved a Democratic-backed ballot measure that would have reconfigured the state’s congressional map. The new map would have flipped as many as four seats in the U.S. House of Representatives from Republican to Democratic control in the upcoming midterm elections, moving Virginia’s congressional delegation from a 6-5 to 10-1 split in favor of Democrats.

But last Friday, in Scott v. McDougle, the Virginia Supreme Court held that the procedure used to advance this proposal violated the Virginia Constitution. So the court invalidated the referendum vote and nullified the electoral map, dealing what both

The vote was 4-3, with Justice Arthur Kelsey writing the majority opinion and Chief Justice Cleo Powell penning the dissent. Here’s the reasoning, via Reuters:

Under Virginia state law, two consecutive legislatures—with a ​state election in between—must approve a proposed constitutional ⁠amendment before it can be put to a vote.

The Democratic legislative majority approved the amendment in October, days before the November state election. Democrats, who gained additional legislative seats in that vote, then passed the amendment for a second time in January and scheduled the referendum for April.

Republicans filed multiple lawsuits, claiming in part that there was no intervening election ​since early voting had already started when the amendment was first passed. In Friday’s decision, the Virginia Supreme Court agreed.

Lawyers for Virginia said in a filing to the Virginia Supreme Court that they plan to seek emergency relief from the U.S. Supreme Court. But because the Virginia Supreme Court’s ruling turned on state rather than federal law, legal experts from across the ideological spectrum, from Ed Whelan to Richard Hasen, cast doubt on Old Dominion’s prospects for success. As Rick Hasen told The Times, the state’s likely argument on appeal—a claim that the Virginia high court improperly interfered with the state legislature’s power over elections—is “a long shot.”

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

  • . In an opinion by JudgeCantero v. Bank of America, N.AMichael Park, a divided panel of the Second Circuit held that New York’s interest-on-escrow requirement is preempted by federal banking law. JudgeMyrna Pérezdissented.
  • . A defendant who was convicted of three counts of unlawful possession of an unregistered silencer challenged his convictions under the Second Amendment—and in an opinion by JudgeUnited States v. SpeedMarvin Quattlebaum, the Fourth Circuit shot down his arguments. In concurrences, JudgeJ. Harvie Wilkinsonargued that “silencers are categorically unprotected by the Second Amendment,” while JudgeJulius Richardsoncontended that his court’s approach to evaluating Second Amendment claims, even if binding on theSpeedpanel, can’t be squared with the Supreme Court’sBrahimiframework.
  • . From John Ross ofUnited States v. BlockerShort Circuit: “After some musings on narrower ways to resolve whether there was a Fourth Amendment violation when Dropbox shared information about a user’s child porn with a quasi-governmental entity, this breezy Seventh Circuit opinion [by JudgeFrank Easterbrook] entrenches a circuit split by holding that the fine print in all the online terms of service you never read means you’ve consented to government searches of your electronic files. Some folks [including Fourth Amendment guruOrin Kerr] are skeptical.”
  • . In an opinion by JudgesBurlap and Barrel, Inc. v. TrumpMark BarnettandClaire Kelly, the U.S. Court of International Trade rejected the Trump administration’s attempt to reimpose broad global tariffs under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, the statute that Trump turned to after the Supreme Court smacked down his effort to impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). JudgeTimothy Stanceudissented.

Litigation of the Week: United States v. Nourafchan .

Last Wednesday, federal prosecutors unsealed two indictments, both filed in the District of Massachusetts, charging 30 individuals with participating in a massive insider-trading scheme that ran for roughly a decade, from 2014 to 2024. The indictments, in United States v. Nourafchan and

The plot described in the indictments involved garden-variety insider trading. Lawyers at top corporate law firms shared material, nonpublic information, concerning an estimated 30 future M&A deals, with co-conspirators. The co-conspirators then traded on it, earning tens of millions of dollars, and paid kickbacks to the attorneys.

What’s striking about the scheme is its sheer scope. It involved lawyers who collectively have worked at some of the most prestigious and profitable law firms in the world, including DLA Piper, Goodwin Procter, Latham & Watkins, Sidley Austin, Wachtell Lipton, Weil Gotshal, and Willkie Farr. (There are no allegations of wrongdoing against any of the firms; to the contrary, they’re described in the indictments as victims, and they have all been cooperating with law enforcement.)

The world of Biglaw is surprisingly small. So I wouldn’t be surprised if some of the individuals whose names have appeared in the indictments or surfaced in the news reporting—as defendants, cooperators, or unindicted co-conspirators—are known to my readers. They include the following:

  • Nicolo Nourafchan. A 2011 graduate of Yale Law School who went on to work at Sidley, Latham, and Goodwin, Nourafchan was an alleged ringleader of the scheme, recruiting other attorneys to join it.
  • Gabriel Gershowitz. A friend of Nourafchan from their time together as undergraduates at George Washington University, he graduated from Columbia Law and worked at Weil, DLA, and Willkie. After pleading guilty in February 2025, he startedcooperatingwith prosecutors (who as of now are recommending a two-year prison sentence for him).
  • Avi Sutton. An alumnus of Yale Law, where he was friends with Nourafchan, and a former Wachtell associate, Sutton isn’t named in the indictments. But various outlets, including Law360 and Roll on Friday, report that he’s the unindicted co-conspirator referred to as “CC-2.” He left Wachtell to work at the Liontree investment bank, where he was most recently the chief operating officer and general counsel (but as of Friday morning, he was gone from its website).

Beyond Biglaw, another lawyer, Robert Yadgarov, was also allegedly involved. A friend of Nourafchan and Gershowitz from their GW years, he was a Touro Law alum with his own personal-injury practice. He wasn’t privy to M&A info himself, but he allegedly assisted Nourafchan in recruiting.

My overall reaction—which may apply to certain other stories in this edition of JN—is admittedly obvious and unoriginal: how can such smart people do such dumb things? When you graduate from places like Yale and Columbia Law and then land that first job in Biglaw, you’re arguably set for life. You might switch firms, go in-house, or even become a legal blogger—I understand, more than most, that legal careers can take surprising turns—but big-picture, you should be able to spend the rest of your days doing reasonably interesting work, for decent (or more than decent) pay, and not in an orange jumpsuit (unless it’s by Hermès). Why risk throwing all that away?

Although the traders made tens of millions, it appears that the lawyers who provided the tips made considerably less. The Fejal indictment alleges that Yadgarov received $6.3 million from the traders, but it didn’t all go to him; he split it with Nourafchan, Sutton, and at least one other source. Round it up to $8 million—or heck, double it to $12 million—and divide by four, yielding $3 million. Setting aside all the legal and ethical problems and conducting a pure risk-benefit analysis, is it really worth it to throw away your career—and perhaps your life—for $3 million?

I wouldn’t do it for any price—not because I’m a paragon of virtue (although I like to think I’m reasonably ethical), but because I’m extremely anxious, and I couldn’t live for years fearing that I might get arrested any day. But even if I were a cold-blooded mercenary, you’d have to add another zero to the end of that $3 million for me to even consider such conduct, even setting aside the fact that it’s illegal and wrong (or at least illegal—I acknowledge there’s a debate about whether it should be illegal).

In other litigation news:

  • What’s the latest in the legal battle between Louisiana and two manufacturers of the abortion drug mifepristone? It’s complicated, but the bottom line is that as of now, because of an administrative stay issued last Monday by Justice

    Samuel Alito, mifepristone remains widely available nationwide. That stayexpiresat 5 p.m. today, however—so stay tuned.

  • I must confess that I wasn’t terribly interested in the long-running, acrimonious legal battle between Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni. Fortunately, I won’t have to devote any more brain cells to this topic: with a May 18 trial date fast approaching, they

    settledthe remaining claims (except forone claimby Lively, relating to what she alleges was a retaliatory defamation lawsuit filed by Baldoni).

  • Speaking of settlements, Apple agreed to pay

    $250 millionto resolve class-action litigation alleging that it made misrepresentations to consumers about the capabilities of its AI system, Apple Intelligence.

  • If your friends in finance have been buzzing about a salacious sexual-assault and harassment lawsuit that banker Chirayu Rana filed against his former employer, JPMorgan Chase, and a former colleague, here’s a helpful overview of the case from

    The Wall Street Journal(gift link).

  • Speaking of employment-related litigation against iconic companies, the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission is

    suingThe New York Times, alleging that its hiring and promotion practices discriminate against white male workers.

Deal of the Week: the $3.9 billion sale of the San Diego Padres to a buyer’s group led by José Feliciano and Kwanza Jones.

Back in November, I broke the news of Davis Polk & Wardwell hiring Jon Oram, a top dealmaker in the world of sports law, from Proskauer. And Oram appears to be plenty busy at DPW: he’s leading a team advising the San Diego Padres and the family of its late owner, Peter Seidler, on the team’s $3.9 billion sale to a group of buyers led by private-equity billionaire José Feliciano and his wife Kwanza Jones. The buyers are represented by a Wachtell Lipton team led by Ed Herlihy and Eric Feinstein.

Law Firm of the Week: Paul Weiss.

Over the past year, Paul Weiss has lost a number of heavy hitters in litigation—including Karen Dunn, Bill Isaacson, and Jeannie Rhee, who left to launch Dunn Isaacson Rhee, and Kannon Shanmugam, who moved to Davis Polk. After rainmaking partners depart from a firm, there’s often less work for the associates they leave behind—which might explain why Paul Weiss parted ways with an unknown number of litigation associates, as reported by Above the Law and Law.com.

The firm characterized the reductions as “performance-based decisions based on the review process we conduct every year.” But on Reddit, purported Paul Weiss posters dispute that characterization, describing the cuts as “bogus performance-based send-offs—bogus because most affected folks are getting negative feedback in the annual review that none of the individual teams are corroborating.”

Meanwhile, Paul Weiss continues to expand its partner ranks. In its three-month-old Houston office, it added three new partners: tax partner James Cole (from Latham & Watkins), M&A partner Aisha Lavinier (from Kirkland & Ellis), and litigation partner Andrea Smith (from Gibson Dunn). Hiring litigators like Smith and Adam Hoeflich, who joined last month from Bartlit Beck, shows that PW isn’t abandoning litigation. But it’s not unreasonable to wonder whether the talent it has added is sufficient to compensate for the talent it has lost.

In other firm news:

  • My overall sense is that Biglaw litigation is treading water, while boutiques are bustling. Take

    Hecker Fink, just profiled by Tatyana Monnay forBloomberg Law—which now boasts 100 lawyers, including partners who came over from firms likeCravathandArnold & Porter.

  • Like Paul Weiss,

    McDermott Will & Schultealsoreduced its associate ranks—but unlike Paul Weiss, McDermott didn’t claim that the dozen or so associates it let go were fired for poor performance. Instead, a spokesperson explained that the firm “made the difficult decision to separate from a small number of associates across the firm, as we continue to align with shifting client needs”—and expressed gratitude to the departing associates, stating that “their work has made a meaningful impact on our clients and the firm.”

Move of the Week: Alston & Bird hiring IP trial lawyers Jennifer Golinveaux, Michael Elkin, and Sean Anderson from Winston & Strawn.

Continuing to expand in IP litigation, Alston & Bird hired a trio of IP trial lawyers from Winston & Strawn: Michael Elkin and Sean Anderson, in New York, and Jennifer Golinveaux, in San Francisco. Elkin previously served as vice-chair of Winston and managing partner of the New York office, while Golinveaux had been global co-chair of Winston’s IP practice. They’re departing ahead of Winston’s merger with a U.K. firm, Taylor Wessing, which is expected to become effective next month.

Other notable moves:

  • Dechertrecruitedthree restructuring partners—Marcus Helt,Debbie Green, andJack Haake—fromMcDermottin Dallas.
  • CooleyhiredMona Dajanito co-chair its new infrastructure, energy, and real estate practice group (alongsideMichelle Schulman, an 18-year Cooley veteran). Dajani came over from the New York office ofBaker Botts.

This brings to a close another edition of Judicial Notice. Sorry for the delay, thanks as always for your readership and support, and I hope you have an excellent week.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!
