items.title: Judicial Notice (05.03.26): Spirited Away
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

With a presence in over a dozen cities across the United States and Asia, Lateral Link boasts an expert recruiting team of former practicing attorneys dedicated to sourcing top-tier legal talent for a diverse clientele, including major international law firms and Fortune 500 companies. To learn more about Lateral Link, please visit our website.

Over the past few weeks—thanks to popular stories like my deep dives into the most prestigious law firms and the most profitable firms, as well as my discussion of a recent AI fail by a top firm—I’ve picked up many free subscribers here at Original Jurisdiction. If you’re a new OJ subscriber, welcome!

What you’re now reading is Judicial Notice, my handy, time-saving summary of the past week in legal news. JN allows paid subscribers to read (or skim) a single post—sometimes a long one, hence the reference to skimming—and get caught up on what’s going on in the law and legal profession. Busy lawyers find it very useful (and occasionally entertaining, depending on the news cycle).

I typically begin JN with a brief personal update, but this week I don’t have much to report (other than a series of annoying health issues, of interest to no one except my loving physician parents). I’ll just say a quick thanks to chief legal officer Jonathan Leiken and his colleagues in the legal department at Danaher, who invited me and Professor Kevin Ashley to discuss how AI is transforming the legal profession. I love hearing about how innovative corporate legal departments are harnessing the power of AI (see also my recent podcast interview with Salesforce CLO Sabastian Niles).

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: James Comey.

Last year, the Trump administration obtained an indictment against former FBI director (and Trump adversary) James Comey, charging him with false-statement and obstruction offenses. But that case was dismissed by Judge Cameron McGowan Currie (D.S.C.), who ruled that Interim U.S. Attorney Lindsey Halligan (E.D. Va.), the prosecutor who single-handedly secured the indictment, wasn’t validly appointed.

Last Tuesday, the Trump administration landed a new indictment against Comey, this time charging him with making a threat against the president and transmitting a threat across state lines. Comey allegedly did so by “publicly post[ing] a photograph [on Instagram] which depicted seashells arranged in a pattern making out ‘86 47,’ which a reasonable recipient who is familiar with the circumstances would interpret as a serious expression of an intent to do harm to the President.” As explained by The New York Times (gift link), “86” is a slang term “often used to mean dismiss or remove.”

As eight experts told The Washington Post (gift link), the new Comey indictment appears to be on shaky legal ground—especially in light of the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision in Elonis v. United States (2015), which held, per The Post, “that prosecutors seeking to convict someone of sending a dangerous message must prove the person intended to make a violent threat—or at least knew there was a substantial chance it would be viewed as threatening.” Here, according to The Times, "[a]fter an uproar ensued over the post, Mr. Comey deleted it, saying that he did not know that it could be seen as having a violent connotation and that he opposed violence of any kind.”

Elonis isn’t the only precedent that’s problematic for this prosecution. As David French pointed out on Advisory Opinions, there’s also Watts v. United States (1969), in which the Court ordered the acquittal of a defendant who said this about then-president Lyndon B. Johnson: “I have already received my draft classification as 1-A, and I have got to report for my physical this Monday coming. I am not going. If they ever make me carry a rifle, the first man I want to get in my sights is L.B.J.”

Trump supporters might dismiss The Post and David French as less than objective, given their past criticisms of the president. But even Professor Jonathan Turley, who frequently defends Trump, wrote a piece for Fox News arguing that “this indictment is facially unconstitutional, absent some unknown new facts.”

Raising that possibility, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche told NBC News that the case rests not just on the Instagram post, but on “a body of evidence that [prosecutors] collected over the series of about 11 months.” And the indictment does indicate that “the unredacted version of this document has been filed under seal.”

So it’s theoretically possible that there’s more here. But based on the cases brought so far against Comey and other Trump foes, there’s reason to be skeptical. Indeed, there’s reason to think that Trump DOJ officials—including Blanche, who’d like to go from acting AG to presidentially nominated, Senate-confirmed AG—feel a certain amount of pressure “to execute [Trump’s] increasingly extreme demands without much pushback,” in the words of The Times (gift link).

If the seashells indictment ends up getting dismissed—or 86’ed, if you prefer—that doesn’t spell clear sailing for Jim Comey. The DOJ is also investigating Comey for possibly leaking classified information, according to Bloomberg Law. When it comes to going after Trump enemies, the motto of the administration seems to be, “If at first you don’t succeed, try—and try, and try, and try—again.”

Other lawyers in the news:

  • The seashells indictment was signed by

    W. Ellis Boyle, currently serving as Interim U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of North Carolina—and nominated to serve as the permanent U.S. Attorney. Although he’s a Trump loyalist, Boyle at least hassomerelevant experience, having previously served as an assistant U.S. attorney (on the civil side). Other Trump U.S. attorney picks have been more problematic, as discussed by Jeffrey Toobin inThe Times(gift link).

  • Is

    William “Bill” Burck, the global co-managing partner ofQuinn Emanuel, back in the good graces of the Trump family? As reported byBloomberg Law, Burck—who was fired last year by the Trump Organization as an outside ethics counsel, after he started defending Harvard in litigation against the administration—is now representing World Liberty Financial, a crypto venture co-founded and co-owned by Trump family members (among other investors).

  • As I

    mentionedlast week, trial inMusk v. Altman—Elon Musk’s lawsuit against fellow billionaire Sam Altman, OpenAI, and Microsoft,challengingOpenAI’s conversion to a for-profit company—is now underway, before JudgeYvonne Gonzalez Rogers(N.D. Cal.). I expect to have more to say about this trial, which should go on for a few weeks; for now, I’ll just highlight the breakout star of the proceedings so far:William Savitt, co-chair of the executive committee and co-chair of litigation atWachtell Lipton. For more on Bill Savitt and hiscontentiouscross-examination of Musk—with whom Savitt has a history, having successfully sued the gazillionaire to follow through on his purchase of Twitter (now X)—see thisBusiness Insider profileby Jacob Shamsian.

Judge of the Week: Justice Elena Kagan.

One of the biggest legal news stories of last week was the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision in Louisiana v. Callais, a major ruling concerning the interaction between Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, the Fourteenth Amendment, and the Fifteenth Amendment. It’s an incredibly controversial and consequential case—and I discuss it in more detail below, as Litigation of the Week.

For now, I’d like to highlight Justice Elena Kagan, who wrote the liberals’ dissent in this 6-3 case, taking on the majority opinion of Justice Samuel Alito (who was Judge of the Week quite recently, so I’m not featuring him again today). Because Justice Kagan is the most moderate and least confrontational of the liberal justices, she sometimes takes flak from the left. But her Callais dissent got her major props, in progressive circles and beyond—as did her reading it from the bench (for 14 minutes), the first oral dissent of the Term (as noted by Mark Walsh of SCOTUSblog).

As Elie Mystal wrote in The Nation, “Justice Elena Kagan brings clarity to what Alito tries to hide.” Professor Richard Hasen, at Election Law Blog, commended her “strenuous” dissent for getting the analysis “exactly right.” At The Bulwark, Professor Kim Wehle praised the justice’s “fierce, eloquent 48-page dissent” (which was, as noted by Adam Feldman over at Legalytics, “52% longer than the majority”).

Here’s the dissent’s eminently quotable conclusion (and yes, many commentators noted the absence of the traditional “respectfully”):

[T]he Court’s gutting of Section 2 puts [the] achievement [of the Voting Rights Act] in peril. I dissent because Congress elected otherwise. I dissent because the Court betrays its duty to faithfully implement the great statute Congress wrote. I dissent because the Court’s decision will set back the foundational right Congress granted of racial equality in electoral opportunity. I dissent.

Whether you agree or disagree with Justice Kagan on the merits, you can’t deny her writing chops. There’s a reason legal writing guru Ross Guberman once told me—half-jokingly, but only half—that BriefCatch, his powerful legal writing and editing tool, was “sort of normed” on Justice Kagan (who “consistently earns the highest BriefCatch writing-quality scores of any living Justice,” per Guberman).

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

  • Justice Kagan and her two fellow liberal justices didn’t attend last Tuesday’s White House state dinner honoring King Charles. It’s not clear whether they were invited—but their six conservative colleagues were, since they all

    showed up.

  • Last week’s featured jurist, Judge

    Alan Albright(W.D. Tex.), recently announced his plan to retire this coming August. He said he wanted to return to litigating—but one can’t help wondering whether he also wanted to escape his massive backlog of cases. As reported by Ryan Autullo ofBloomberg Law, Judge Albright had 446 undecided motions on his latest “six-month list,” the list of civil motions ripe for a decision for six months or longer but not yet resolved. Many district judges I know aim for a six-month list of zero, or maybe a number in the low single digits—so 446 is staggering. (For perspective, JudgeGeorge Daniels(S.D.N.Y) wound up on the front page ofThe Times(gift link) back in 2004 because he had, as reported by Ben Weiser, 289 motions on his six-month list.)

In memoriam: Judge James Lawrence King—who served on the federal bench for some 55 years, including a stint as chief judge for the Southern District of Florida—passed away at 98. May he rest in peace.

Job of the Week: an opportunity at a leading legal recruiter.

Lateral Link seeks a high-caliber recruiter for a leadership-capable role on its Am Law–level platform. This is an opportunity to grow within a flat, merit-focused organization that rewards ambition and fosters lifelong careers in legal recruiting. Lateral Link partners with firms and corporate legal teams nationwide, handling a wide range of senior and niche placements. Qualified candidates will have a J.D. from a top school or a proven track record at a premier search firm, along with the integrity and drive to advance eight core values: Trust, Respect, Integrity, Accountability, Cooperation, Resourcefulness, Diversity, and Compassion. Compensation is market-leading: 50–70% ramped commissions, year-end discretionary bonuses up to 15%, and principal-level bonuses, with total earnings frequently above peers. To learn more, please contact Mike Allen at mallen@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: Louisiana v. Callais .

Last Wednesday, as mentioned above, the Supreme Court issued its eagerly anticipated ruling in Louisiana v. Callais. For media coverage—and there was a ton, as you’d expect for a ruling of this importance—see Howard Bashman’s How Appealing.

In an opinion by Justice Alito, the six conservative justices struck down Louisiana’s voting map as an unconstitutional racial gerrymander. The map had been redrawn to include an additional majority-Black district because of a 2022 federal-court ruling holding that the earlier map, which had only one majority-Black district, likely violated Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act. But according to Justice Alito, “[t]he State’s attempt to satisfy the [2022] ruling, although understandable, was an unconstitutional racial gerrymander.” As for future claims brought under the Voting Rights Act, according to Callais, Section 2 “imposes liability only when the evidence supports a strong inference that the State intentionally drew its districts to afford minority voters less opportunity because of their race” (emphasis added).

Justice Kagan, joined by Justices Sonia Sotomayor and Ketanji Brown Jackson, dissented. According to the dissent, under the majority’s view, “a State can, without legal consequence, systematically dilute minority citizens’ voting power…. Without a basis in Section 2’s text or the Constitution, the majority formulates new proof requirements for plaintiffs alleging vote dilution.” In the dissenters’ view, Callais “renders Section 2 all but a dead letter.”

Justice Clarence Thomas, joined by Justice Neil Gorsuch, joined Justice Alito’s opinion in full—but would have gone further. In Justice Thomas’s view, Section 2 of the VRA “does not regulate districting at all.” Why? The statutory text prevents states “from imposing or applying a ‘voting qualification,’ ‘prerequisite to voting,’ or ‘standard, practice, or procedure,’ in a manner that results in a denial or abridgement of the right to vote based on race.” Drawing election maps “does not fall within any of those three categories,” in Thomas’s view—case closed.

Reactions to Callais ran the gamut. Professor Rick Hasen, writing at Slate, declared that the majority “gutt[ed] what remained of [VRA] protections for minority voters,” and “will go down in history as one of the most pernicious and damaging Supreme Court decisions of the past century.” Meanwhile, the editorial board of the WSJ (gift link) praised Callais as “a large step toward ending the partisan abuse of race to carve up Congressional districts in a way that violates the Constitution.”

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

  • . Because it came down on the same day asFirst Choice Women’s Resource Centers, Inc. v. DavenportCallais,First Choicereceived very little attention. Writing for a unanimous Court, Justice Gorsuch held that First Choice—”a religious nonprofit organization that has provided counseling and resources to pregnant women in New Jersey,” generally urging them not to have abortions—could go straight to federal court to challenge a subpoena from the New Jersey Attorney General’s Office (seeking donor information).
  • . In a one-paragraph, summary disposition, the justicesAbbott v. LULACreverseda lower-court decision that would have blocked Texas’s plans for redrawing its congressional districts (in favor of Republicans). The justices had temporarily given Texas’s new map the green light back in December; this latest ruling put the new map in place indefinitely. The three liberal justices dissented.
  • . From John Ross ofCarroll v. TrumpShort Circuit: “the Second Circuit willnotreconsider its panel opinion affirming E. Jean Carroll’s $83.3 million defamation award against Trump for statements he made while president. Three dissenting judges [JudgesSteven Menashi,Michael Park, andDebra Ann Livingston] would have granted rehearing to consider presidential immunity arguments and to allow the United States to belatedly substitute itself in Trump’s shoes.”
  • . In an opinion by JudgeCunha v. FredenJoseph Bianco, the Second Circuitdepartedfrom the Fifth and Eighth Circuits and rejected the Trump administration’s claim that federal law “prohibits the setting of bond for all inadmissible noncitizens” (i.e., the government argues that “mandatory detention must continue regardless of how long removal proceedings take”).
  • . The Fifth Circuit voted against rehearing en banc in a challenge to the federal ban on machineguns. But five judges joined three opinions—by JudgesUnited States v. WilsonDon Willett,James Ho, andAndrew Oldham—expressing a willingness to reconsider the constitutionality of the ban, under the Commerce Clause and/or the Second Amendment (in this case or a more appropriate one).
  • The Rohingya are a minority in Myanmar that has been subjected to discrimination and violence for decades. In an opinion by JudgeJane Doe 1 v. Meta Platforms, Inc.Ryan Nelson, the Ninth Circuit affirmed the dismissal of a lawsuit filed by members of the Rohingya community, seeking to hold Meta liable for the proliferation of anti-Rohingya content on Facebook. The panel reached this result based on circuit precedent about Section 230 immunity—but Judges Nelson andMarsha Berzon, in concurrences, called that precedent into question.
  • andDickinson v. Trump. In orders authored by JudgesReach Community Development v. United States Department of Homeland SecurityKenneth LeeandEric Tung, the Ninth Circuit granted stays requested by the Trump administration of preliminary injunctions regulating various actions by ICE in Portland, Oregon. JudgeAna de Albadissented (in whole or in part).
  • . All I’ll say about this Ninth Circuit habeas case is, “Don’t be surprised if it gets summarily reversed by SCOTUS”—for the reasons given in JudgeHogan v. BeanDaniel Bress’s dissental, joined by eight of his colleagues.

Litigations of the Week: Danco Laboratories, LLC v. State of Louisiana and GenBioPro, Inc. v. State of Louisiana .

Louisiana, which has a near-total ban on abortion, specifically prohibits administering, prescribing, or selling a drug like mifepristone to perform abortions. Last year, Louisiana filed a legal challenge to a Biden-era FDA regulation allowing mifepristone to be prescribed online and dispensed through the mail, without any need for an in-person visit to a doctor. Responding to Louisiana’s lawsuit, the FDA, now under Trump, said it was conducting a comprehensive review of its mifepristone protocols—but in the meantime, the FDA opposed suspension of the regulation.

Judge David Joseph (W.D La.) agreed that Louisiana was likely to prevail in its challenge to the mifepristone regulation—but balancing the equities and the public interest, he declined to stay the regulation in the meantime. So Louisiana sought a stay pending appeal from the Fifth Circuit—and on Friday, in an opinion by Judge Stuart Kyle Duncan, the court granted the state its requested stay.

On Saturday, two manufacturers of mifepristone sought emergency relief from the U.S. Supreme Court, as reported by Amy Howe of SCOTUSblog. Specifically, the companies—Danco Laboratories, represented by Hogan Lovells, and GenBioPro, represented by Arnold & Porter—asked the Court to (1) vacate the Fifth Circuit’s stay pending appeal, i.e., reinstate the FDA’s regulation allowing mifepristone to be prescribed online and distributed through the mail; and (2) enter an immediate administrative stay, to preserve the (pre-Fifth Circuit) status quo in the meantime.

The significance of this litigation, whether you support Louisiana or the drug companies, can’t be denied. As Kelly Baden—vice president for public policy at the Guttmacher Institute, a research organization that supports abortion rights—said to The Times, “This decision represents the most sweeping threat to abortion since the overturning of Roe v. Wade.” Meanwhile, Marjorie Dannenfelser, president of Susan B. Anthony Pro-Life America, praised the Fifth Circuit’s ruling to The Times as “a huge victory for victims and survivors of Biden’s reckless mail-order abortion drug regime.”

What happens next? As Howe of SCOTUSblog explained, the drug companies’ applications will first go to Justice Alito, who handles emergency requests from the Fifth Circuit as its circuit justice, and he’ll probably ask Louisiana to respond before taking action. So just as the Court is entering its busiest period, with the justices (and their clerks) toiling away on landmark opinions in cases involving birthright citizenship and transgender athletes, it has a new issue on its emergency/interim/shadow docket—and not just any issue, but one of indisputable importance and impact.

In other litigation news:

  • In the latest legal battle between the Trump administration and blue states, the DOJ filed

    two lawsuitsagainst New Jersey, challenging state laws (1) providing financial aid to undocumented immigrants attending public colleges in the Garden State and (2) banning most law enforcement agents, including federal agents, from wearing masks.

  • OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma was officially

    sentencedon Tuesday by JudgeMadeline Cox Arleo(D.N.J.), under a plea agreement requiring it to pay billions in fines ​and penalties—after which Purdue concluded operations, exited bankruptcy, andre-emergedas a nonprofit dedicated to treating opioid addiction.

  • Speaking of resolving major mass-tort litigations, the Archdiocese of New York agreed to an

    $800 million settlementwith 1,300 survivors of sexual abuse with claims against the Catholic Church.

  • Speaking of settlements, the American Bar Association

    resolveda lawsuit brought by Edward Blum’s American Alliance for Equal Rights, by agreeing to provide scholarships previously reserved for “racial and ethnic minority students” on a “race/ethnicity-neutral” basis going forward.

  • Still on the subject of settlements, could the Trump DOJ end up settling… Donald Trump’s lawsuit against the federal government over the 2019 leak of his tax returns, in which he seeks $10 billion in damages? Judge

    Kathleen Williams(S.D. Fla.)orderedDOJ lawyers and Trump’s personal attorneys to address “whether the parties are sufficiently adverse to each other,” now that Trump himself controls the Department of Justice.

  • Reflecting Trump’s degree of control over the DOJ, on Monday the Justice Department filed a

    motionin the White House ballroom construction litigation, asking JudgeRichard Leon(D.D.C.) to dissolve his injunction blocking the construction—and channeling Trump’s “recognizable online voice,” perThe Times. Consider this excerpt: “Because it is DONALD J. TRUMP, a highly successful real estate developer, who has abilities that others don’t, especially those who assume the Office of President, this frivolous and meritless lawsuit was filed. Again, it’s called TRUMP DERANGEMENT SYNDROME.”

  • Oops, she (allegedly) did it again: Britney Spears was

    chargedwith a misdemeanor count of driving under the influence of ​alcohol or drugs. The pop star was previously hit with two driving-related misdemeanor charges back in 2007, arising out of an allegedhit-and-run incident(but one charge was dismissed by a judge, and a second was the subject of a mistrial).

Deal of the Week: the Trump administration’s (unsuccessful) effort to rescue Spirit Airlines.

Deals come, and deals go. One possible acquisition mentioned in these pages apparently won’t be happening: French spirits company Pernod Ricard dropped its pursuit of Jack Daniel’s maker Brown-Forman, after a failure to agree on terms. But other transactions have entered the pipeline, including a possible offer for eBay from GameStop (whose market cap of around $11 billion is less than a quarter of eBay’s).

Another deal that proved evanescent was a government-led rescue of Spirit Airlines, which filed for Chapter 11 last year—and had come up with a reorganization plan, before the war-induced spike in fuel prices threw a wrench into the works. The Trump administration tried to put together a $500 million bailout that would give the feds a 90% stake in Spirit (which the government would then try to sell for a profit after oil prices returned to earth). But the talks failed after creditors objected to how they’d be treated under the deal, per the WSJ (gift link). So on Saturday, Spirit Airlines shut down permanently, canceling all its flights. Spirit Airlines, RIP.

Law Firm of the Week: King & Spalding.

Besides litigation shops like Quinn Emanuel, Williams & Connolly, Susman Godfrey, Kellogg Hansen, and Boies Schiller, which Am Law 200 firms remain committed to lit? I’d mention—among others, this list of three is far from exhaustive—Gibson Dunn, Jones Day, and my latest Law Firm of the Week, King & Spalding.

For his latest Litigators of the Week, Ross Todd of Am Law Litigation Daily selected K&S partners Lazar Raynal, Craig Carpenito, and Mike Lombardo. They represented Skillz, the publicly traded maker of a skill-based gaming app, which accused a competitor, Papaya, of false advertising. Specifically, Skillz alleged that Papaya utilized bots as “players” in games that it falsely advertised as skill-based, in violation of the Lanham Act. After a two-week trial before Judge Denise Cote (S.D.N.Y.), a jury agreed with Skillz—and awarded $420 million in damages.

Skillz Platform Inc. v. Papaya Gaming Ltd. is a good example of a trend I recently wrote about: corporations, including public companies, becoming more comfortable with being plaintiffs. And King & Spalding is a good example of an elite law firm moving beyond traditional defense work to handle affirmative litigation, often on contingency (a subject I’ll be moderating a panel about on Wednesday morning, for Am Law 200 litigation partners who are interested in learning more).

As reflected in the latest Am Law 100 rankings, King & Spalding is #18 in revenue per lawyer and #12 in profits per equity partner. When you’re this high in the rankings, the competition is fierce. If K&S took Skillz v. Papaya on contingency—whether partial or full—it could be a nice boost for its 2026 financials.

Move of the Week: Sheppard adding 15 IP litigators from Perkins Coie.

Intellectual property litigators are flocking to Sheppard. After pulling a 21-lawyer IP litigation group from Ropes & Gray last September and adding three partners from Perkins Coie last June, Sheppard returned to Perkins and picked up another 15 IP litigators. Led by five partners—Veronica Ascarrunz, Robin Brewer, Andrew Klein, Tom Millikan, and Joseph Reid—the group includes attorneys in San Francisco, Silicon Valley, Southern California, and Washington, D.C. For more on the move, see Bloomberg Law, Law.com, and Law360.

Other notable moves:

  • Vinson & Elkinswelcomedfour corporate partners fromWilmerHale:Keith Trammell,Chalyse Robinson, andDavid Strong, in Denver, andAlex Bahn, in D.C.

  • Continuing to poach partners from other top firms,

    Paul HastingshiredAmanda PersaudfromRopes & Grayin New York. She will co-chair the rapidly growing funds and private-capital practice at Paul Hastings.

  • How to cut deals like a porn star: serving as the

    general counsel to OnlyFanshas to be one of the more interesting in-house gigs out there. And it will soon be held byDavid Eisman, who’s leavingSkadden Arpsto assume the position.
  • Jason Manion, former counselor to the U.S. attorney general, is departing from the DOJ andjoiningthe new White House Task Force to Eliminate Fraud, where he’ll serve as its general counsel.

Thanks for joining me for this latest edition of Judicial Notice. And for those of you who will be in New York City this coming Wednesday, May 6, please join me for a panel discussion about Biglaw’s move into contingency litigation, hosted by Burford Capital. Breakfast will be provided—as will CLE credit, courtesy of Lawline—and I look forward to seeing some of you there.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!

Elonis isn’t the only precedent that’s problematic for this prosecution. As David French pointed out on Advisory Opinions, there’s also Watts v. United States (1969), in which the Court ordered the acquittal of a defendant who said this about then-president Lyndon B. Johnson: “I have already received my draft classification as 1-A, and I have got to report for my physical this Monday coming. I am not going. If they ever make me carry a rifle, the first man I want to get in my sights is L.B.J.”

Trump supporters might dismiss The Post and David French as less than objective, given their past criticisms of the president. But even Professor Jonathan Turley, who frequently defends Trump, wrote a piece for Fox News arguing that “this indictment is facially unconstitutional, absent some unknown new facts.”

Raising that possibility, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche told NBC News that the case rests not just on the Instagram post, but on “a body of evidence that [prosecutors] collected over the series of about 11 months.” And the indictment does indicate that “the unredacted version of this document has been filed under seal.”

So it’s theoretically possible that there’s more here. But based on the cases brought so far against Comey and other Trump foes, there’s reason to be skeptical. Indeed, there’s reason to think that Trump DOJ officials—including Blanche, who’d like to go from acting AG to presidentially nominated, Senate-confirmed AG—feel a certain amount of pressure “to execute [Trump’s] increasingly extreme demands without much pushback,” in the words of The Times (gift link).

If the seashells indictment ends up getting dismissed—or 86’ed, if you prefer—that doesn’t spell clear sailing for Jim Comey. The DOJ is also investigating Comey for possibly leaking classified information, according to Bloomberg Law. When it comes to going after Trump enemies, the motto of the administration seems to be, “If at first you don’t succeed, try—and try, and try, and try—again.”

Other lawyers in the news:

  • The seashells indictment was signed by

    W. Ellis Boyle, currently serving as Interim U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of North Carolina—and nominated to serve as the permanent U.S. Attorney. Although he’s a Trump loyalist, Boyle at least hassomerelevant experience, having previously served as an assistant U.S. attorney (on the civil side). Other Trump U.S. attorney picks have been more problematic, as discussed by Jeffrey Toobin inThe Times(gift link).

  • Is

    William “Bill” Burck, the global co-managing partner ofQuinn Emanuel, back in the good graces of the Trump family? As reported byBloomberg Law, Burck—who was fired last year by the Trump Organization as an outside ethics counsel, after he started defending Harvard in litigation against the administration—is now representing World Liberty Financial, a crypto venture co-founded and co-owned by Trump family members (among other investors).

  • As I

    mentionedlast week, trial inMusk v. Altman—Elon Musk’s lawsuit against fellow billionaire Sam Altman, OpenAI, and Microsoft,challengingOpenAI’s conversion to a for-profit company—is now underway, before JudgeYvonne Gonzalez Rogers(N.D. Cal.). I expect to have more to say about this trial, which should go on for a few weeks; for now, I’ll just highlight the breakout star of the proceedings so far:William Savitt, co-chair of the executive committee and co-chair of litigation atWachtell Lipton. For more on Bill Savitt and hiscontentiouscross-examination of Musk—with whom Savitt has a history, having successfully sued the gazillionaire to follow through on his purchase of Twitter (now X)—see thisBusiness Insider profileby Jacob Shamsian.

Judge of the Week: Justice Elena Kagan.

One of the biggest legal news stories of last week was the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision in Louisiana v. Callais, a major ruling concerning the interaction between Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, the Fourteenth Amendment, and the Fifteenth Amendment. It’s an incredibly controversial and consequential case—and I discuss it in more detail below, as Litigation of the Week.

For now, I’d like to highlight Justice Elena Kagan, who wrote the liberals’ dissent in this 6-3 case, taking on the majority opinion of Justice Samuel Alito (who was Judge of the Week quite recently, so I’m not featuring him again today). Because Justice Kagan is the most moderate and least confrontational of the liberal justices, she sometimes takes flak from the left. But her Callais dissent got her major props, in progressive circles and beyond—as did her reading it from the bench (for 14 minutes), the first oral dissent of the Term (as noted by Mark Walsh of SCOTUSblog).

As Elie Mystal wrote in The Nation, “Justice Elena Kagan brings clarity to what Alito tries to hide.” Professor Richard Hasen, at Election Law Blog, commended her “strenuous” dissent for getting the analysis “exactly right.” At The Bulwark, Professor Kim Wehle praised the justice’s “fierce, eloquent 48-page dissent” (which was, as noted by Adam Feldman over at Legalytics, “52% longer than the majority”).

Here’s the dissent’s eminently quotable conclusion (and yes, many commentators noted the absence of the traditional “respectfully”):

[T]he Court’s gutting of Section 2 puts [the] achievement [of the Voting Rights Act] in peril. I dissent because Congress elected otherwise. I dissent because the Court betrays its duty to faithfully implement the great statute Congress wrote. I dissent because the Court’s decision will set back the foundational right Congress granted of racial equality in electoral opportunity. I dissent.

Whether you agree or disagree with Justice Kagan on the merits, you can’t deny her writing chops. There’s a reason legal writing guru Ross Guberman once told me—half-jokingly, but only half—that BriefCatch, his powerful legal writing and editing tool, was “sort of normed” on Justice Kagan (who “consistently earns the highest BriefCatch writing-quality scores of any living Justice,” per Guberman).

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

  • Justice Kagan and her two fellow liberal justices didn’t attend last Tuesday’s White House state dinner honoring King Charles. It’s not clear whether they were invited—but their six conservative colleagues were, since they all

    showed up.

  • Last week’s featured jurist, Judge

    Alan Albright(W.D. Tex.), recently announced his plan to retire this coming August. He said he wanted to return to litigating—but one can’t help wondering whether he also wanted to escape his massive backlog of cases. As reported by Ryan Autullo ofBloomberg Law, Judge Albright had 446 undecided motions on his latest “six-month list,” the list of civil motions ripe for a decision for six months or longer but not yet resolved. Many district judges I know aim for a six-month list of zero, or maybe a number in the low single digits—so 446 is staggering. (For perspective, JudgeGeorge Daniels(S.D.N.Y) wound up on the front page ofThe Times(gift link) back in 2004 because he had, as reported by Ben Weiser, 289 motions on his six-month list.)

In memoriam: Judge James Lawrence King—who served on the federal bench for some 55 years, including a stint as chief judge for the Southern District of Florida—passed away at 98. May he rest in peace.

Job of the Week: an opportunity at a leading legal recruiter.

Lateral Link seeks a high-caliber recruiter for a leadership-capable role on its Am Law–level platform. This is an opportunity to grow within a flat, merit-focused organization that rewards ambition and fosters lifelong careers in legal recruiting. Lateral Link partners with firms and corporate legal teams nationwide, handling a wide range of senior and niche placements. Qualified candidates will have a J.D. from a top school or a proven track record at a premier search firm, along with the integrity and drive to advance eight core values: Trust, Respect, Integrity, Accountability, Cooperation, Resourcefulness, Diversity, and Compassion. Compensation is market-leading: 50–70% ramped commissions, year-end discretionary bonuses up to 15%, and principal-level bonuses, with total earnings frequently above peers. To learn more, please contact Mike Allen at mallen@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: Louisiana v. Callais .

Last Wednesday, as mentioned above, the Supreme Court issued its eagerly anticipated ruling in Louisiana v. Callais. For media coverage—and there was a ton, as you’d expect for a ruling of this importance—see Howard Bashman’s How Appealing.

In an opinion by Justice Alito, the six conservative justices struck down Louisiana’s voting map as an unconstitutional racial gerrymander. The map had been redrawn to include an additional majority-Black district because of a 2022 federal-court ruling holding that the earlier map, which had only one majority-Black district, likely violated Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act. But according to Justice Alito, “[t]he State’s attempt to satisfy the [2022] ruling, although understandable, was an unconstitutional racial gerrymander.” As for future claims brought under the Voting Rights Act, according to Callais, Section 2 “imposes liability only when the evidence supports a strong inference that the State intentionally drew its districts to afford minority voters less opportunity because of their race” (emphasis added).

Justice Kagan, joined by Justices Sonia Sotomayor and Ketanji Brown Jackson, dissented. According to the dissent, under the majority’s view, “a State can, without legal consequence, systematically dilute minority citizens’ voting power…. Without a basis in Section 2’s text or the Constitution, the majority formulates new proof requirements for plaintiffs alleging vote dilution.” In the dissenters’ view, Callais “renders Section 2 all but a dead letter.”

Justice Clarence Thomas, joined by Justice Neil Gorsuch, joined Justice Alito’s opinion in full—but would have gone further. In Justice Thomas’s view, Section 2 of the VRA “does not regulate districting at all.” Why? The statutory text prevents states “from imposing or applying a ‘voting qualification,’ ‘prerequisite to voting,’ or ‘standard, practice, or procedure,’ in a manner that results in a denial or abridgement of the right to vote based on race.” Drawing election maps “does not fall within any of those three categories,” in Thomas’s view—case closed.

Reactions to Callais ran the gamut. Professor Rick Hasen, writing at Slate, declared that the majority “gutt[ed] what remained of [VRA] protections for minority voters,” and “will go down in history as one of the most pernicious and damaging Supreme Court decisions of the past century.” Meanwhile, the editorial board of the WSJ (gift link) praised Callais as “a large step toward ending the partisan abuse of race to carve up Congressional districts in a way that violates the Constitution.”

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

  • . Because it came down on the same day asFirst Choice Women’s Resource Centers, Inc. v. DavenportCallais,First Choicereceived very little attention. Writing for a unanimous Court, Justice Gorsuch held that First Choice—”a religious nonprofit organization that has provided counseling and resources to pregnant women in New Jersey,” generally urging them not to have abortions—could go straight to federal court to challenge a subpoena from the New Jersey Attorney General’s Office (seeking donor information).
  • . In a one-paragraph, summary disposition, the justicesAbbott v. LULACreverseda lower-court decision that would have blocked Texas’s plans for redrawing its congressional districts (in favor of Republicans). The justices had temporarily given Texas’s new map the green light back in December; this latest ruling put the new map in place indefinitely. The three liberal justices dissented.
  • . From John Ross ofCarroll v. TrumpShort Circuit: “the Second Circuit willnotreconsider its panel opinion affirming E. Jean Carroll’s $83.3 million defamation award against Trump for statements he made while president. Three dissenting judges [JudgesSteven Menashi,Michael Park, andDebra Ann Livingston] would have granted rehearing to consider presidential immunity arguments and to allow the United States to belatedly substitute itself in Trump’s shoes.”
  • . In an opinion by JudgeCunha v. FredenJoseph Bianco, the Second Circuitdepartedfrom the Fifth and Eighth Circuits and rejected the Trump administration’s claim that federal law “prohibits the setting of bond for all inadmissible noncitizens” (i.e., the government argues that “mandatory detention must continue regardless of how long removal proceedings take”).
  • . The Fifth Circuit voted against rehearing en banc in a challenge to the federal ban on machineguns. But five judges joined three opinions—by JudgesUnited States v. WilsonDon Willett,James Ho, andAndrew Oldham—expressing a willingness to reconsider the constitutionality of the ban, under the Commerce Clause and/or the Second Amendment (in this case or a more appropriate one).
  • The Rohingya are a minority in Myanmar that has been subjected to discrimination and violence for decades. In an opinion by JudgeJane Doe 1 v. Meta Platforms, Inc.Ryan Nelson, the Ninth Circuit affirmed the dismissal of a lawsuit filed by members of the Rohingya community, seeking to hold Meta liable for the proliferation of anti-Rohingya content on Facebook. The panel reached this result based on circuit precedent about Section 230 immunity—but Judges Nelson andMarsha Berzon, in concurrences, called that precedent into question.
  • andDickinson v. Trump. In orders authored by JudgesReach Community Development v. United States Department of Homeland SecurityKenneth LeeandEric Tung, the Ninth Circuit granted stays requested by the Trump administration of preliminary injunctions regulating various actions by ICE in Portland, Oregon. JudgeAna de Albadissented (in whole or in part).
  • . All I’ll say about this Ninth Circuit habeas case is, “Don’t be surprised if it gets summarily reversed by SCOTUS”—for the reasons given in JudgeHogan v. BeanDaniel Bress’s dissental, joined by eight of his colleagues.

Litigations of the Week: Danco Laboratories, LLC v. State of Louisiana and GenBioPro, Inc. v. State of Louisiana .

Louisiana, which has a near-total ban on abortion, specifically prohibits administering, prescribing, or selling a drug like mifepristone to perform abortions. Last year, Louisiana filed a legal challenge to a Biden-era FDA regulation allowing mifepristone to be prescribed online and dispensed through the mail, without any need for an in-person visit to a doctor. Responding to Louisiana’s lawsuit, the FDA, now under Trump, said it was conducting a comprehensive review of its mifepristone protocols—but in the meantime, the FDA opposed suspension of the regulation.

Judge David Joseph (W.D La.) agreed that Louisiana was likely to prevail in its challenge to the mifepristone regulation—but balancing the equities and the public interest, he declined to stay the regulation in the meantime. So Louisiana sought a stay pending appeal from the Fifth Circuit—and on Friday, in an opinion by Judge Stuart Kyle Duncan, the court granted the state its requested stay.

On Saturday, two manufacturers of mifepristone sought emergency relief from the U.S. Supreme Court, as reported by Amy Howe of SCOTUSblog. Specifically, the companies—Danco Laboratories, represented by Hogan Lovells, and GenBioPro, represented by Arnold & Porter—asked the Court to (1) vacate the Fifth Circuit’s stay pending appeal, i.e., reinstate the FDA’s regulation allowing mifepristone to be prescribed online and distributed through the mail; and (2) enter an immediate administrative stay, to preserve the (pre-Fifth Circuit) status quo in the meantime.

The significance of this litigation, whether you support Louisiana or the drug companies, can’t be denied. As Kelly Baden—vice president for public policy at the Guttmacher Institute, a research organization that supports abortion rights—said to The Times, “This decision represents the most sweeping threat to abortion since the overturning of Roe v. Wade.” Meanwhile, Marjorie Dannenfelser, president of Susan B. Anthony Pro-Life America, praised the Fifth Circuit’s ruling to The Times as “a huge victory for victims and survivors of Biden’s reckless mail-order abortion drug regime.”

What happens next? As Howe of SCOTUSblog explained, the drug companies’ applications will first go to Justice Alito, who handles emergency requests from the Fifth Circuit as its circuit justice, and he’ll probably ask Louisiana to respond before taking action. So just as the Court is entering its busiest period, with the justices (and their clerks) toiling away on landmark opinions in cases involving birthright citizenship and transgender athletes, it has a new issue on its emergency/interim/shadow docket—and not just any issue, but one of indisputable importance and impact.

In other litigation news:

  • In the latest legal battle between the Trump administration and blue states, the DOJ filed

    two lawsuitsagainst New Jersey, challenging state laws (1) providing financial aid to undocumented immigrants attending public colleges in the Garden State and (2) banning most law enforcement agents, including federal agents, from wearing masks.

  • OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma was officially

    sentencedon Tuesday by JudgeMadeline Cox Arleo(D.N.J.), under a plea agreement requiring it to pay billions in fines ​and penalties—after which Purdue concluded operations, exited bankruptcy, andre-emergedas a nonprofit dedicated to treating opioid addiction.

  • Speaking of resolving major mass-tort litigations, the Archdiocese of New York agreed to an

    $800 million settlementwith 1,300 survivors of sexual abuse with claims against the Catholic Church.

  • Speaking of settlements, the American Bar Association

    resolveda lawsuit brought by Edward Blum’s American Alliance for Equal Rights, by agreeing to provide scholarships previously reserved for “racial and ethnic minority students” on a “race/ethnicity-neutral” basis going forward.

  • Still on the subject of settlements, could the Trump DOJ end up settling… Donald Trump’s lawsuit against the federal government over the 2019 leak of his tax returns, in which he seeks $10 billion in damages? Judge

    Kathleen Williams(S.D. Fla.)orderedDOJ lawyers and Trump’s personal attorneys to address “whether the parties are sufficiently adverse to each other,” now that Trump himself controls the Department of Justice.

  • Reflecting Trump’s degree of control over the DOJ, on Monday the Justice Department filed a

    motionin the White House ballroom construction litigation, asking JudgeRichard Leon(D.D.C.) to dissolve his injunction blocking the construction—and channeling Trump’s “recognizable online voice,” perThe Times. Consider this excerpt: “Because it is DONALD J. TRUMP, a highly successful real estate developer, who has abilities that others don’t, especially those who assume the Office of President, this frivolous and meritless lawsuit was filed. Again, it’s called TRUMP DERANGEMENT SYNDROME.”

  • Oops, she (allegedly) did it again: Britney Spears was

    chargedwith a misdemeanor count of driving under the influence of ​alcohol or drugs. The pop star was previously hit with two driving-related misdemeanor charges back in 2007, arising out of an allegedhit-and-run incident(but one charge was dismissed by a judge, and a second was the subject of a mistrial).

Deal of the Week: the Trump administration’s (unsuccessful) effort to rescue Spirit Airlines.

Deals come, and deals go. One possible acquisition mentioned in these pages apparently won’t be happening: French spirits company Pernod Ricard dropped its pursuit of Jack Daniel’s maker Brown-Forman, after a failure to agree on terms. But other transactions have entered the pipeline, including a possible offer for eBay from GameStop (whose market cap of around $11 billion is less than a quarter of eBay’s).

Another deal that proved evanescent was a government-led rescue of Spirit Airlines, which filed for Chapter 11 last year—and had come up with a reorganization plan, before the war-induced spike in fuel prices threw a wrench into the works. The Trump administration tried to put together a $500 million bailout that would give the feds a 90% stake in Spirit (which the government would then try to sell for a profit after oil prices returned to earth). But the talks failed after creditors objected to how they’d be treated under the deal, per the WSJ (gift link). So on Saturday, Spirit Airlines shut down permanently, canceling all its flights. Spirit Airlines, RIP.

Law Firm of the Week: King & Spalding.

Besides litigation shops like Quinn Emanuel, Williams & Connolly, Susman Godfrey, Kellogg Hansen, and Boies Schiller, which Am Law 200 firms remain committed to lit? I’d mention—among others, this list of three is far from exhaustive—Gibson Dunn, Jones Day, and my latest Law Firm of the Week, King & Spalding.

For his latest Litigators of the Week, Ross Todd of Am Law Litigation Daily selected K&S partners Lazar Raynal, Craig Carpenito, and Mike Lombardo. They represented Skillz, the publicly traded maker of a skill-based gaming app, which accused a competitor, Papaya, of false advertising. Specifically, Skillz alleged that Papaya utilized bots as “players” in games that it falsely advertised as skill-based, in violation of the Lanham Act. After a two-week trial before Judge Denise Cote (S.D.N.Y.), a jury agreed with Skillz—and awarded $420 million in damages.

Skillz Platform Inc. v. Papaya Gaming Ltd. is a good example of a trend I recently wrote about: corporations, including public companies, becoming more comfortable with being plaintiffs. And King & Spalding is a good example of an elite law firm moving beyond traditional defense work to handle affirmative litigation, often on contingency (a subject I’ll be moderating a panel about on Wednesday morning, for Am Law 200 litigation partners who are interested in learning more).

As reflected in the latest Am Law 100 rankings, King & Spalding is #18 in revenue per lawyer and #12 in profits per equity partner. When you’re this high in the rankings, the competition is fierce. If K&S took Skillz v. Papaya on contingency—whether partial or full—it could be a nice boost for its 2026 financials.

Move of the Week: Sheppard adding 15 IP litigators from Perkins Coie.

Intellectual property litigators are flocking to Sheppard. After pulling a 21-lawyer IP litigation group from Ropes & Gray last September and adding three partners from Perkins Coie last June, Sheppard returned to Perkins and picked up another 15 IP litigators. Led by five partners—Veronica Ascarrunz, Robin Brewer, Andrew Klein, Tom Millikan, and Joseph Reid—the group includes attorneys in San Francisco, Silicon Valley, Southern California, and Washington, D.C. For more on the move, see Bloomberg Law, Law.com, and Law360.

Other notable moves:

  • Vinson & Elkinswelcomedfour corporate partners fromWilmerHale:Keith Trammell,Chalyse Robinson, andDavid Strong, in Denver, andAlex Bahn, in D.C.

  • Continuing to poach partners from other top firms,

    Paul HastingshiredAmanda PersaudfromRopes & Grayin New York. She will co-chair the rapidly growing funds and private-capital practice at Paul Hastings.

  • How to cut deals like a porn star: serving as the

    general counsel to OnlyFanshas to be one of the more interesting in-house gigs out there. And it will soon be held byDavid Eisman, who’s leavingSkadden Arpsto assume the position.
  • Jason Manion, former counselor to the U.S. attorney general, is departing from the DOJ andjoiningthe new White House Task Force to Eliminate Fraud, where he’ll serve as its general counsel.

Thanks for joining me for this latest edition of Judicial Notice. And for those of you who will be in New York City this coming Wednesday, May 6, please join me for a panel discussion about Biglaw’s move into contingency litigation, hosted by Burford Capital. Breakfast will be provided—as will CLE credit, courtesy of Lawline—and I look forward to seeing some of you there.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!
items.description: This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by With a presence in over a dozen cities across the United States and Asia, Lateral Link boasts an expert recruiting team of former practicing attorneys dedicated to sourcing top-tier legal talent for a diverse clientele, including major international law…

items.title: The Top 20 Most Profitable Law Firms (2025)
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This post is sponsored by

Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding cost or risk or giving up control. Clients include Fortune 500 companies and Am Law 100 firms, who turn to Burford to pursue strong claims, manage legal costs and accelerate recoveries. Learn more at burfordcapital.com.

When I recently mentioned the compensation paid to the most coveted lateral partners, some readers—especially the older ones, who recall when $6 million was at or near the top of the market, circa 20 years ago—were incredulous.

Is it really true that Jeff Wall, SCOTUS advocate extraordinaire, is getting $15 million a year from Gibson Dunn, as reported by The Wall Street Journal (gift link)? Or that some Biglaw partners earn as much as $40 million a year, according to Law360 and Law.com?

Yes, Virginia, there is a Santa Claus. And he comes down the chimneys of partners with giant bags full of cash.

And Santa Claus, whom I’m playfully invoking because of the famous quotation, isn’t the right comparison. Santa brings gifts; partner compensation is earned.

As David Boies once told me—speaking about associate bonuses, but the same point applies to partner pay—“law firms are not eleemosynary institutions.” They pay high compensation, to associates and partners alike, because it’s in their self-interest.

Some senior readers who were skeptical of huge pay packages invoked Dewey & LeBoeuf—which doled out crazy compensation, to both lateral and homegrown partners, before collapsing and filing for bankruptcy in 2012. They asked: Dewey now have a bubble in partner pay on our hands?

There’s no doubt that some individual partners are overpaid. If an expensive lateral hire leaves a firm after only two or three years, sometimes it’s because they had guaranteed comp for a certain period, the guarantee ran out, and their pay was set to drop (because they’re not generating enough revenue at their new firm to justify their desired pay under the firm’s standard comp system).

But I wouldn’t say there’s a partner-pay bubble overall. Unlike Dewey, many if not most high-paying firms have the financial wherewithal to be offering what they’re offering. And, in fact, these firms need to compensate partners at these levels, to secure the talent they need to stay competitive.

And what’s my basis for saying this? The 2026 Am Law 100 rankings, published earlier this month by The American Lawyer.

The rankings and their supporting data show that elite firms are doing quite well for themselves, thank you very much. So these firms are investing in their futures by onboarding more and more superstars, which creates a virtuous cycle: great lateral hires increase firm profitability, enabling the firms that excel at it to use their higher profitability and growing war chests to attract even more top talent.

Let’s start with the big picture for Biglaw, reflected in certain key metrics (based on firms’ financial performances in calendar year 2025):

Total gross revenue: $178.95 billion, up 13.0%

Revenue per lawyer (RPL): $1.39 million, up 8.7%

Profits per equity partner (PEP): $3.59 million, up 14.0%

No, these numbers aren’t adjusted for inflation. But with inflation at around 2.7% in 2025, Biglaw beat it by a multiple of three to five.

And how did 2025 stack up compared to recent years? Here’s some data:

In his analysis of the latest rankings, Patrick Smith of The American Lawyer accurately observed that “Am Law 100 firms, for the most part, reproduced their stellar year from 2024.” And that’s nothing to complain about, since 2024 was the best year for Biglaw since the banner year of 2021 (when the industry was bouncing back from the short, but deep, pandemic-induced recession).

One metric where 2024 outpaced 2025 was headcount. In 2024, the attorney population of the Am Law 100 grew by 7.7%; in 2025, headcount grew at around half that pace, by 4%. (In case you’re curious, it’s now at 128,868 attorneys—a reminder that even if Biglaw dominates the pages of Original Jurisdiction, its lawyers account for a small share of America’s 1.4 million lawyers.)

Not all Biglaw attorney populations grew at the same rate—and the differing rates help explain the growth in profits per equity partner. The ranks of nonequity partners increased by almost 7%, while the number of equity partners grew by only 2%. The overall headcount growth of 4% is a blend of the growth rates of equity partners, nonequity partners, and associates.

Because overall headcount grew by 4% but the number of equity partners grew by only 2%, leverage—defined as the ratio of (1) lawyers who aren’t equity partners to (2) equity partners—increased as well. And increased leverage generally means increased profits per equity partner, because the profit generated by each lawyer who’s not an equity partner winds up in the pockets of the equity partners.

Up until now, we’ve been discussing the collective performance of Biglaw, which conceals a fair amount of variation. Now let’s look at how individual firms fared, in terms of gross revenue, revenue per lawyer, and profits per equity partner—and maybe gossip a bit about what some of the numbers say about different firms.

And yes, I will explain how firms get to $30 million and $40 million partner paydays….

Gross Revenue

The Am Law 100 ranking is a ranking of the largest law firms in the United States based on gross revenue. So let’s eat our salad first and look at the revenue ranking—before we turn to the entree of revenue per lawyer, followed by the dessert of profits per equity partner.

Collectively, the Am Law 100 grew their total gross revenue by 13 percent, which provides one possible benchmark for evaluating the performance of individual firms (although it’s not a perfect apples-to-apples comparison, since the firms that make up the Am Law 100 change slightly from year to year). If a firm increased its gross revenue by more than 13 percent, it’s fair to say that firm had a strong year.

In 2025, 62 firms had $1 billion or more in revenue in 2025, compared to 58 in 2024. And 94 firms in the Am Law 100 firm grew their gross revenue last year—another data point confirming that 2025, on the whole, treated Biglaw well. Only two firms witnessed revenue drops (and four firms were new to the Am Law 100 this year, so they had no year-over-year change).

Here are the top 20 firms in the 2026 Am Law 100, ranked by their gross revenue in 2025. You can access the full list at The American Lawyer.

As usual, there was a lot of stability in the top 20:

As noted by Am Law, the top 10 was fairly stable, with seven firms not moving at all. Two firms, Gibson Dunn and Skadden Arps, swapped places (at #4 and #5). Simpson Thacher climbed two spots, to break into the top 10; Morgan Lewis dropped by one, to #11.

In the top 20 firms, 19 of the firms were returnees. The newcomer, at #16, was McDermott Will & Schulte, created through the merger of McDermott Will & Emery (formerly #23) and Schulte Roth & Zabel (formerly #91). The firm that left the top 20 to make room for MWS was Goodwin, which went from #19 to #21.

For the ninth year in a row, Kirkland & Ellis was the #1 firm in revenue. It grew its revenue by almost 20%, to more than $10.5 billion, becoming the first firm in history to break the $10 billion mark. And three firms in the top 20 boasted even larger percentage gains than Kirkland: Simpson Thacher (22.7%), Paul Weiss (23.8%), and Davis Polk (26.0%—the biggest jump in the top 20).

We don’t have the Fortune 500 ranking based on 2025 financial performance yet. But if we use the ranking based on 2024 financial performance, the cutoff for inclusion was $7.4 billion in revenue—meaning that Kirkland and Latham would be Fortune 500 members, if law firms were eligible for inclusion.

Breaking into the top 20 required almost $2.8 billion in revenue in 2025, compared to $2.4 billion in 2024.

Moving beyond the top 20, two firms in the Am Law 100 experienced revenue drops: Fragomen (-5.50%) and Cadwalader Wickersham & Taft (-3.35%). Fragomen is a high-end immigration firm that does a lot of work for corporate clients on employment-based visas, so it was hurt by the Trump administration’s policy changes—especially the $100,000 fee for H-1B visas. As for Cadwalader, its troubles have been chronicled extensively in these pages, but it saved itself through a merger with Hogan Lovells (so next year, CWT won’t be in the Am Law 100 as an independent firm).

There were four newcomers to the Am Law 100, all fueled by mergers: McDermott, discussed above; Troutman Pepper Locke, forged in January 2025 through the merger of Troutman Pepper (#52) and Locke Lord (#99); Taft Stettinius & Hollister, which has grown through a series of mergers and acquisitions (most recently, its rescue of Morris Manning & Martin); and Womble Bond Dickinson, which merged with Lewis Roca.

Per Am Law, the firm that grew revenue the most without a merger was Littler Mendelson, whose revenue skyrocketed by 40.89% to $1.029 billion. Like Fragomen, Littler was also affected by changes in law and policy: as a labor and employment powerhouse, Littler benefited from the first year of the Trump administration, which forced employers to deal with legal issues related to civil rights, discrimination, DEI, and immigration.

After Littler, seven other firms grew revenue by more than 20%: Shook Hardy & Bacon (28.43%), Ballard Spahr (27.63%), Davis Polk (25.98%), Polsinelli (25.52%), Paul Weiss (23.8%), Susman Godfrey (23.17%), and Simpson (22.7%).

What can be said about the four firms that fought executive orders from the Trump administration, including Susman, and the nine firms that reached settlements, including Paul Weiss and Simpson? As reported by Abigail Adcox of Am Law, seven out of the nine settling firms saw double-digit revenue growth (with only Cadwalader seeing a decline, and A&O Shearman not reporting yet).

Results were mixed for the four fighting firms: Susman (23.17%), Jenner & Block (14.86%), Perkins Coie (2.0%), and WilmerHale (0.6%). The EOs, enjoined by judges, never went into effect—so I’m guessing they didn’t have much of an impact on the firms’ business. Rather, their performances can be chalked up to other factors; for example, WilmerHale does a fair amount of white-collar defense and investigations work, which has been slow under Trump.

Revenue Per Lawyer

I agree with The American Lawyer: revenue per lawyer is “the most reliable measure of a firm’s financial health.” It’s not as easy to increase as gross revenue, which a firm can juice through mass hiring or mergers, or profits per equity partner, which a firm can hack by shrinking its equity partnership aka “de-equitizing.”

It was a good year for Biglaw in terms of RPL. For the Am Law 100 as a whole, RPL increased by 8.7%, and the gains were widespread, with 92 firms growing RPL.

Here are the top 20 firms in the 2026 Am Law 100 ranking, ranked by their revenue per lawyer in 2025. You can access the full list at The American Lawyer.

The top 10 firms in RPL stayed the same, simply changing positions among themselves. But the #1 and #2 firms stayed the same: Wachtell Lipton, with a mind-boggling $5 million in RPL, and Susman Godfrey, with $2.9 million in RPL.

In discussing the recent Vault ranking of the most prestigious firms, I wrote that Wachtell Lipton is arguably the nation’s #1 corporate firm, and Susman Godfrey is arguably the nation’s #1 litigation firm. I’d argue that it’s no coincidence that they’re #1 and #2 in RPL. See why I like RPL as a measure of a firm quality?

The top 20 firms in RPL were almost entirely the same, again just changing places within the top 20. There was only one newcomer: Dechert, which climbed from #21 to #16. The firm that dropped out of the top 20 to make room for Dechert was Sidley Austin, which fell from #20 to #21.

As I’ve noted in years past, RPL correlates generally with prestige. Of the top 20 firms in RPL, 14 are in the Vault 20. These are the six firms that are top 20 in RPL but not in the Vault 20: Paul Hastings (#12 in RPL, #21 in Vault); Dechert (#16 in RPL, #51 in Vault); Fenwick & West (#17 in RPL, #71 in Vault); King & Spalding (#18 in RPL, #36 in Vault); Proskauer Rose (#19 in RPL, #33 in Vault); and Debevoise (#20 in RPL, #23 in Vault).

And of the Vault 20 firms, 14 are in the top 20 in RPL. These are the six Vault 20 firms that are not in the top 20 by RPL: Sidley (#11 in Vault, #21 in RPL); Covington & Burling (#12 in Vault, #36 in RPL); Weil Gotshal (#15 in Vault, #27 in RPL); Cleary Gottlieb (#16 in Vault, #25 in RPL); Williams & Connolly (#17 in Vault, #29 in RPL); and White & Case (#18 in Vault, #47 in RPL).

As you’d expect, high RPL correlates not only with prestige, but with profitability. There’s a lot of overlap between the top 20 firms in RPL and the top 20 firms in profits per equity partner (listed below). There are only three firms in the top 20 by RPL that are not in the top 20 by PEP: Susman (#2 in RPL, #22 in PEP); Fenwick (#17 in RPL, #31 in PEP); and Proskauer (#19 in RPL, #24 in PEP).

As for the three firms that are in the top 20 by PEP but not by RPL, those would be Cleary (#17 in PEP, #25 in RPL); Sidley (#19 in PEP, #21 in RPL); and Weil (#20 in PEP, #27 in RPL).

Reflecting the relative difficulty of growing RPL (compared to gross revenue or profits per equity partner), only four firms in the Am Law 100 grew RPL by more than 20%: Littler Mendelson (33.73%), Shook Hardy (24.12%), Quinn Emanuel (23.39%), and Susman (20.39%).

Three firms experienced dips in RPL: Gordon Rees (-15.79%), Cadwalader (-0.07%), and… Cravath (-0.04%). It’s not much of a drop, but having basically flat RPL isn’t a great place for Cravath to be—and combined with the recent partner departures, it makes me wonder if it’s not the firm it once was.

Profits Per Equity Partner

And now, what you’ve all been waiting for: the top 20 firms in the 2026 Am Law 100 ranking of profits per equity partner, based on PEP in 2025. You can access the full list at The American Lawyer.

Fueled by a robust market for big-ticket, public M&A, Wachtell Lipton, the #1 firm, grew profits per equity partner by 34.48%, to $12.152 million. Wresting its PEP crown back from K&E, WLRK set a new record for profits per equity partner, becoming the first Am Law 100 firm to break the $12 million mark.

It might have dropped to #2, but Kirkland had a great year as well: growing PEP by a healthy 20.19%, it hit $11.121 million.

In 2025, a remarkable 15 firms broke the $7 million mark in PEP—up from eight in 2024 and three in 2023—and an impressive 17 firms out of the top 20 grew PEP by double digits.

The top 10 firms were almost entirely the same, just switching places within the group, except that Paul Hastings (#9) replaced Cravath (#13).

The top 20 firms were also almost entirely the same firms, just reshuffling internally, except Fried Frank (#21) was replaced by Dechert (#16).

For this year’s ranking, it took $5.593 million in PEP to make the top 20—up significantly from the $4.989 million it took for last year’s ranking.

What’s the deal with the “N/A” for Ropes & Gray? As explained in this article, “For the first time, Law.com will report a nonequity tier for Ropes & Gray’s most recent financial results, consistent with the Am Law 100 methodology for nonequity partners. As a result of the change in reporting the firm’s partnership structure, year-over-year percentage changes for Ropes’ net income, profits per equity partners and average compensation per partner are not available.”

With these PEP figures in mind, let’s return to the topic of partner compensation. PEP is an average, so at every firm, some partners earn more and some earn less. The gap between the highest- and lowest-paid equity partners is captured by a concept called the “spread”: if the spread is 3-to-1, the highest-paid partner earns three times as much as the lowest-paid partner, whereas if the spread is 20-to-1, the highest-paid partner earns 20 times as much as the lowest-paid partner.

Back in the heyday of lockstep compensation, when partners were paid based on seniority, spreads tended to be narrow or low. For example, back in 2007, Cravath and Cleary had spreads of 3:1. Cravath’s PEP back then was around $3 million—so you could imagine a lockstep scale in which the most junior partners made $1.5 million, midlevel partners made $3 million, and the most senior partners made $4.5 million.

But with lockstep compensation largely a thing of the past, replaced by so-called “eat what you kill” compensation—or, to be more genteel, “merit-based” compensation—spreads today are dramatically higher. I don’t know the highest spread out there today—please drop me a line if you think your firm has a particularly high one—but I can say that spreads of 15:1 or even 20:1 are no longer unheard of.

Imagine a firm with PEP of $7 million—of which there are 15, per Am Law. It might have the lowest-paid equity partner making $1.5 million, and the highest-paid equity partner making $30 million—for a spread of 20:1. Or take a firm with PEP of $8 million, of which there are nine. It might have the lowest-paid equity partner making $2 million, and the highest-paid equity partner making $40 million.

Because the partners who make $1.5 million or $2 million vastly outnumber the partners who make $30 million or $40 million, it’s not hard to get the math to work with PEP of $7 million or $8 million. Yes, the mean in terms of partner comp is still $7 million or $8 million, but the distribution is far from a bell curve. Instead, it’s heavily weighted toward the low end of the curve—and even the modal partner pay might be below $7 million or $8 million. All the partners earning less than the average are making possible the eye-popping pay of the highest-paid partners.

I expect to have more to say about this in future posts, including a discussion of the rules of thumb about how much a partner should be paid based on their book of business (which suggest that certain partners might be undercompensated at $30 million or $40 million). For now, suffice it to say that based on the latest profits per partner data from the Am Law 100—combined with other trends, including ever-increasing billing rates, ballooning books of business, growing nonequity tiers, rising leverage, and astronomically larger partner-pay spreads—I can confirm that eight-figure partner paydays are both real and spectacular.

Ed. note: If you’re a litigation partner at Am Law 200 firm or leading boutique who’s interested in learning more about increasing your firm’s profitability by handling plaintiff-side work for corporate clients, please join us for our upcoming Burford Briefing breakfast panel, “Biglaw’s Embrace of Contingency Litigation,” on May 6 in New York City. To learn more and register your interest, please visit the event page.

Earlier:

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!
