items.title: The 25 Most Prestigious Law Firms In America (2026)
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
Did the nine firms that reached controversial settlements with the Trump administration last year suffer negative consequences as a result?

In terms of their financial results in 2025, reported in the newly released Am Law 100 rankings (which I’ll be discussing soon), these firms did just fine. As noted by The American Lawyer, seven out of the nine firms enjoyed double-digit revenue growth.

In terms of prestige, however, some settling firms might have taken a hit—at least according to the latest Vault 100, an annual ranking of the nation’s most prestigious law firms, which was released earlier this month. As explained by the Vault Law editors, their rankings¹ reflect the views of more than 20,000 U.S.-based associates, who responded during the survey window of October 22, 2025, to January 23, 2026.

Let’s take it from the top. Here are the 10 highest-ranked firms in the 2026-2027 Vault 100,² with changes in rank from last year noted parenthetically (“—” means no change):

1. Cravath Swaine & Moore (—)
2. Wachtell Lipton Rosen & Katz (—)
3. Skadden Arps Slate Meagher & Flom (—)
4. Latham & Watkins (—)
5. Sullivan & Cromwell (+1)
6. Kirkland & Ellis (-1)
7. Davis Polk & Wardwell (—)
8. Gibson Dunn & Crutcher (+3)
9. Milbank (—)
10. Simpson Thacher & Bartlett (—)

Some thoughts:

Because reputation is “sticky,” i.e., slow and resistant to change, it’s not surprising to see stability in the top 10. Nine out of the top 10 firms were in the top 10 last year. Seven of the top 10 didn’t even change their rank. And for the 11th consecutive year, the #1, #2, and #3 firms remained the same—ever since Cravath ended Wachtell’s 13-year reign at the top, back in 2016.

I’m admittedly biased as a Wachtell alum, but for those of you who will be Vault voters next year, I think it’s time to return Wachtell to the top spot. The firm increased its profits per equity partner by 35 percent, hitting $12.2 million—becoming the first firm in the history of the Am Law 100 to achieve that feat, according to Patrick Smith of Am Law. Meanwhile, Cravath grew its PEP in 2025 to $7.2 million—nothing to sneeze at, but only a 5.2% increase over 2024 (during a year when PEP was up by 14 percent for the Am Law 100 as a whole).³

In addition, Cravath has seen an unusually high number of partner departures in 2026—by my count, nine partners year to date—which could be viewed as a sign that it’s not the firm it once was, at least among partners. And associate perceptions of firms tend to follow those of partners, after a delay. As I wrote last year, Vault rankings are something of a lagging indicator compared to partner perceptions because associates, who don’t follow industry news as closely as partners, might not notice certain developments until they become more obvious.

In 2025, Kirkland reported record-setting financial results: $10.6 billion in gross revenue, up 20%, and $11.1 million in profits per equity partner, also up 20%. So did Paul Weiss: $3.3 billion in gross revenue, up 24%, and $8.6 million in PEP, up 14%. Both also made impressive lateral partner hires in the past year. So why did Kirkland drop a spot, and why did Paul Weiss fall out of the top 10 entirely? These firms’ settlements with the Trump administration strike me as a perfectly plausible contributing factor.⁴ And Paul Weiss also endured some negative publicity related to former chair Brad Karp’s emails with Jeffrey Epstein, which contributed to Karp’s decision to step down as chair.

Yes, Sullivan & Cromwell, the firm that gained a spot at K&E’s expense, actually represents Trump in various appellate matters—and S&C’s chair, Robert Giuffra, played a role in brokering Paul Weiss’s settlement with the Trump administration. But there’s an argument that defending The Donald in a personal legal matter, in an adversarial system where everyone is entitled to representation, is more defensible than settling with the administration.

Congrats to Gibson on making the top 10 this year, taking the #8 spot previously occupied by Paul Weiss. It’s not surprising to me that Gibson was the beneficiary of PW’s drop: GDC has been a top 10 firm in the past, as recently as 2024, and it’s easier to return to the top 10 than it is to break into it. The firm had a good year in 2025, growing revenue by 18 percent and PEP by 24 percent, and it also ranked #3 in Vault’s best law firms for inclusion.

For additional analysis of the Vault 10 or “V10” firms, see Staci Zaretsky’s post over at Above the Law.

Now let’s discuss the rest of the top 25, as well as all firms throughout the Vault 100 that moved up or down by three or more spots (because smaller moves are usually just noise). Changes in rank can offer an interesting window into the state of a firm—or at least how a firm is perceived in the market.

I include my own candid comments on these moves and their significance, likely to be appreciated only by hard-core Biglaw nerds. I was going to apologize for all the wonkery, but then I thought to myself, “Who am I kidding? This is exactly why people subscribe to Original Jurisdiction.”

Here are the firms ranked #11 through #25 in the 2026-2027 Vault 100:

11. Sidley Austin (+1)
12. Covington & Burling (+1)
13. Paul Weiss Rifkind Wharton & Garrison (-5)
14. Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan (—)
15. Weil Gotshal & Manges (—)
16. Cleary Gottlieb Steen & Hamilton (—)
17. Williams & Connolly (+1)
18. White & Case (-1)
19. Susman Godfrey (+4)
20. Ropes & Gray (—)
21. Paul Hastings (-2)
22. Cooley (—)
23. Debevoise & Plimpton (-2)
24. Perkins Coie (+4)
25. WilmerHale (+2)

My observations:

As usual, there’s not much movement in this section of the rankings either. The biggest movers were firms involved in the Trump EOs: Paul Weiss, which settled and dropped five places; and Susman Godfrey and Perkins Coie, which fought and rose four spots apiece. WilmerHale, another fighting firm, gained two places.

What about firms that are representing the fighting firms in litigation against the EOs? Williams & Connolly, counsel to Perkins, rose one spot to #17, and Cooley, counsel to Jenner & Block, held steady at #22.

In my discussion of last year’s Vault ranking, I correctly predicted that Perkins Coie and Susman Godfrey would rise—Perkins because “associates will see having the backbone to stand up to Trump as ‘prestigious,’” and Susman because “at #23, this litigation powerhouse is still ranked far too low, and it’s one of four firms fighting an EO from Trump.”

I still think Susman is too low, but this highlights a limitation of the Vault rankings: comparing Susman, arguably the #1 litigation firm, and Wachtell, arguably the #1 corporate firm, is an apples-to-oranges comparison. Because Biglaw firms are driven by their transactional practices, making the Vault 100 a ranking of largely deal-focused firms, I get why Wachtell is higher in the Vault 100 than Susman (which is #1 in Vault’s separate ranking of litigation specialty firms). But it strikes me as bizarre to say, for example, that Susman, at #19, is less prestigious than White & Case, at #18.⁵

I touted my correct predictions, so I should note my inaccurate ones as well. In 2025, I wrote that I expected “to see gains by Quinn Emanuel, which has been involved in many headline-making cases lately,” and “Paul Hastings, which has been on a lateral partner hiring spree.” But Quinn stayed in place, and Paul Hastings actually dropped by two spots.

Paul Hastings might not care about the associate perceptions reflected in the Vault rankings, since it’s clearly rising in esteem among partners.⁶ But as I previously wrote, if it wants to climb in the Vault 100 next year, it needs to “pull a Milbank” and become a leader rather than a follower in associate comp.

Beyond the top 25, we saw more volatility, as usual—with one firm falling eight spots, and another firm rising 17 places. Reviewing the Vault 100 ranking in its entirety, here are all 26 firms that moved up or down by three or more spots, accompanied by my commentary on some of them:

Gibson Dunn (+3, from #11 to #8)

Paul Weiss (-5, from #8 to #13)

Susman Godfrey (+4, from #23 to #19)

Perkins Coie (+4, from #28 to #24)

Morrison & Foerster (-3, from #24 to #27)

Munger Tolles & Olson (+7, from #36 to #29)

MTO announced a revamped summer program, which I praised, and it also represents Susman Godfrey in the EO litigation. (The fourth firm handling an EO case—Clement & Murphy, counsel to WilmerHale—is a boutique, #5 in Vault’s ranking of litigation specialty firms.)

Willkie Farr & Gallagher (-6, from #29 to #35)

In the wake of its settlement with the administration—which was criticized by one of its most prominent partners, former Second Gentleman Doug Emhoff—Willkie gave back its six-spot gain from the year before (as I predicted).

Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld (-7, from #31 to #38)

Akin enjoyed a third consecutive year of strong growth, so I’m not sure what to make of this seven-spot drop—one of the biggest in the Vault 100. If you have any thoughts, drop me a line.

Jenner & Block (+17, from #60 to #43)

One of the four fighting firms, Jenner jumped a whopping 17 spots—the biggest gain in the Vault 100. I predicted it would rise, but not by this much. Kudos!

McDermott Will & Schulte (-3, from #46 to #43)

Formed through the merger of McDermott Will & Emery and Schulte Roth & Zabel, which took effect in summer 2025, McDermott Will & Schulte debuted at #46—solid, considering that legacy McDermott was #43 and legacy Schulte was #87.

A&O Shearman (+6, from #62 to #56)

A&O Shearman was the only one of nine settling firms to rise in the ranking—by six spots, a pretty significant jump—and I’m not sure why. We don’t know what kind of year the firm had in 2025, but we’ll find out soon. The firm’s financial year will end on April 30, and because it’s a member of the Global 100 rather than the Am Law 100, its finances will be analyzed by Am Law when the Global 100 is published, probably in September.

Boies Schiller Flexner (-6, from #61 to #55)

This move also puzzles me, since it appears that BSF had a solid 2025 (at least based on its associate bonuses; we won’t learn about its finances until later in the year, because it’s an Am Law 200 rather than 100 firm). A contributing factor might have been former partner John Kucera’s AI fail, but I find it hard to believe that drove the entire drop. I’m guessing the firm will fare better in next year’s ranking, based on things like the $35 million and $72.5 million settlements it secured for Jeffrey Epstein victims (both announced after the closing of the Vault survey window).

Cadwalader Wickersham & Taft (-8, from #56 to #64)

Falling by eight spots, CTW experienced the largest drop in the entire Vault 100. Yes, it was one of the nine settling firms—but it had other issues, including a partner exodus and 3.3% drop in revenue. Cadwalader won’t be in next year’s Vault 100, at least not as an independent entity: it’s merging with Hogan Lovells to form Hogan Lovells Cadwalader, a combination that the partnerships approved earlier this month.

Sheppard (+3, from #66 to #69)

Kellogg Hansen (+4, from #74 to #70)

The elite litigation firm continues to climb. Over the course of three years, it went from #88 to #70.

Cahill Gordon & Reindel (-7, from #73 to #66)

Seven spots is a sizable drop. It will be interesting to see, when the Am Law 200 ranking comes out, how Cahill fared financially in 2025 (after a solid year in 2024).

Nixon Peabody (-3, from #72 to #75)

Steptoe (-3, from #76 to #79)

Haynes and Boone (+3, from #84 to #81)

The firm gained three spots last year and three spots the year before.

Faegre Drinker (+3, from #88 to #85)

Faegre also appears to be on an upward trajectory; three years ago, it was #99, and now it’s #85.

Cozen O’Connor (+3, from #91 to #88)

Foley Hoag (+5, from #95 to #90)

Hunton Andrews Kurth (+4, from #97 to #93)

Husch Blackwell (+4, from #100 to #96)

Eversheds Sutherland (from unranked to #98)

I’ve included Eversheds on this list of firms that rose by three or more spots in the latest Vault ranking, although technically we don’t know how much it climbed by, since it emerged from the unranked (as did Littler).

Littler (from unranked to #100)

The labor and employment powerhouse reported the biggest revenue gain in the Am Law 100, growing its top line by a whopping 41 percent.

And this concludes my commentary on the latest Vault 100. For the rankings junkies among you, check out the additional rankings over at the Vault website. And if you have observations of your own, please post them in the comments. Thanks!

I say “rankings,” plural, since Vault offers many other rankings—such as the best firms to work for, the best firms for inclusion (renamed from “diversity and inclusion”), and the best firms in different practice areas and regions. But I’ve focused my discussion of the Vault 100, by far the most closely followed of the various Vault rankings.

Vault referred to this latest ranking as the “2026-2027” Vault 100—which I hope reflects Vault’s intention to abandon its confusing practice of being ahead of the calendar by a year (e.g., last year’s ranking, released in 2025, was referred to as the “2026 Vault 100”). This used to be the practice of U.S. News for its law school rankings, but it dropped it a while ago—and I’m guessing Vault plans to do the same. Referring to the latest ranking as the “2026-2027” ranking paves the way for referring to next year’s ranking as simply the “2027” ranking, naming each ranking after the year in which it appears.

My guess is that the former practice reflected the thought that applicants would use the Vault ranking of year X to help them decide where to work in year X+1. But in the current chaotic recruiting environment, people are all over the map in terms of when they choose a firm versus when they start working there.

The acronym has evolved. When I first started writing about law firm economics, it was “PPP,” standing for “profits per partner.” As non-equity tiers proliferated, it became “PPEP,” or “profits per equity partner.” Now, Am Law’s preferred acronym is “PEP,” a shorter abbreviation for “profits per equity partner,” and that’s what I’ll use going forward.

As noted by the Vault Law editors in their own rankings write-up, even if only Kirkland and Paul Weiss experienced a change in their rankings, five out of the nine settling firms “saw downward changes in their average [raw] scores, but no change in their rankings.” So it could be said that the prestige of these five firms suffered as well—just not enough to result in a rankings change, given the stickiness of prestige.

I incorporate by reference my discussion from a few years ago of what I called the “BMW versus Bugatti” problem: “True car connoisseurs know that Bugattis, which start at around $1.7 million and go as high as $12.5 million, are far more elite than BMWs. But BMWs are far more famous, just because there are so many more of them on the road, and in that sense, BMW is more ‘prestigious’ than Bugatti (to the extent that prestige measures both high esteem and widespread recognition).”

Applying this to the Vault rankings, they “measure the high esteem in which a firm is held, yes, but they also measure sheer name recognition. And firms that are relatively small in headcount and focused on a single practice area, like Susman Godfrey and Kellogg Hansen, will never enjoy as much name recognition as the Cravaths and Skaddens of the world.”

In my opinion (as a former legal recruiter), even firms focused on recruiting lateral partners should care about how they’re perceived in the market by associates. Why? Being able to provide partners with stronger associates is a selling point for a firm; over the years, multiple partners have cited the superiority of the associates at their new firm as a reason they’re glad they made a move. And the quality of a firm’s associates is affected significantly by its overall prestige, one of the biggest factors that law students and young lawyers take into account when picking a firm.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!
items.description: Did the nine firms that reached controversial settlements with the Trump administration last year suffer negative consequences as a result? In terms of their financial results in 2025, reported in the newly released Am Law 100 rankings (which I’ll be discussing soon), these firms did just fine. As…

items.title: Judicial Notice (04.12.26): ‘I Love You, Sir’
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Chambers-ranked and Harvard-educated, Jeff is the trusted closer for high-stakes disputes. His battle-tested system of managed communications empowers clients to make clear, strong decisions in a calm, informed environment. It makes the right resolution obvious. If settlement seems impossible, call him.

Last Monday, I traveled down to lovely Lexington, Virginia, to speak at Washington and Lee University School of Law. I delivered the latest version of my talk about Donald Trump’s executive orders against law firms and the litigation and settlements that followed, which I’ve given to law school chapters of the Federalist Society, the American Constitution Society, and the ACLU (with this particular event sponsored by W&L’s small but active FedSoc chapter). Thanks to the Trump administration for continuing to fight this (losing) battle and giving my talk a longer shelf life.

While down in Old Dominion, I discussed whether Justice Samuel Alito might retire with Ann Marimow of The New York Times (gift link). Sources told Marimow that although Alito hasn’t completed his clerk hiring for October Term 2026, he’s interviewing. Why is this noteworthy? If you look at the last five justices to retire, four of them failed to hire a full slate of clerks right before retiring. (Speaking of SCOTUS clerk hiring, If you’re aware of anyone hired since my last roundup—especially by Alito—please email me at davidlat@substack.com or text me at 917-397-2751.)

And this past weekend, Zach and I made a quick trip to D.C. to attend a launch party for our friend Sarah Isgur’s new book, Last Branch Standing: A Potentially Surprising, Occasionally Witty Journey Inside Today’s Supreme Court (gift link). I’ll have more coverage later; for now, I’ll just urge you to buy the book, which will appeal to Original Jurisdiction readers (who appreciate thoughtful, nuanced, fair-minded commentary).

Otherwise, my week was focused on producing content for OJ. I published stories three days in a row: a write-up of the 2026 U.S. News law school ranking, in which Stanford Law replaced Yale as the #1 school; a podcast interview with Jennifer Bennett of Gupta Wessler, a rising star of the Supreme Court bar with a 4-0 record (while representing workers and employees, who don’t always win before the current Court); and a story about Biglaw firms moving into plaintiff-side work, a collaboration with my friends at Burford Capital (a longtime sponsor of OJ and my co-host for an upcoming breakfast event, for any of you in Manhattan on Wednesday, May 6).

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: Todd Blanche.

After Pam Bondi was terminated by Trump as attorney general, the spotlight shifted to Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche. He’s now the Acting Attorney General—as well as a leading candidate to be nominated as the long-term, Senate-confirmed AG.

On Monday, Blanche held his first press conference as acting AG, where he focused on the Department of Justice’s fraud-fighting efforts—to be led by Assistant Attorney General Colin McDonald, recently confirmed to lead the DOJ’s new National Fraud Enforcement Division. When asked about why Bondi was fired, Blanche disputed media reports—which focused on her lack of success in prosecuting Trump’s political foes, as well as her handling of the controversy over the Jeffrey Epstein files—and said, “Nobody has any idea… except for the president.”

As for his own elevation to acting AG and interest in serving as the permanent AG, Blanche gushed: “I love working for President Trump… If President Trump chooses to keep me as acting, that’s an honor. If he chooses to nominate me, that’s an honor. If he chooses to nominate somebody else and asks me to go do something else, I will say, ‘Thank you very much. I love you, sir.’” (Contacted for comment, First Lady Melania Trump quipped, “That makes one of us.”)

Commentators criticized Blanche’s comments as unseemly, arguing that they call into question his ability to objectively oversee prosecutions of Trump allies accused of wrongdoing. What might have motivated Blanche’s profession of “love” for The Donald? Former U.S. attorney John Fishwick told Fox News, “Blanche seems to be trying out for the top job today in his opening press conference—and surely trying to catch Trump’s attention with his criticism of many of the questions by the press.”

With Blanche as acting AG, who will assume the crucial responsibilities of deputy attorney general, which basically involve running the Department’s day-to-day operations? Blanche announced that conservative litigator Trent McCotter will serve as principal associate deputy attorney general—a powerful position, vacant since Emil Bove left for the Third Circuit—and in that capacity, McCotter will discharge the duties of DAG (with help from the newly confirmed Colin McDonald).

In other news involving lawyers and the Trump administration:

Staying on the DOJ, Professor Dan Rodriguez published an interesting analysis of a proposed rule that would potentially displace state bars in policing ethics violations by Department lawyers. Noting that the rule has been widely criticized as lying beyond federal authority, he concluded that it’s “not clear that [critics of the rule] are right in these claims,” but it’s “not clear that they are wrong.”

Immigration judges are actually part of the Justice Department, not the judiciary—and since the start of Trump’s second term, around 100 have been dismissed and 140 have been appointed. In the wake of this reshaping of the ranks, asylum grant rates have dropped from almost 50 percent to under 10 percent.

In other Trump-related turnover, the Securities and Exchange Commission named David Woodcock, currently a partner in the Dallas and D.C. offices of Gibson Dunn, as the next director of the Division of Enforcement. He’ll succeed Margaret “Meg” Ryan, who stepped down after only six months on the job (reportedly over objections that the SEC was going too easy on Trump allies).

Judge of the Week: Justice Sonia Sotomayor.

In our SCOTUS-obsessed household, we talk a lot about Justice Sonia Sotomayor. According to our son Harlan—who recently delivered a presentation to this second-grade class about Justice Sotomayor, the notable woman he picked to profile for Women’s History Month—Zach and I talk about her “42,774,681 times a day.”

And many OJ readers have been buzzing about Justice Sotomayor as well. At an event hosted by the University of Kansas School of Law last Tuesday, while discussing Noem v. Vasquez Perdomo—a case from September 2025, in which the Court issued an interim order giving the green light to certain immigration stops in the Los Angeles area, over Justice Sotomayor’s forceful dissent—she said, “I had a colleague in that case who wrote [that] these are only temporary stops. This is from a man whose parents were professionals. And probably doesn’t really know any person who works by the hour.”

She didn’t name this colleague, but she didn’t have to. The only justice who explained his Perdomo vote was Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who wrote a concurrence stating that while “apparent ethnicity alone cannot furnish reasonable suspicion,” it can be a “relevant factor… along with other salient factors.” Based on this concurrence, progressives started referring to such immigration stops as “Kavanaugh stops.”

Justice Sotomayor wrote a passionate dissent in Perdomo, which at 21 pages was more than twice as long as the Kavanaugh concurrence, and ended it with “I dissent” (as opposed to the more common “I respectfully dissent”). She criticized the concurrence as “all but declar[ing] that all Latinos, U.S. citizens or not, who work low-wage jobs are fair game to be seized at any time.”

Yes, justices go after each other in opinions—but they typically “leave it on the page,” i.e., don’t bring up their grievances in other contexts. So it was striking to see a justice criticize a colleague outside a written opinion, in a public appearance, and in somewhat personal terms. This led the editorial board of The Wall Street Journal (gift link) to accuse Justice Sotomayor of “profil[ing]” Justice Kavanaugh, in terms of making assumptions about him based on his demographic background as a white male from a well-to-do, professional family. (And her assumptions might not be correct; as a teen, Kavanaugh had summer jobs in both construction and lawn care, where he might have met people who work by the hour—and not $4,000 an hour.)

Perdomo was decided last September—another reason it was so notable to see Justice Sotomayor bring it up now, many months later. I wouldn’t be surprised if Justice Kavanaugh regrets his Perdomo concurrence: in December, he wrote a concurrence in Trump v. Illinois in which he explicitly emphasized that immigration officers “must not make interior immigration stops or arrests based on race or ethnicity.” Maybe The Wise Latina, with the benefit of hindsight, might similarly see her pointed comments about a colleague as unwise?

Or maybe not. Two days later, in a Thursday appearance at the University of Alabama School of Law, Justice Sotomayor said that with “virtually all” of her fellow justices, she has “a civil relationship,” and with “many of them,” she has “a friendship.” One can’t help wondering whether Justice Kavanaugh might be in the first category. As noted by Jimmy Hoover, last week’s remarks appear to diverge from her 2018 comment in which she said that Justice Kavanaugh, recently confirmed after a bruising confirmation process, was now part of the Supreme Court “family.”

What could be going on here? Dahlia Lithwick and Mark Joseph Stern suggested that this could be strategic behavior on Justice Sotomayor’s part, perhaps aimed at pressuring Justice Kavanaugh into voting her way in future immigration-related cases. Another possibility is that behind the scenes, Justice Kavanaugh did something recently to incur Justice Sotomayor’s ire—e.g., voted in a certain case against her position, or circulated a draft opinion she found objectionable. With October Term 2025 entering its home stretch, we might know something sooner rather than later.

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Last week’s featured jurist—Judge Nathan Milliron of Harris County, Texas, who was widely criticized for mistreating court employees and counsel—was apparently unhappy with Houston lawyer James Stafford, an attorney who spoke negatively about Milliron to the media. He accused Stafford of sending an improper “ex parte” communication to the court—even though Stafford doesn’t have, and never has had, any matters before Milliron—and ordered Stafford to show up to court and explain himself. Stafford ignored the order, refusing to appear—and that might be the end of it, since Milliron hasn’t followed up.

Another judge previously mentioned in these pages, Judge Thomas Ludington (E.D. Mich.), pleaded “no contest” to a single charge of operating a vehicle while intoxicated (and a second charge, of operating a vehicle with a blood-alcohol content of 0.17 or more, was dismissed). He’ll be sentenced on May 13. It appears Judge Ludington plans to remain on the bench; his lawyer, Jonathan Steffy, said that His Honor “looks forward to continuing to serve the Federal Court at the highest level and focus on his work, along with his wife and family.”

Judge Chris Taylor won election to the Wisconsin Supreme Court, defeating Judge Maria Lazar, one of her colleagues on the Wisconsin Court of Appeals. Judge Taylor will replace a conservative, Justice Rebecca Bradley, and give liberals a 5-2 majority on the Wisconsin high court.

In other news from the Badger State, former Milwaukee County Judge Hannah Dugan, whom a jury found guilty of obstruction of federal agents, filed motions seeking acquittal or a new trial; Judge Lynn Adelman (E.D. Wis.) denied them.

Judge Roy Altman (S.D. Fla.) wrote a new book, Israel on Trial: Examining the History, the Evidence, and the Law.

In nominations news (with thanks to Howard Bashman’s How Appealing):

Donald Trump announced two new circuit court nominees: former Ohio solicitor general Benjamin Flowers (6th Cir.) and Sullivan & Cromwell partner Matthew Schwartz (2d Cir.). Both clerked for conservative members of SCOTUS—Flowers for the late Justice Scalia, Schwartz for Justice Alito—and Schwartz currently represents Trump in both civil and criminal appellate proceedings.

Judge Ralph Erickson (8th Cir.) became the first Trump appellate appointee to reveal his retirement, announcing that he’ll take senior status upon confirmation of a successor. And who might that be? Per Mike Fragoso, contenders include Judge Daniel Traynor (D.N.D.), North Dakota Solicitor General Phil Axt, and Justice Jerod Tufte of the North Dakota Supreme Court. I give the edge to Judge Traynor—as a sitting federal judge, he’d be easy to confirm, and his elevation would open up a district-court spot for the administration to fill—with Axt as a dark-horse candidate. (As a 2018 graduate of Yale Law, Axt would be criticized by some for not having enough experience—see next bullet.)

For the first time in Trump’s second term, the American Bar Association rated one of his judicial nominees “not qualified” (by a split vote of the committee): RNC senior counsel Katie Lane, nominated to the District of Montana. ABA committee chair Pamela Roberts praised Lane as “a talented lawyer, indeed at the top of her peer group,” but said that with less than nine years of post-law-school experience (including clerkships), she didn’t have the twelve years of experience that the committee generally seeks.

Job of the Week: an opportunity at a Bay Area litigation boutique.

Lateral Link has a confidential opportunity with a premier Bay Area litigation boutique known for handling high-stakes, complex disputes—and producing strong trial results. The firm’s founding partners are planning their succession and seeking a midlevel or senior litigation associate to work closely with them. This opportunity offers a clear path to equity partnership, direct mentorship from highly accomplished trial lawyers, and the chance to inherit and manage a thriving practice. For more information, please contact Buddy Broome at bbroome@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: AFSCME v. SSA.

Legal commentators and law professors aren’t the only folks arguing about the Supreme Court’s interim docket. Lower-court judges have strong views too—as we saw from the Fourth Circuit’s en banc decision last Friday in American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees v. Social Security Administration.

AFSCME v. SSA might ring a bell because it went up to the Supreme Court last June. Over dissenting votes from the three liberal justices, a majority of justices voted to grant emergency relief to the government and stay a district-court preliminary injunction that would have blocked the Department of Government Efficiency from accessing certain records held by the Social Security Administration.

The case went back down to the Fourth Circuit, which went en banc to consider it. By a vote of 9-6, the court voted to vacate the preliminary injunction, but disagreed on the reasoning:

Judge Toby Heytens, joined by two fellow Democratic appointees, voted to vacate the injunction because the plaintiffs “did not show—based on the record before the district court when it entered this preliminary injunction—that they were ‘likely to suffer irreparable harm in the absence of preliminary relief.’”

Judge J. Harvie Wilkinson, joined by three other Republican appointees, voted to vacate, arguing that “the Supreme Court’s issuance of the stay in this case is by far the most salient factor dictating our denial of the preliminary injunction.”

Judge Julius Richardson, joined by five other Republican appointees, similarly contended that “the Supreme Court’s interim order in this case controls the outcome here”—and added that the plaintiffs “are not likely to succeed on the merits because they have not shown standing or irreparable harm.”

The six dissenters, all Democratic appointees, would have upheld the preliminary injunction. Judge Robert Bruce King, in an opinion joined by all six, argued that the SCOTUS stay should have “no role” here. Judge James Wynn, joined by five out of the six, argued that “[t]o treat interim orders as binding precedent abandons our long-held jurisprudence of deciding constitutional law through reasoned opinions, not emergency motions made under intense time pressure”—and treating interim orders like opinions would “weaken the public’s confidence in the integrity of our judicial system’s commitment to deliberation and transparency.”

This did not sit well with Judge Wilkinson, who condemned what he called Judge Wynn’s “rhetorical assault upon the Supreme Court”; defending himself, Judge Wynn argued that Judge Wilkinson “mistakes disagreement for insubordination.” And I’m just scratching the surface of the 88 pages of opinions—for more on their extended debate about how much weight to give pronouncements from the Supreme Court’s interim or emergency docket, see Bloomberg Law and Law.com.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

It was a quiet week at SCOTUS, with no new opinions. But the justices vacated the judgments in two high-profile criminal cases, paving the way for the defendants—former Trump adviser Stephen Bannon, and former Cincinnati city council member Alexander “P.G.” Sittenfeld—to return to their respective district courts, where the Trump administration plans to dismiss the indictments against them. For additional background on Sittenfeld’s (in my view unjustified) prosecution, as well as an explanation of why the presidential pardon he received didn’t make his case moot, see his petition for certiorari filed by Jones Day.

Judge Wilkinson had a busy week. In addition to dueling with Judge Wynn in AFSCME v. SSA, he wrote an opinion in Perry v. Marteney that upheld West Virginia’s compulsory vaccination law (over a dissent by Judge Paul Niemeyer).

In addition to announcing his intent to take senior status (discussed above), Judge Erickson penned notable opinions that allowed two controversial Iowa laws to go into effect: Iowa Safe Schools v. Reynolds, challenging a law “preclud[ing] a school district from providing instruction relating to gender identity or sexual orientation to students in kindergarten through grade six,” and Penguin Random House, LLC v. Robbins, involving a law that “require[s] removal of books containing ‘descriptions or visual depictions of a sex act’” from school libraries.

Here is John Ross’s summary for Short Circuit of Faulk v. Dimerco Express USA Corp.: “Nobody on staff here at Short Circuit is an employment lawyer, but we suspect that having your human-resources manager make a PowerPoint presentation indicating that ‘Ideal Sales Candidates’ would be ‘American and Caucasian (preferred) ethnicity’ is what experts in the field would call ‘pretty bad.’ Anyway, this Eleventh Circuit [opinion by Chief Judge Bill Pryor]… turns out how you’d probably expect.”

In Trump-related litigation, there have been a slew of rulings from the district courts in recent weeks—too many to chronicle here—but I’ll mention two cases that made their way to the D.C. Circuit. First, on Wednesday, that court denied Anthropic’s request to stop the Defense Department from designating the AI company “a supply-chain risk.” Second, on Saturday, the court remanded the White House ballroom case to Judge Richard Leon (D.D.C.), directing him to clarify certain aspects of his ruling (which would have put the construction of Trump’s controversial ballroom on hold—but Judge Leon stayed his own ruling to allow for an appeal, and the D.C. Circuit extended the stay until April 17).

Judge Sherilyn Garnett (C.D. Cal.) sentenced Jasveen Sangha, the drug dealer known as the “Ketamine Queen,” to 15 years in prison, for her role in the 2023 death of Friends actor Matthew Perry.

Litigations of the Week: the legal battles surrounding Kalshi and other prediction markets.

Prediction markets, where people can bets on current events, are “culturally ascendant,” according to The New York Times. On platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket, people around the world are betting billions on events such as whether Justice Alito might retire—and some folks are doing it for a living, making millions.

At least in the United States, are these operations being conducted in accordance with the law? And if so, who decides—the federal government, or the states? As Jenna Greene explained in a recent Reuters column, “the disputes come down to questions of federal preemption. Are the wagers—known as event contracts—swaps that fall under the purview of the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission? And if so, would that preclude state-level efforts to regulate and tax the companies?”

As of now, litigation related to Kalshi and other prediction markets is breaking out all over. It includes the CFTC suing states, alleging that their efforts to police the markets are unlawful; state regulators pursuing Kalshi, both civilly and criminally; Kalshi suing state regulators, seeking injunctions to block the enforcement of sports betting laws against the company; and appellate proceedings in circuit courts across the country, including the Sixth and Ninth Circuits.

As of now, the highest court to rule on prediction-market issues is the Third Circuit. Last Monday, in KalshiEX, LLC v. Flaherty, a divided panel of that court affirmed a preliminary injunction blocking the New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement from enforcing state law against Kalshi. In an opinion by Judge David Porter, the majority concluded that “Kalshi has demonstrated a reasonable chance of success on its argument that the Commodity Exchange Act preempts otherwise applicable state law.” Judge Jane Roth dissented, arguing that “[b]ecause Kalshi is facilitating gambling, it can be subjected to state regulation,” and her colleagues in the majority “fail[ed] to adequately consider the presumption against preemption.”

When might the Supreme Court get involved? They’re betting on this at Polymarket.

In other litigation news:

The political career of Representative Eric Swalwell (D-Cal.), at one point a leading candidate for California governor, appears to be falling apart, amid allegations of sexual assault and misconduct made by multiple women. And some of these could turn into a criminal prosecution, with the Manhattan District Attorney’s Office opening an investigation into Swalwell.

A Second Circuit panel gave serious consideration to the arguments advanced by Alexandra Shapiro—one of the nation’s leading criminal appellate lawyer, as well as my former podcast guest—in favor of resentencing Sean “Diddy” Combs.

Long Island architect Rex Heuermann pleaded guilty to murdering seven women, a series of long-unsolved crimes known as the Gilgo Beach killings (named after the beach where Heuermann disposed of the bodies).

Deal of the Week: Pershing Square’s $64 billion bid for Universal Music.

For Deal of the Week, I typically recognize a transaction with a high chance of closing, as opposed to something speculative like a bid. But this is no ordinary bid: it has a giant price tag, and it involves a famous company whose “products” many of us enjoy.

Pershing Square Capital Management, the hedge fund founded by billionaire Bill Ackman, offered to buy Universal Music Group for around $64 billion in cash and stock. One of the so-called “Big Three” record labels, Universal works with artists including Taylor Swift, Elton John, Bad Bunny, Kendrick Lamar, and Drake.

Three law firmsare advising Pershing Square: Sullivan & Cromwell, White & Case, and the Dutch law firm Stibbe (because Universal Music Group N.V. is actually a Dutch entity). We don’t know which firms are counseling Universal—whose board acknowledged receipt of the bid but otherwise didn’t say much, besides expressing “complete confidence” in UMG’s strategy and management.

Law Firm of the Week: Simpson Thacher & Bartlett.

For a top 10 firm in both profits and prestige—yes, I know the new Vault rankings are out, and I’ll cover them soon—Simpson Thacher & Bartlett has a fairly low profile. But it was in the news last week for a few interesting reasons.

First, STB is handling some notable transactional matters. It’s advising Carlyle in the private-equity giant’s $2.8 billion acquisition of a majority stake in MAI Capital Management, an investment advisory firm, and it’s shepherding Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust Inc., a newly formed REIT focused on data centers, in its IPO.

Second, driven by such deal work, Simpson is flourishing financially. According to The American Lawyer, the firm grew revenue in 2025 by 22.5 percent, to $3.55 billion, and increased profits per equity partner by 11.8 percent, to a staggering $8.57 million. And it didn’t raise its PPEP by shrinking its equity partnership, a tactic employed by many firms; to the contrary, as noted by Roy Strom of Bloomberg Law, Simpson added 25 equity partners—a 12 percent increase, taking the partnership to 229.

Third, STB added three sports dealmakers to its corporate practice: Michael Kuh and Eric Geffner, who will serve as co-heads of the firm’s sports group, and Matthew Carpenter-Dennis. Kuh came from Hogan Lovells, which has been shedding sports lawyers; Geffner joined from Sidley Austin; and Carpenter-Dennis was most recently vice president and assistant general counsel at the NBA. As I reported last year, the sports sector is booming—and Simpson is there for it.

In other firm news:

Jones Day confirmed that it was the victim of a cyberattack, in which an unauthorized party accessed the files of 10 clients.

Anisha Mehta, a former associate at DLA Piper, is suing the firm for pregnancy discrimination, and the case is now being tried before Judge Analisa Torres (S.D.N.Y.). The dispute centers on whether Mehta was fired for pregnancy or performance-related reasons.

I normally don’t discuss U.K. legal news—there’s more than enough to cover here in the U.S.—but I’ll mention this story because everyone’s talking about it. WilmerHale is being sued in London's High Court over a $35 million legal bill by a former client—Alberto Safra, a child of the late Joseph Safra, the billionaire banker. There has been breathless reporting about Safra getting billed as much as $162,000 a day, with top partners charging as much as $2,095 an hour. But as someone who’s very familiar with what it costs to hire a top Biglaw firm like Wilmer, I am—like Olympic gymnast McKayla Maroney—not impressed. This was a multibillion-dollar battle over Alberto’s alleged disinheritance by Joseph; the parties reached a “global, amicable resolution” in 2024; and $2,000 an hour is far from the top in Biglaw billing rates. What did Safra expect it to cost?

Move of the Week: Barnes & Thornburg hiring a 12-lawyer group of insurance-recovery lawyers.

Certain practices don’t sit together easily under the same roof—such as representing policyholders in insurance-recovery work and representing insurance companies. Today, a relatively small number of Am Law 100 firms represent policyholders in insurance litigation, including Covington, Blank Rome, and Pillsbury Winthrop.

And don’t forget Barnes & Thornburg, which has been growing rapidly in recent years. It just hired around a dozen insurance-recovery lawyers, led by Lynda Bennett, from Lowenstein Sandler, as reported Law360. Lowenstein apparently wants to grow its client base in the insurance industry, so BT was a better fit for Bennett’s group, and they left with no hard feelings. As Lowenstein chair Gary Wingens told Law.com, “Lynda Bennett and our former insurance recovery team are talented and committed lawyers. They leave the Lowenstein community with our deep respect and genuine friendship.”

Other notable moves:

Partner departures from Cravath now number nine (year to date). The latest is Scott Bennett—the firm’s top IPO lawyer, per Meghan Tribe of Bloomberg Law—who joined Sidley Austin to lead its technology capital markets group.

The M&A market is busy—as is the lateral market for M&A partners. White & Case hired two, both in New York: Jennifer Cheng, former global M&A chair at Reed Smith, and Andrea Merediz Basham, from Freshfields.

Looking to litigation, Morgan Lewis added a trio of partners—Christopher Pardo, Shawn Regan, and Torsten Kracht—from Hunton Andrews Kurth in Boston, New York, and D.C., respectively.

Winston & Strawn welcomed two white-collar partners, Anne Murray and Guy Singer, in D.C. and New York, respectively.

Ahead of its merger with Taylor Wessing, scheduled to close next month, Winston is also losing some lawyers. King & Spalding hired its third team of Winston partners in as many months: Jeff Cole and Brian Jansen, Dallas-based finance and restructuring practitioners who focus on leveraged finance.

Speaking of the restructuring space, Goodwin Procter picked up a pair of partners from WilmerHale, Andrew Goldman and Benjamin Loveland, in New York and Boston, respectively. Goldman now co-chairs Goodwin’s restructuring practice (having held the equivalent role at Wilmer).

That’s all for this week’s Judicial Notice. Thanks for reading, sorry it’s a little on the late side (today has been a long day)—and for fellow denizens of the New York tristate area, get ready for some warm weather coming our way.

items.title: Judicial Notice (04.12.26): ‘I Love You, Sir’
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Chambers-ranked and Harvard-educated, Jeff is the trusted closer for high-stakes disputes. His battle-tested system of managed communications empowers clients to make clear, strong decisions in a calm, informed environment. It makes the right resolution obvious. If settlement seems impossible, call him.

Last Monday, I traveled down to lovely Lexington, Virginia, to speak at Washington and Lee University School of Law. I delivered the latest version of my talk about Donald Trump’s executive orders against law firms and the litigation and settlements that followed, which I’ve given to law school chapters of the Federalist Society, the American Constitution Society, and the ACLU (with this particular event sponsored by W&L’s small but active FedSoc chapter). Thanks to the Trump administration for continuing to fight this (losing) battle and giving my talk a longer shelf life.

While down in Old Dominion, I discussed whether Justice Samuel Alito might retire with Ann Marimow of The New York Times (gift link). Sources told Marimow that although Alito hasn’t completed his clerk hiring for October Term 2026, he’s interviewing. Why is this noteworthy? If you look at the last five justices to retire, four of them failed to hire a full slate of clerks right before retiring. (Speaking of SCOTUS clerk hiring, If you’re aware of anyone hired since my last roundup—especially by Alito—please email me at davidlat@substack.com or text me at 917-397-2751.)

And this past weekend, Zach and I made a quick trip to D.C. to attend a launch party for our friend Sarah Isgur’s new book, Last Branch Standing: A Potentially Surprising, Occasionally Witty Journey Inside Today’s Supreme Court (gift link). I’ll have more coverage later; for now, I’ll just urge you to buy the book, which will appeal to Original Jurisdiction readers (who appreciate thoughtful, nuanced, fair-minded commentary).

Otherwise, my week was focused on producing content for OJ. I published stories three days in a row: a write-up of the 2026 U.S. News law school ranking, in which Stanford Law replaced Yale as the #1 school; a podcast interview with Jennifer Bennett of Gupta Wessler, a rising star of the Supreme Court bar with a 4-0 record (while representing workers and employees, who don’t always win before the current Court); and a story about Biglaw firms moving into plaintiff-side work, a collaboration with my friends at Burford Capital (a longtime sponsor of OJ and my co-host for an upcoming breakfast event, for any of you in Manhattan on Wednesday, May 6).

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: Todd Blanche.

After Pam Bondi was terminated by Trump as attorney general, the spotlight shifted to Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche. He’s now the Acting Attorney General—as well as a leading candidate to be nominated as the long-term, Senate-confirmed AG.

On Monday, Blanche held his first press conference as acting AG, where he focused on the Department of Justice’s fraud-fighting efforts—to be led by Assistant Attorney General Colin McDonald, recently confirmed to lead the DOJ’s new National Fraud Enforcement Division. When asked about why Bondi was fired, Blanche disputed media reports—which focused on her lack of success in prosecuting Trump’s political foes, as well as her handling of the controversy over the Jeffrey Epstein files—and said, “Nobody has any idea… except for the president.”

As for his own elevation to acting AG and interest in serving as the permanent AG, Blanche gushed: “I love working for President Trump… If President Trump chooses to keep me as acting, that’s an honor. If he chooses to nominate me, that’s an honor. If he chooses to nominate somebody else and asks me to go do something else, I will say, ‘Thank you very much. I love you, sir.’” (Contacted for comment, First Lady Melania Trump quipped, “That makes one of us.”)

Commentators criticized Blanche’s comments as unseemly, arguing that they call into question his ability to objectively oversee prosecutions of Trump allies accused of wrongdoing. What might have motivated Blanche’s profession of “love” for The Donald? Former U.S. attorney John Fishwick told Fox News, “Blanche seems to be trying out for the top job today in his opening press conference—and surely trying to catch Trump’s attention with his criticism of many of the questions by the press.”

With Blanche as acting AG, who will assume the crucial responsibilities of deputy attorney general, which basically involve running the Department’s day-to-day operations? Blanche announced that conservative litigator Trent McCotter will serve as principal associate deputy attorney general—a powerful position, vacant since Emil Bove left for the Third Circuit—and in that capacity, McCotter will discharge the duties of DAG (with help from the newly confirmed Colin McDonald).

In other news involving lawyers and the Trump administration:

Staying on the DOJ, Professor Dan Rodriguez published an interesting analysis of a proposed rule that would potentially displace state bars in policing ethics violations by Department lawyers. Noting that the rule has been widely criticized as lying beyond federal authority, he concluded that it’s “not clear that [critics of the rule] are right in these claims,” but it’s “not clear that they are wrong.”

Immigration judges are actually part of the Justice Department, not the judiciary—and since the start of Trump’s second term, around 100 have been dismissed and 140 have been appointed. In the wake of this reshaping of the ranks, asylum grant rates have dropped from almost 50 percent to under 10 percent.

In other Trump-related turnover, the Securities and Exchange Commission named David Woodcock, currently a partner in the Dallas and D.C. offices of Gibson Dunn, as the next director of the Division of Enforcement. He’ll succeed Margaret “Meg” Ryan, who stepped down after only six months on the job (reportedly over objections that the SEC was going too easy on Trump allies).

Judge of the Week: Justice Sonia Sotomayor.

In our SCOTUS-obsessed household, we talk a lot about Justice Sonia Sotomayor. According to our son Harlan—who recently delivered a presentation to this second-grade class about Justice Sotomayor, the notable woman he picked to profile for Women’s History Month—Zach and I talk about her “42,774,681 times a day.”

And many OJ readers have been buzzing about Justice Sotomayor as well. At an event hosted by the University of Kansas School of Law last Tuesday, while discussing Noem v. Vasquez Perdomo—a case from September 2025, in which the Court issued an interim order giving the green light to certain immigration stops in the Los Angeles area, over Justice Sotomayor’s forceful dissent—she said, “I had a colleague in that case who wrote [that] these are only temporary stops. This is from a man whose parents were professionals. And probably doesn’t really know any person who works by the hour.”

She didn’t name this colleague, but she didn’t have to. The only justice who explained his Perdomo vote was Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who wrote a concurrence stating that while “apparent ethnicity alone cannot furnish reasonable suspicion,” it can be a “relevant factor… along with other salient factors.” Based on this concurrence, progressives started referring to such immigration stops as “Kavanaugh stops.”

Justice Sotomayor wrote a passionate dissent in Perdomo, which at 21 pages was more than twice as long as the Kavanaugh concurrence, and ended it with “I dissent” (as opposed to the more common “I respectfully dissent”). She criticized the concurrence as “all but declar[ing] that all Latinos, U.S. citizens or not, who work low-wage jobs are fair game to be seized at any time.”

Yes, justices go after each other in opinions—but they typically “leave it on the page,” i.e., don’t bring up their grievances in other contexts. So it was striking to see a justice criticize a colleague outside a written opinion, in a public appearance, and in somewhat personal terms. This led the editorial board of The Wall Street Journal (gift link) to accuse Justice Sotomayor of “profil[ing]” Justice Kavanaugh, in terms of making assumptions about him based on his demographic background as a white male from a well-to-do, professional family. (And her assumptions might not be correct; as a teen, Kavanaugh had summer jobs in both construction and lawn care, where he might have met people who work by the hour—and not $4,000 an hour.)

Perdomo was decided last September—another reason it was so notable to see Justice Sotomayor bring it up now, many months later. I wouldn’t be surprised if Justice Kavanaugh regrets his Perdomo concurrence: in December, he wrote a concurrence in Trump v. Illinois in which he explicitly emphasized that immigration officers “must not make interior immigration stops or arrests based on race or ethnicity.” Maybe The Wise Latina, with the benefit of hindsight, might similarly see her pointed comments about a colleague as unwise?

Or maybe not. Two days later, in a Thursday appearance at the University of Alabama School of Law, Justice Sotomayor said that with “virtually all” of her fellow justices, she has “a civil relationship,” and with “many of them,” she has “a friendship.” One can’t help wondering whether Justice Kavanaugh might be in the first category. As noted by Jimmy Hoover, last week’s remarks appear to diverge from her 2018 comment in which she said that Justice Kavanaugh, recently confirmed after a bruising confirmation process, was now part of the Supreme Court “family.”

What could be going on here? Dahlia Lithwick and Mark Joseph Stern suggested that this could be strategic behavior on Justice Sotomayor’s part, perhaps aimed at pressuring Justice Kavanaugh into voting her way in future immigration-related cases. Another possibility is that behind the scenes, Justice Kavanaugh did something recently to incur Justice Sotomayor’s ire—e.g., voted in a certain case against her position, or circulated a draft opinion she found objectionable. With October Term 2025 entering its home stretch, we might know something sooner rather than later.

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Last week’s featured jurist—Judge Nathan Milliron of Harris County, Texas, who was widely criticized for mistreating court employees and counsel—was apparently unhappy with Houston lawyer James Stafford, an attorney who spoke negatively about Milliron to the media. He accused Stafford of sending an improper “ex parte” communication to the court—even though Stafford doesn’t have, and never has had, any matters before Milliron—and ordered Stafford to show up to court and explain himself. Stafford ignored the order, refusing to appear—and that might be the end of it, since Milliron hasn’t followed up.

Another judge previously mentioned in these pages, Judge Thomas Ludington (E.D. Mich.), pleaded “no contest” to a single charge of operating a vehicle while intoxicated (and a second charge, of operating a vehicle with a blood-alcohol content of 0.17 or more, was dismissed). He’ll be sentenced on May 13. It appears Judge Ludington plans to remain on the bench; his lawyer, Jonathan Steffy, said that His Honor “looks forward to continuing to serve the Federal Court at the highest level and focus on his work, along with his wife and family.”

Judge Chris Taylor won election to the Wisconsin Supreme Court, defeating Judge Maria Lazar, one of her colleagues on the Wisconsin Court of Appeals. Judge Taylor will replace a conservative, Justice Rebecca Bradley, and give liberals a 5-2 majority on the Wisconsin high court.

In other news from the Badger State, former Milwaukee County Judge Hannah Dugan, whom a jury found guilty of obstruction of federal agents, filed motions seeking acquittal or a new trial; Judge Lynn Adelman (E.D. Wis.) denied them.

Judge Roy Altman (S.D. Fla.) wrote a new book, Israel on Trial: Examining the History, the Evidence, and the Law.

In nominations news (with thanks to Howard Bashman’s How Appealing):

Donald Trump announced two new circuit court nominees: former Ohio solicitor general Benjamin Flowers (6th Cir.) and Sullivan & Cromwell partner Matthew Schwartz (2d Cir.). Both clerked for conservative members of SCOTUS—Flowers for the late Justice Scalia, Schwartz for Justice Alito—and Schwartz currently represents Trump in both civil and criminal appellate proceedings.

Judge Ralph Erickson (8th Cir.) became the first Trump appellate appointee to reveal his retirement, announcing that he’ll take senior status upon confirmation of a successor. And who might that be? Per Mike Fragoso, contenders include Judge Daniel Traynor (D.N.D.), North Dakota Solicitor General Phil Axt, and Justice Jerod Tufte of the North Dakota Supreme Court. I give the edge to Judge Traynor—as a sitting federal judge, he’d be easy to confirm, and his elevation would open up a district-court spot for the administration to fill—with Axt as a dark-horse candidate. (As a 2018 graduate of Yale Law, Axt would be criticized by some for not having enough experience—see next bullet.)

For the first time in Trump’s second term, the American Bar Association rated one of his judicial nominees “not qualified” (by a split vote of the committee): RNC senior counsel Katie Lane, nominated to the District of Montana. ABA committee chair Pamela Roberts praised Lane as “a talented lawyer, indeed at the top of her peer group,” but said that with less than nine years of post-law-school experience (including clerkships), she didn’t have the twelve years of experience that the committee generally seeks.

Job of the Week: an opportunity at a Bay Area litigation boutique.

Lateral Link has a confidential opportunity with a premier Bay Area litigation boutique known for handling high-stakes, complex disputes—and producing strong trial results. The firm’s founding partners are planning their succession and seeking a midlevel or senior litigation associate to work closely with them. This opportunity offers a clear path to equity partnership, direct mentorship from highly accomplished trial lawyers, and the chance to inherit and manage a thriving practice. For more information, please contact Buddy Broome at bbroome@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: AFSCME v. SSA.

Legal commentators and law professors aren’t the only folks arguing about the Supreme Court’s interim docket. Lower-court judges have strong views too—as we saw from the Fourth Circuit’s en banc decision last Friday in American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees v. Social Security Administration.

AFSCME v. SSA might ring a bell because it went up to the Supreme Court last June. Over dissenting votes from the three liberal justices, a majority of justices voted to grant emergency relief to the government and stay a district-court preliminary injunction that would have blocked the Department of Government Efficiency from accessing certain records held by the Social Security Administration.

The case went back down to the Fourth Circuit, which went en banc to consider it. By a vote of 9-6, the court voted to vacate the preliminary injunction, but disagreed on the reasoning:

Judge Toby Heytens, joined by two fellow Democratic appointees, voted to vacate the injunction because the plaintiffs “did not show—based on the record before the district court when it entered this preliminary injunction—that they were ‘likely to suffer irreparable harm in the absence of preliminary relief.’”

Judge J. Harvie Wilkinson, joined by three other Republican appointees, voted to vacate, arguing that “the Supreme Court’s issuance of the stay in this case is by far the most salient factor dictating our denial of the preliminary injunction.”

Judge Julius Richardson, joined by five other Republican appointees, similarly contended that “the Supreme Court’s interim order in this case controls the outcome here”—and added that the plaintiffs “are not likely to succeed on the merits because they have not shown standing or irreparable harm.”

The six dissenters, all Democratic appointees, would have upheld the preliminary injunction. Judge Robert Bruce King, in an opinion joined by all six, argued that the SCOTUS stay should have “no role” here. Judge James Wynn, joined by five out of the six, argued that “[t]o treat interim orders as binding precedent abandons our long-held jurisprudence of deciding constitutional law through reasoned opinions, not emergency motions made under intense time pressure”—and treating interim orders like opinions would “weaken the public’s confidence in the integrity of our judicial system’s commitment to deliberation and transparency.”

This did not sit well with Judge Wilkinson, who condemned what he called Judge Wynn’s “rhetorical assault upon the Supreme Court”; defending himself, Judge Wynn argued that Judge Wilkinson “mistakes disagreement for insubordination.” And I’m just scratching the surface of the 88 pages of opinions—for more on their extended debate about how much weight to give pronouncements from the Supreme Court’s interim or emergency docket, see Bloomberg Law and Law.com.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

It was a quiet week at SCOTUS, with no new opinions. But the justices vacated the judgments in two high-profile criminal cases, paving the way for the defendants—former Trump adviser Stephen Bannon, and former Cincinnati city council member Alexander “P.G.” Sittenfeld—to return to their respective district courts, where the Trump administration plans to dismiss the indictments against them. For additional background on Sittenfeld’s (in my view unjustified) prosecution, as well as an explanation of why the presidential pardon he received didn’t make his case moot, see his petition for certiorari filed by Jones Day.

Judge Wilkinson had a busy week. In addition to dueling with Judge Wynn in AFSCME v. SSA, he wrote an opinion in Perry v. Marteney that upheld West Virginia’s compulsory vaccination law (over a dissent by Judge Paul Niemeyer).

In addition to announcing his intent to take senior status (discussed above), Judge Erickson penned notable opinions that allowed two controversial Iowa laws to go into effect: Iowa Safe Schools v. Reynolds, challenging a law “preclud[ing] a school district from providing instruction relating to gender identity or sexual orientation to students in kindergarten through grade six,” and Penguin Random House, LLC v. Robbins, involving a law that “require[s] removal of books containing ‘descriptions or visual depictions of a sex act’” from school libraries.

Here is John Ross’s summary for Short Circuit of Faulk v. Dimerco Express USA Corp.: “Nobody on staff here at Short Circuit is an employment lawyer, but we suspect that having your human-resources manager make a PowerPoint presentation indicating that ‘Ideal Sales Candidates’ would be ‘American and Caucasian (preferred) ethnicity’ is what experts in the field would call ‘pretty bad.’ Anyway, this Eleventh Circuit [opinion by Chief Judge Bill Pryor]… turns out how you’d probably expect.”

In Trump-related litigation, there have been a slew of rulings from the district courts in recent weeks—too many to chronicle here—but I’ll mention two cases that made their way to the D.C. Circuit. First, on Wednesday, that court denied Anthropic’s request to stop the Defense Department from designating the AI company “a supply-chain risk.” Second, on Saturday, the court remanded the White House ballroom case to Judge Richard Leon (D.D.C.), directing him to clarify certain aspects of his ruling (which would have put the construction of Trump’s controversial ballroom on hold—but Judge Leon stayed his own ruling to allow for an appeal, and the D.C. Circuit extended the stay until April 17).

Judge Sherilyn Garnett (C.D. Cal.) sentenced Jasveen Sangha, the drug dealer known as the “Ketamine Queen,” to 15 years in prison, for her role in the 2023 death of Friends actor Matthew Perry.

Litigations of the Week: the legal battles surrounding Kalshi and other prediction markets.

Prediction markets, where people can bets on current events, are “culturally ascendant,” according to The New York Times. On platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket, people around the world are betting billions on events such as whether Justice Alito might retire—and some folks are doing it for a living, making millions.

At least in the United States, are these operations being conducted in accordance with the law? And if so, who decides—the federal government, or the states? As Jenna Greene explained in a recent Reuters column, “the disputes come down to questions of federal preemption. Are the wagers—known as event contracts—swaps that fall under the purview of the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission? And if so, would that preclude state-level efforts to regulate and tax the companies?”

As of now, litigation related to Kalshi and other prediction markets is breaking out all over. It includes the CFTC suing states, alleging that their efforts to police the markets are unlawful; state regulators pursuing Kalshi, both civilly and criminally; Kalshi suing state regulators, seeking injunctions to block the enforcement of sports betting laws against the company; and appellate proceedings in circuit courts across the country, including the Sixth and Ninth Circuits.

As of now, the highest court to rule on prediction-market issues is the Third Circuit. Last Monday, in KalshiEX, LLC v. Flaherty, a divided panel of that court affirmed a preliminary injunction blocking the New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement from enforcing state law against Kalshi. In an opinion by Judge David Porter, the majority concluded that “Kalshi has demonstrated a reasonable chance of success on its argument that the Commodity Exchange Act preempts otherwise applicable state law.” Judge Jane Roth dissented, arguing that “[b]ecause Kalshi is facilitating gambling, it can be subjected to state regulation,” and her colleagues in the majority “fail[ed] to adequately consider the presumption against preemption.”

When might the Supreme Court get involved? They’re betting on this at Polymarket.

In other litigation news:

The political career of Representative Eric Swalwell (D-Cal.), at one point a leading candidate for California governor, appears to be falling apart, amid allegations of sexual assault and misconduct made by multiple women. And some of these could turn into a criminal prosecution, with the Manhattan District Attorney’s Office opening an investigation into Swalwell.

A Second Circuit panel gave serious consideration to the arguments advanced by Alexandra Shapiro—one of the nation’s leading criminal appellate lawyer, as well as my former podcast guest—in favor of resentencing Sean “Diddy” Combs.

Long Island architect Rex Heuermann pleaded guilty to murdering seven women, a series of long-unsolved crimes known as the Gilgo Beach killings (named after the beach where Heuermann disposed of the bodies).

Deal of the Week: Pershing Square’s $64 billion bid for Universal Music.

For Deal of the Week, I typically recognize a transaction with a high chance of closing, as opposed to something speculative like a bid. But this is no ordinary bid: it has a giant price tag, and it involves a famous company whose “products” many of us enjoy.

Pershing Square Capital Management, the hedge fund founded by billionaire Bill Ackman, offered to buy Universal Music Group for around $64 billion in cash and stock. One of the so-called “Big Three” record labels, Universal works with artists including Taylor Swift, Elton John, Bad Bunny, Kendrick Lamar, and Drake.

Three law firmsare advising Pershing Square: Sullivan & Cromwell, White & Case, and the Dutch law firm Stibbe (because Universal Music Group N.V. is actually a Dutch entity). We don’t know which firms are counseling Universal—whose board acknowledged receipt of the bid but otherwise didn’t say much, besides expressing “complete confidence” in UMG’s strategy and management.

Law Firm of the Week: Simpson Thacher & Bartlett.

For a top 10 firm in both profits and prestige—yes, I know the new Vault rankings are out, and I’ll cover them soon—Simpson Thacher & Bartlett has a fairly low profile. But it was in the news last week for a few interesting reasons.

First, STB is handling some notable transactional matters. It’s advising Carlyle in the private-equity giant’s $2.8 billion acquisition of a majority stake in MAI Capital Management, an investment advisory firm, and it’s shepherding Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust Inc., a newly formed REIT focused on data centers, in its IPO.

Second, driven by such deal work, Simpson is flourishing financially. According to The American Lawyer, the firm grew revenue in 2025 by 22.5 percent, to $3.55 billion, and increased profits per equity partner by 11.8 percent, to a staggering $8.57 million. And it didn’t raise its PPEP by shrinking its equity partnership, a tactic employed by many firms; to the contrary, as noted by Roy Strom of Bloomberg Law, Simpson added 25 equity partners—a 12 percent increase, taking the partnership to 229.

Third, STB added three sports dealmakers to its corporate practice: Michael Kuh and Eric Geffner, who will serve as co-heads of the firm’s sports group, and Matthew Carpenter-Dennis. Kuh came from Hogan Lovells, which has been shedding sports lawyers; Geffner joined from Sidley Austin; and Carpenter-Dennis was most recently vice president and assistant general counsel at the NBA. As I reported last year, the sports sector is booming—and Simpson is there for it.

In other firm news:

Jones Day confirmed that it was the victim of a cyberattack, in which an unauthorized party accessed the files of 10 clients.

Anisha Mehta, a former associate at DLA Piper, is suing the firm for pregnancy discrimination, and the case is now being tried before Judge Analisa Torres (S.D.N.Y.). The dispute centers on whether Mehta was fired for pregnancy or performance-related reasons.

I normally don’t discuss U.K. legal news—there’s more than enough to cover here in the U.S.—but I’ll mention this story because everyone’s talking about it. WilmerHale is being sued in London's High Court over a $35 million legal bill by a former client—Alberto Safra, a child of the late Joseph Safra, the billionaire banker. There has been breathless reporting about Safra getting billed as much as $162,000 a day, with top partners charging as much as $2,095 an hour. But as someone who’s very familiar with what it costs to hire a top Biglaw firm like Wilmer, I am—like Olympic gymnast McKayla Maroney—not impressed. This was a multibillion-dollar battle over Alberto’s alleged disinheritance by Joseph; the parties reached a “global, amicable resolution” in 2024; and $2,000 an hour is far from the top in Biglaw billing rates. What did Safra expect it to cost?

Move of the Week: Barnes & Thornburg hiring a 12-lawyer group of insurance-recovery lawyers.

Certain practices don’t sit together easily under the same roof—such as representing policyholders in insurance-recovery work and representing insurance companies. Today, a relatively small number of Am Law 100 firms represent policyholders in insurance litigation, including Covington, Blank Rome, and Pillsbury Winthrop.

And don’t forget Barnes & Thornburg, which has been growing rapidly in recent years. It just hired around a dozen insurance-recovery lawyers, led by Lynda Bennett, from Lowenstein Sandler, as reported Law360. Lowenstein apparently wants to grow its client base in the insurance industry, so BT was a better fit for Bennett’s group, and they left with no hard feelings. As Lowenstein chair Gary Wingens told Law.com, “Lynda Bennett and our former insurance recovery team are talented and committed lawyers. They leave the Lowenstein community with our deep respect and genuine friendship.”

Other notable moves:

Partner departures from Cravath now number nine (year to date). The latest is Scott Bennett—the firm’s top IPO lawyer, per Meghan Tribe of Bloomberg Law—who joined Sidley Austin to lead its technology capital markets group.

The M&A market is busy—as is the lateral market for M&A partners. White & Case hired two, both in New York: Jennifer Cheng, former global M&A chair at Reed Smith, and Andrea Merediz Basham, from Freshfields.

Looking to litigation, Morgan Lewis added a trio of partners—Christopher Pardo, Shawn Regan, and Torsten Kracht—from Hunton Andrews Kurth in Boston, New York, and D.C., respectively.

Winston & Strawn welcomed two white-collar partners, Anne Murray and Guy Singer, in D.C. and New York, respectively.

Ahead of its merger with Taylor Wessing, scheduled to close next month, Winston is also losing some lawyers. King & Spalding hired its third team of Winston partners in as many months: Jeff Cole and Brian Jansen, Dallas-based finance and restructuring practitioners who focus on leveraged finance.

Speaking of the restructuring space, Goodwin Procter picked up a pair of partners from WilmerHale, Andrew Goldman and Benjamin Loveland, in New York and Boston, respectively. Goldman now co-chairs Goodwin’s restructuring practice (having held the equivalent role at Wilmer).

That’s all for this week’s Judicial Notice. Thanks for reading, sorry it’s a little on the late side (today has been a long day)—and for fellow denizens of the New York tristate area, get ready for some warm weather coming our way.
